article_title: The Best Burgers in Austin, Texas
name: Top Notch
must_try: Top Notch is also known for its fried foods, including fried chicken, fish, and shrimp, sides like fried okra and hand-battered onion rings, and fried apple and cherry pies for dessert.
notes: In Crestview, Top Notch still charcoal-grills its burgers like it’s 1971. The drive-in setup and vintage signage haven’t changed much since Dazed and Confused made it a cult favorite, and neither has the burger, which has remained thick, smoky, and deeply satisfying. The prices are nostalgic, too, with burgers for around $6 and combos for about $12. It’s a classic Austin experience, whether you dine inside or let a carhop bring it to your window. Top Notch’s menu offers gluten-free buns and veggie burgers.

article_title: The Best Burgers in Austin, Texas
name: Buddy's Burger
address: 9001 Cameron Rd Ste 101, Austin, TX 78754, USA
must_try: The $5 hand-spun milkshakes include classic flavors like strawberry, chocolate, and vanilla, plus more inventive options, like Mexican vanilla and coffee.
notes: This North Austin drive-thru hits all the right notes with crispy-edged smashburgers made from Angus beef and topped with fresh lettuce, tomato, and house sauce on a soft potato bun. Buddy’s is a family-run spot with quick service, indoor seating, and a few patio tables if you want to hang out. Burgers run from $5 to $8. Try the spicy version of the classic cheeseburger if you’re feeling bold. It’s topped with pepper jack cheese, grilled onions, jalapeños, and Buddy’s homemade spicy Smack sauce, which delivers a kick without going overboard.

article_title: The Best Burgers in Austin, Texas
name: Dan's Hamburgers
address: 5602 N Lamar Blvd, Austin, TX 78751, USA
must_try: Make it a meal with one of its creamy malts.
notes: Dan’s has been keeping things simple since the 1970s, and that’s precisely the point. With four locations across town, including outposts in South Austin and Airport Boulevard, the focus remains on no-fuss burgers, fast service, and curly fries that always hit the mark. The spot is dependable, affordable, and proudly old school: score quarter-pound burgers for $5 and doubles for $8. Breakfast is served all day, so if the mood strikes, you can mix it up with sides like hashbrowns.

article_title: The Best Burgers in Austin, Texas
name: The Silver Medal
notes: Perched off the 290 frontage road in Coronado Hills, Silver Medal is a genuine dive bar that embraces its unpretentious charm. Inside, find wood-paneled walls, a jukebox, and a menu featuring standout smash burgers, chili dogs, and a ton of vegetarian-friendly options. The bar offers $2 Jello shots, beer on tap, and a laid back atmosphere that feels like a friend’s basement hangout. Tip: While there’s no fries, order the $12 combo, which is maybe the best deal in town, consisting of a chili dog, cheeseburger, and a draft Bud.

article_title: The Best Burgers in Austin, Texas
name: Billy’s On Burnet
notes: This old-school spot in Allandale is the kind of place where the football games are loud, the beers are cheap, and the burgers are crave-worthy. The classic cheeseburger is all about simplicity: thick, juicy patty, melted American, toasted bun — no frills, no fuss, and it rings it at $14. Billy’s has that no-nonsense Austin charm, especially if you catch it on trivia night or post up on the outdoor patio when the Green Bay Packers are playing. Bonus points for the surprisingly expansive vegetarian burger menu, which offers as much variety as the original list. Know before you go: Aside from burgers, Billy’s is also big on beer, with more than 30 draft beers to choose from and pints of Lone Star going for $2.50 all day, every day.

article_title: The Best Burgers in Austin, Texas
name: JewBoy Burgers
must_try: Order the battered onion rings, but make them loaded by adding queso and chili.
notes: JewBoy serves burgers in North Loop with a serious El Paso-slash-Jewish deli twist. The patties are pressed with grilled onions and layered with options like pastrami, green chiles, or even crispy latkes. It’s cozy inside with an extra few tables out front, and the vibe is laidback like the food, which is messy in a good way, and it’s affordable — the $11 burger is one of the cheapest items on the menu. Take your pick from the fried-and-true sides: chips and Homeboy chile con queso, crinkle-cut fries, or crispy tots.

article_title: The Best Burgers in Austin, Texas
name: Hopfields
notes: On Guadalupe in West Campus, Hopfields is a French-ish gastropub with a surprisingly luxe burger. The Pascal features a juicy patty, Camembert cheese, and whole-grain mustard on a brioche bun, with a side of pommes frites for $23 during lunch, brunch, or dinner. Make it fancy with a glass or bottle of wine, or more casual with a pick from the extensive list of more than 30 draft beers. Cocktails here are also a fun companion, with the Blinker, made with rye whiskey, grapefruit, cranberry, and a pomegranate-hibiscus black tea. Expect a moody vibe inside with low lighting, and a leafy patio out back that’s perfect for lingering. Know before you go: Hopfields can get busy, so book a reservation to secure your spot or take a gamble up front with at-the-bar ordering.

article_title: The Best Burgers in Austin, Texas
name: Dirty Martin’s Place
notes: Dirty Martin’s A West Campus institution since 1926, Dirty Martin’s is all grease, grit, and nostalgia, in the best way possible. Priced between $7 and $11, the burgers come smashed and sizzling off the flat-top, wrapped in wax paper with crispy edges and a soft, squishy bun. There’s no pretense here — just classic diner fare and a patio full of regulars, students, and the occasional hungover professor. The picnic-style seating indoors makes this ideal for a family or friend group outing; solo diners will feel plenty comfortable at the bar. Tip: Order a hand-spun shake and sit under the carport — it’s a rite of passage.

article_title: The Best Burgers in Austin, Texas
name: Crown & Anchor Pub
address: 2911 San Jacinto Blvd, Austin, TX 78705, USA
notes: A North Campus favorite since 1987, this beloved neighborhood pub nails the dive-bar burger formula. No bells and whistles — just a grilled patty made up of fresh, never frozen double-grinded beef and melted Swiss, American, or cheddar cheeses, plus add-ons like jalapeños, bacon, and mushrooms. Pair with fries, with the optional addition of melted cheese and ranch dressing, and a cold pint on the side. Diners can unwind on the pub’s spacious dog-friendly patio, which is often full of regulars who will claim it’s the best burger in town, or settle in inside, where there’s plenty of seating, pool tables, and dartboards — tournaments happen every Wednesday starting at 7 p.m. Best for: A burger with beer. With more than 30 crafted beers on tap and 30 canned varieties, Crown & Anchor features beer specials every day of the week. Catch happy hour prices on all draft beers from 2 p.m. to 7 p.m. daily.

article_title: The Best Burgers in Austin, Texas
name: Dai Due
notes: Over in Cherrywood, Dai Due takes the Texas burger to another level, and it sometimes comes with slight variations. The huge $26 house burger blends dry-aged beef, then tops it with cheese, fancy mayo of some kind, and crispy pickles, onions, or jalapeños. It’s rich, earthy, and worth the relatively higher price point, especially with a glass of natural wine on the shaded patio. Vibe check: Want a romantic spot for a crucial date? Hit this patio. Need a grown-up-seeming spot to take your parents? This dining room. Just like bougie stuff? Here, go here.

article_title: The Best Burgers in Austin, Texas
name: Bar Toti
notes: Located next to its sister restaurant Este off Manor Road, Bar Toti serves a bold, spicy burger with serious flair. La Smashburgesa stacks two patties with white American cheese, escabeche, griddled ham, and a smoky chipotle mayo. It’s as indulgent as it sounds, best enjoyed with a 50/50 Manhattan (rye, amaro, rosso vermouth, Oloroso sherry, and mole bitters) on the sunny front patio. Know before you go: Time your visit to happy hour (5 p.m. to 6 p.m. Sundays through Thursdays and all night on Mondays). The $16 La Smashburgesa is always half off.

article_title: The Best Burgers in Austin, Texas
name: Pool Burger
address: 2315 Lake Austin Blvd, Austin, TX 78703, USA
notes: Hidden behind Deep Eddy Cabaret in Tarrytown, Pool Burger feels like a breezy beach shack that’s pineapple-scented and rum-soaked. The Blue Hawaiian burger adds a thick grilled pineapple slice to a beef patty and Thousand Island dressing, which pairs well with the crinkle-cut fries for $14. It’s playful and well-executed. The spot also has two vegetarian-friendly burgers, a $13 patty melt, and a classic double cheeseburger, among other options. The thatched-roof patio only adds to the charm. Best for: Having a burger with a side of pool therapy. Take a dip in the neighboring Deep Eddy Pool and get a tiki drink, like a classic Mai Tai.

article_title: The Best Burgers in Austin, Texas
name: Clark’s Oyster Bar
notes: It might be hard to believe that one of Austin’s best burgers is at this Clarksville oyster and seafood bar for $25, but so be it. Although the skinny shoestring fries are pretty divisive, it’s hard to argue with the thick, crusty beef patty doused in zesty gribiche sauce and Gruyere cheese. Order some bivalves for an appetizer and down your burger with an ice-cold, dry martini inside the sunny restaurant. Tip: If the weather’s nice, dine under the yellow-and-white awning on the patio overlooking West Sixth Street. It’s one of the best patios in town.

article_title: The Best Burgers in Austin, Texas
name: Better Half Coffee & Cocktails
notes: A few blocks west of Downtown, Better Half keeps things cool with an all-day menu and a solid cheeseburger for $12; during happy hour from 3 p.m. to 6 p.m. Tuesday through Friday, the burger’s price comes down to a very affordable $7. It’s the kind of cheeseburger that doesn’t need fuss: juicy patty, crisp vegetables, Dijonnaise, and a toasty bun, best paired with a side of cauliflower tots or french fries. There’s a veggie burger option, too, plus plenty of space to sprawl on the patio on its cute, millennial pink picnic tables on fair weather days. Order a coffee-turned-cocktail inside. If you drive: Better Half’s front parking lot, shared with its brewery Hold Out Brewing, is pretty spacious, but if it is full, check out its back lot off of Fourth Street.

article_title: The Best Burgers in Austin, Texas
name: Delray Cafe
notes: This East Side food trailer parked outside Nickel City is all about beefy, griddled sliders with a nod to Detroit. Don’t be fooled by the “slider” name — the burgers here are plenty big, especially if you go for the double or triple stack. Each cheesy burger is laced with mustard, grilled onions, and pickles, all of which are served on a steamed bun starting at about $7. If you’re a vegetarian, fear not: Delray’s veggie version, made with seasoned Impossible meat, is also unreasonably good. Pro tip: Pair with a frozen Irish coffee when it’s hot outside. Visit on Tuesdays for the spot’s two-for-one burger night, or enjoy burgers well into the late night. Delray is open until 2 a.m. daily.

article_title: The Best Burgers in Austin, Texas
name: Casino El Camino
notes: A Downtown dive through and through, Casino El Camino cranks out monster burgers — three-quarters of a pound of charbroiled Angus beef, to be exact. Priced between $15 and $18, these burgers are messy, meaty, and unapologetically over-the-top. We’re partial to the Buffalo burger, grilled with the dive’s hot wing sauce and smothered with bleu cheese (it can be ordered on a cheddar-jalapeño bun, or, for the gluten-sensitive, swapped with a gluten-free bun for an additional $2). It’s dark inside, the music’s loud, and the fries are hefty enough to count as a meal on their own. Pro tip: Go hungry, and don’t wear white.

article_title: The Best Burgers in Austin, Texas
name: Gimme Burger
notes: Located in the courtyard at Butler Pitch & Putt, this Riverside Drive food truck is as much of a draw as the golf. The standard order is the namesake Gimme Burger, which features a double beef patty and double cheese for $12. The patties are smashed for a salty, edge-to-edge crust and come dressed with “shrettuce,” onion, tomatoes, pickles, and signature sauce on a sesame seed bun. Vegetarians can also swap in a veggie patty. While the menu offers hot dogs, fries, and a single-patty Kiddie Burger, the fried okra — available as a $4 add-on — is the standout side. Know before you go: Breakfast sandwiches are available Friday through Sunday from 8 a.m. to 11 a.m.

article_title: The Best Burgers in Austin, Texas
name: Golden Tiger
notes: While the fried chicken sandwich at Golden Tiger is one of the main draws of this East Austin food truck, the double smashburger also stands out. Order a classic old fashioned or seasonal cocktail inside at Whisler’s and then head out back to the festive patio to chow down. The $12 burger comes with shredded lettuce, pickles, red onions, American cheese, and special sauce. If you aren’t hungry enough for the double, you can also grab a dainty single-patty burger for $8. Good to know: The deep-fried tofu sandwich is a worthy alternative to beef or chicken.

article_title: The Best Burgers in Austin, Texas
name: Sandy’s Hamburgers
address: 603 Barton Springs Rd, Austin, TX, 78704, US
notes: Tucked just south of the river off Barton Springs Road, Sandy’s is part-burger stand, part-time capsule. The restaurant makes for an excellent post-Barton Springs lunch spot: The old-fashioned, no-frills cheeseburger hits all the right notes — hot, salty, and just a little bit messy — and costs about $6. Adding a malted shake (try the butterscotch or Oreo) or dipped frozen custard cone is a non-negotiable. Grab your burgers and dessert at the walk-up window and snag a picnic table outside. Don’t forget to grab some napkins. Know before you go: Kickstart your morning with a burger. Though it’s technically still “lunch” fare, this spot opens at 10 a.m.

article_title: The Best Burgers in Austin, Texas
name: Frazier's Long & Low
notes: On Riverside, Frazier’s leans into the retro bar vibe with 1970s wood paneling, cold beer, and unfussy comfort food in a casual environment. It also serves a sneaky-good burger. The $6 Little (OG) comes classic-style with a smashed patty, shredded lettuce, and special sauce. Patty melts, chopped cheeses, and there are also vegan burgers made with Beyond Meat. Expect arcade games along the wall, billiards tables at the center, and a relaxed crowd of regulars at the bar. Know before you go: Frazier’s is 21-and-up only, so leave the kids at home.

article_title: The Best Burgers in Austin, Texas
name: Leroy And Lewis Barbecue
notes: This celebrated, one-Michelin-starred barbecue joint in Garrison Park serves a burger worth seeking out. (It even received a shout-out on Somebody Feed Phil.) The juicy patty is a mix of brisket and beef, making for a smoky, decadent burger piled high with grilled onions, cheese, and sauce on a potato bun for $16. Have the burger with some sides or as a complement to a bigger group barbecue spread. For those dining on-site: There are plenty of tables indoors, and some picnic-style seating on the patio for when the weather is forgiving. Pro-tip: It may be a meat-centric spot, but don’t skip the vegetables, especially the cauliflower burnt ends.

article_title: The Best Burgers in Austin, Texas
name: Patty Palace
must_try: The fried onion petals, which are served with a side of Zesty sauce.
notes: With two locations — one at St. Elmo Brewing down South and another at Zilker Brewing on the East Side — this food truck is all about smash burgers. The namesake burger layers two griddled patties with sharp cheddar, a smoky Royal sauce, grilled onions, and just enough crunch from house pickles. The $10 vegan burger is also a worthy order and slightly thicker than the beefy version, while the Palace Melt (double beef patties on toasted rye, caramelized onions, melty Swiss and American cheeses, finished with creamy peppercorn sauce) is insanely gooey.

article_title: The Best Sushi Restaurants in Austin
name: Tare
notes: This Austin omakase restaurant within the North Star neighborhood focuses on meshing Japanese, South Texan, and Mexican flavors and ingredients throughout its kappo-style Japanese menu. Helmed by chef Michael Carranza and pastry chef Danielle Martinez, expect nigiri and small plates such as mesquite-smoked ocean trout with Texas wildflower honey or salsa macha brushed atop bluefin tuna, all in an elegant space hidden within an office building. Book reservations online — it’s gotten popular since earning a Michelin recommendation.

article_title: The Best Sushi Restaurants in Austin
name: Ichi-Umi Sushi
notes: The North Austin sushi restaurant serves substantial sushi for lunch and dinner and is a popular choice for gluten-free and vegetarian diners. The slightly seared selection (aburi-style) is lovely. Dine in at the casual restaurant or order takeout online.

article_title: The Best Sushi Restaurants in Austin
name: Tatsumi Sushi
address: 2700 W Anderson Ln Ste 212, Austin, TX, 78757, US
notes: While its location in a North Austin strip mall might not be anything remarkable, step inside Tatsumi and you’ll be treated to an exciting array of high-low options, from affordable classics like the spicy tuna roll, to the indulgent Ebi Uni Maguro stuffed with charred A5 Miyazaki beef, and more decadent and inventive dishes like the Negi Toro Toast topped with matcha powder, kaluga caviar, and brown butter. Executive chef Elvin Jin grew up shadowing his parents at their sushi restaurant in the Bay Area, then he studied in Japan before opening Tatsumi.

article_title: The Best Sushi Restaurants in Austin
name: Choo Sando
address: 5222 Burnet Rd Ste 535, Austin, TX, 78756, US
notes: While most people know this counter-service Rosedale restaurant for its great Japanese sandwiches, its sushi box selection shouldn’t be overlooked. The array often features exquisite pieces of fish such as bluefin tuna, salmon, and scallops. There are a variety of lunch sushi boxes available as grab-and-go to eat in the small, cheery restaurant or its few outdoor tables (or order online). Or, go big and preorder an omakase with sides for up to five people.

article_title: The Best Sushi Restaurants in Austin
name: Komé
notes: This popular North Loop restaurant serves unique rolls, like the Texas Surf and Turf, an ode to the state made with shrimp tempura and hanger steak, maki, and hand-pressed sushi. The traditional offerings are stellar, as is the lunchtime chirashi bowl. There are a few dining tables outside, and expect a crowd pretty much all the time.

article_title: The Best Sushi Restaurants in Austin
name: Craft Omakase
address: 4400 North Lamar Boulevard, Suite 102, Austin, Texas 78756
notes: This Rosedale restaurant is Austin’s first sushi restaurant to earn a Michelin star. Run by Uchiko alums, the Japanese restaurant serves omakase only and focuses on letting ingredients shine through its nigiri, small dishes, and drinks. Think octopus with a touch of citrus or bluefin tuna topped with a dollop of caviar. Book reservations online for the uber-sleek indoor omakase counter.

article_title: The Best Sushi Restaurants in Austin
name: Tsuke Edomae
address: 4600 Mueller Blvd Apt 1035, Austin, TX, 78723, US
notes: Chef Michael Che is behind one of the best immersive omakase experiences in Austin, hands down. Reservations for this tiny eight-seat indoor sushi bar in Mueller are essential and must be made the second bookings become available — unless there’s a rare opening in the waitlist. The meals focus on edomae-style sushi, which is largely unadorned, so there’s little flash and flair — just excellent sushi.

article_title: The Best Sushi Restaurants in Austin
name: Musashino Sushi Dokoro
address: 2905 San Gabriel St Ste 200, Austin, TX, 78705, US
notes: West Campus is the perfect setting for this textbook-traditional Tokyo-style sushi. Open since 1993, Musashino has become an Austin icon, and many of the city’s sushi greats trained here, including Uchi’s Tyson Cole, Také Asazu of Kome and Uroko, and Fukumoto’s Kazu Fukumoto.

article_title: The Best Sushi Restaurants in Austin
name: Endo
notes: Endo, an unexpectedly great 10-seat omakase spot in West Campus, focuses on traditional edomae sushi. The traditional omakase is a 20-course menu that changes daily, but its $99 16-course menu is available on select dates and times. There’s also lounge seating to explore the restaurant’s substantial Japanese whisky collection.

article_title: The Best Sushi Restaurants in Austin
name: Uroko
address: 1023 Springdale Rd, Bldg 1, Suite C, Austin, TX, 78721, US
notes: The second restaurant from the Kome team is a stall in the Springdale General complex that’s been freshly anointed by the James Beard Awards. By day, it offers to-go hand rolls (there are indoor and outdoor dining areas if staying on-site), and by night, a 45-minute power omakase at the sushi counter with all the classics. There’s also an at-home omakase offering, and reservations are required for either omakase.

article_title: The Best Sushi Restaurants in Austin
name: Fukumoto
notes: Owner Kazu Fukumoto is a Musashino alum, which means he pays close attention to the cuts of fish he uses for every single piece through the more casual East Austin restaurant. The yakitori, like the savory tamago or juicy chicken thigh, and specials are worth trying. The dining room is open and airy, so the kitchen is on full display, but takeout is an option, too.

article_title: The Best Sushi Restaurants in Austin
name: Papercut
notes: Papercut takes bar décor to another level. The artwork on the walls isn’t just for show — the rotating works by local artists directly inspire its drinks menu. In the back, you’ll find Konbini, a collaboration with the Michelin-recognized Tare team, serving a tight menu of sushi. You don’t have a lot of opportunities to have good sushi with outstanding cocktails in Austin (most places don’t have a full bar).

article_title: The Best Sushi Restaurants in Austin
name: Toshokan
notes: The East Austin Japanese restaurant has to be the only Michelin-recommended sushi spot operating within a mini-golf course (don’t worry, after passing through the secret door, the interior reflects the high-end omakase experience this is). Chef Saine Wong treats every omakase seating at the sushi speakeasy-like a dinner party with friends, occasionally pulling down his guitar for a serenade. Toshokan’s mix of creative and traditional sushi plus a great cocktail and sake program earned it an Eater Austin Award for the best omakase of 2022. Reservations are essential and should be booked as soon as they drop.

article_title: The Best Sushi Restaurants in Austin
name: Uchi & Uchiko
address: 801 S Lamar Blvd, Austin, TX, 78704, US
notes: Uchi and Uchiko are classic Austin Japanese restaurants for good reason. Both have some of the best, most inventive sushi in the city. Prepare to spend money or opt for the more budget-friendly sake social hour. Takeout orders can be placed online (Uchi, Uchiko); there are DoorDash deliveries (Uchi, Uchiko); and there are indoor and outdoor dine-in services.

article_title: The Best Sushi Restaurants in Austin
name: Soto South
address: 1100 South Lamar Boulevard, Suite 2115, Austin, Texas 78704
notes: Rolls at this Japanese restaurant can be spendy but high-quality, like the decadent salmon with shaved truffles. There’s also great nigiri for a more simple date night. There are two locations — one in South Lamar with a nice patio and the original in Cedar Park that’s indoor-only — and both accept takeout orders online.

article_title: The Best Sushi Restaurants in Austin
name: Tancho Sushi
notes: There’s no shortage of exclusive, high-priced sushi establishments in Austin, which is why Tancho’s motto is “Omakase for everyone.” With two locations — one in West Oak Hill, and another in Rosedale — Tancho isn’t trying to reinvent the experience or offer gimmicks to get you in the door. Instead, they’re focused on fresh fish without many of the frills that typically accompany a high-dollar omakase. Visitors can choose between several omakase tiers, ranging from $31 to $61 and featuring a variety of hand rolls, sashimi, and nigiri. If you’re new to the world of omakase, Tancho is a great place to start without breaking the bank.

article_title: The Best Sushi Restaurants in Austin
name: Foodie + Tina
address: 2153 South Lamar Boulevard, Austin, Texas 78704, United States
notes: What began as a monthly pop-up omakase hosted in chefs Becca Seiler and Spencer Brown’s backyard barn will soon be a fully fledged brick-and-mortar. Set to open on South Lamar in the summer of 2026, the husband-and-wife duo will continue to build dinners around sustainably sourced produce and Gulf Coast fish (along with responsibly-caught options from the East Coast, West Coast, and Japan). With an ever-evolving seasonal menu, you never know exactly what you’ll get, but you can count on it being a creative fusion of traditional sushi with Texas accents, like the Texas persimmon miso or nori salsa macha. Until they open, you can keep an eye on Instagram to pre-order omakase boxes, or check the calendar for the next pop-up.

article_title: The Best Sushi Restaurants in Austin
name: Neighborhood Sushi
notes: On your first visit, you might be walking around in circles in a bit of a daze, glancing up from your chosen map app, muttering, “is this it?” Neighborhood Sushi is hidden really well on South Congress in a back alley, but once you finally open those doors, you’re transported inside a minimalist serene escape from Austin’s busiest street. The focus is on a broad selection of nigiri and sashimi like King salmon, fatty tuna, and fluke. Exploring beyond fish won’t leave you disappointed either, with mouth-watering Tempura vegetables, miso-glazed sweet potato yaki, and ube panna cotta on the menu too.

article_title: The Best New Restaurants in Austin
name: Roya
address: 7858 Shoal Creek Boulevard, STE C, Austin, Texas 78757, United States
notes: Chef Amir Hajimaleki’s Persian restaurant Roya has been in progress since 2018, and in February it finally opened in its full glory. Located on top of one of Hajimaleki’s other restaurants, District Kitchen + Cocktails, the rich blue space sets a sumptuous tone with intricate tilework, art, and unique chandeliers. Drawing from Hajimaleki’s upbringing in Iran, the restaurant serves dishes like mahiche gheymeh (saffron tomato braised lamb shank), wagyu kebabs with fenugreek seasoning and tarragon chimichurri, and a variety of maze spreads and bites, plus a full cocktail menu. Decision-haters can be guided with sofreh, the chef’s tasting menu based in Persian hospitality.

article_title: The Best New Restaurants in Austin
name: Tzintzuntzan
address: 2302 West North Loop Boulevard, Austin, Texas 78756, United States
notes: An absolutely stunning creation from the team behind nearby Mexican icon Fonda San Miguel, Tzintzuntzan (which means “place of hummingbirds” in the Purépecha language) finally opened in February as a fancy breakfast and lunch spot. Dishes include enfrijoladas, chilaquiles with an earthy red or piquant green salsa, and decadent chocoflan. There’s a to-go window with coffee and pastries for those in a hurry, but this is a place to linger. The airy dining room features Mexican art, dried flowers, and a view of the gorgeous walled-in patio that feels like a secret garden bordered by tall cacti with artsy sculptures and a soothing fountain.

article_title: The Best New Restaurants in Austin
name: Twin Isle
notes: Janelle Romeo, the chef who ran Shirley’s Trini Cuisine food truck, has opened Twin Isle at 1401 Rosewood Avenue in the spot formerly occupied by Project Pollo. The fast-casual restaurant serves flavors from her native Trinidad and Tobago, offering roti wraps, customizable bowls, jerk chicken, oxtail sandwiches, and more Caribbean classics. The tender oxtail is a must-try, and it’s even better as a topping on cheesy fries. Hours are Tuesday through Sunday from 11 a.m. to 8 p.m.

article_title: The Best New Restaurants in Austin
name: Small’s Pizza
address: 1023 Springdale Rd Bldg 1 Ste 1, Austin, TX, 78721, US
notes: Kelsey Small, from the New Haven, Connecticut area, moved to Austin, Texas, where he worked at Franklin Barbecue and later Bufalina. After a run as a food truck and pop-up, Small’s Pizza now has a permanent home in the Springdale General complex, serving New Haven-style pies with proper char and a wonderfully tangy tomato sauce — the type that dances just a little bit on the inside of your cheek.

article_title: The Best New Restaurants in Austin
name: Sugarwolf
address: 401 West 4th Street, #120, Austin, Texas 78701, United States
notes: Just six months after opening Restaurant Francois, Guy + Larry Restaurants opened a downtown bakery and coffee bar that’s seeing consistent crowds. Conor Smith helms the bakery, which churns out fresh breads, croissants, and pastries throughout the day — check the bake schedule online and don’t miss the pillowy soft cinnamon with tangy icing. There are also breakfast and lunch dishes, like a ham and cheese focaccia and a shredded kale and apple salad, plus a bar with coffee, tea, beer, wine, and cocktails. The interior leans industrial, but it’s still spacious and welcoming, with glistening pastries on full display.

article_title: The Best New Restaurants in Austin
name: Kappo Kappo
address: 600 West 2nd Street, Austin, Texas 78701, United States
notes: Kappo Kappo brings a personal take on Japanese dining to downtown Austin. Opened by twin brothers Haru and Gohei Kishi, veterans of kitchens across Tokyo, Paris, and LA, the 25-seat restaurant focuses on kappo, a style that invites diners to engage directly with chefs at the counter as they prepare an 11-course tasting menu. Expect wagyu from Texas and Japan, seafood flown in from Toyosu Market, and seasonal produce from local farms, all presented with a balance of Japanese tradition and French technique, a nod to the Kishis’ background growing up in France.

article_title: The Best New Restaurants in Austin
name: Restaurant Francois
address: 401 West 3rd Street, STE 100, Austin, Texas 78701, United States
notes: French food is having a moment in Austin right now, and this restaurant from Guy + Larry Restaurants (Salty Saw, Red Ash) is going all-in on the bit. The downtown restaurant, which opened in December, serves dishes like Maine lobster thermidor, duck confit, boneless Wagyu short rib bourguignon, and the beefy dry-aged steaks the restaurant group is known for. There’s also bar seating for those wishing to drop in for a drink. The art deco dining room is the perfect backdrop for the fancy fare — this is definitely a place to get gussied up for.

article_title: The Best New Restaurants in Austin
name: Shokunin
address: 1009 East 6th Street, Austin, Texas 78702, United States
notes: Phillip Frankland Lee and Margarita Kallas-Lee’s Scratch Restaurants have built their reputation on high-end tasting menus like Sushi by Scratch and Pasta|Bar, and the messy burgers at Not a Damn Chance Burger. Their latest, Shokunin, lands between those two worlds, a high-quality sushi bar that’s relaxed where diners can choose their own adventure — whether that means a moderately priced meal or spending a pretty penny on an omakase dinner for $145 or $250. With fish flown in weekly from Tokyo’s famed Toyosu Market, the quality shows.

article_title: The Best New Restaurants in Austin
name: Poeta
address: 1108 East 6th Street, Austin, Texas 78702, United States
notes: After a stint as a prix fixe restaurant, Poeta, the Michelin-recommended sibling restaurant to Intero, moved into East Austin Hotel in January. There are a la carte options like milk-braised pork and lasagne (the prix fixe menu will return in late February). Poeta brunch means the return of the infamous “wafflegato”— a waffle served with gelato and espresso butter. Poeta has also taken over the lounge area upstairs, serving cocktails and bites until midnight on weekends.

article_title: The Best New Restaurants in Austin
name: Konbini
address: 908 East 5th Street, STE 107, Austin, Texas 78702, United States
notes: Konbini, a late-night sushi spot collab between Papercut and Tare, began its pop-up in July. Chefs Michael Carranza and Danielle Martinez of Michelin-recognized Tare are running the kitchen, creating dishes that combine Japanese technique with Texan and coastal Mexican flavors. For now, it’s walk-in only. Head behind Papercut to find it.

article_title: The Best New Restaurants in Austin
name: Space Kat BBQ
notes: Space Kat opened in March serving smoked meats out of an offbeat converted schoolbus on Webberville Road, but — is this even possible in Texas? — there’s no brisket on the menu. Instead, pitmaster Jack Zizzo offers cuts like tri-tip, lamb ribs, hot links, and coppa along with expected sides like mac and cheese and coleslaw. Zizzo, who comes to Texas by way of Ohio and Southern California, met his partner Justin Payton while working at Barley Swine in 2015, so expect tender meats with the occasional fine-dining touch.

article_title: The Best New Restaurants in Austin
name: Oria
address: 1530 Barton Springs Road, Austin, Texas 78704, United States
notes: Oria has transformed the Zoe Tong space on Barton Springs Boulevard into a Mediterranean wonderland. The vibe is neutral colors with wood, stucco, and greenery everywhere and a wood-burning oven where diners can watch pita bread cooking while they sit with a fig leaf martini in hand. From the team behind East Seventh’s Space Cowboy, dishes include fire-roasted harissa branzino, smoked baba ghanoush, and Basque cheesecake.

article_title: The Best New Restaurants in Austin
name: Kinsho
address: 51 Rainey Street, STE 140a, Austin, Texas 78701, United States
notes: This sushi restaurant on Rainey Street opened in March from Nick Ford and Wade McElroy, the duo behind vibey North Loop Italian restaurant Rocco’s. Kinsho is not an omakase destination, instead it’s focused on a la carte nigiri, rolls, and skewers, with cocktails. The vibe is similar to Rocco’s, with dark booths and a cozy atmosphere tucked away from the noise of Rainey.

article_title: The Best New Restaurants in Austin
name: Ciccio Bomba
address: 1100 South Lamar Boulevard, STE 2140, Austin, Texas 78704, United States
notes: Comedian Tom Segura loved chef Gianba Vinzoni’s chocolate croissants at LA’s Cinque Terre West so much that, after moving to Austin, he partnered with the chef to open this bakery and cafe in his new hometown. After refining the concept at two downtown pop-ups, the flagship location of Ciccio Bomba opened at the South Lamar Union in May, serving daytime pastries, focaccia, and sandwiches along with a new dinner menu of entrees like pasta and grilled branzino. The space has a standing coffee bar as well as table seating for lingering.

article_title: The Best New Restaurants in Austin
name: Austin Oyster Co.
address: 2500 E. Cesar Chavez St. Austin, TX 78723
notes: Transport yourself to a New England summer in this chic Holly neighborhood restaurant that opened in March. Even oyster naysayers will be converted by the impressive selection of briny treasures, curated by founder Brendan Yancy. The clams diablo — littleneck clams in a Calabrian broth with bloody mary relish — reportedly made the menu after approval from chef José Andrés. Landlubbers will find dishes like steak frites and a tomato salad, plus cocktails, including a martini menu. Sit inside the airy dining room or outside on the patio deck.

article_title: The Best New Restaurants in Austin
name: Kiin Di
address: 2414 S Lamar Blvd Suite A, Austin, TX 78704
notes: Popular Thai food truck Kiin Di outgrew its trailer at Corner Bar and opened a full restaurant just down the street on South Lamar in January. Joining favorites like creamy curry crab and flaky roti are new dishes like pork ribs kati cooked in coconut milk along with cocktails. The dining room is big, open, and pretty nondescript, but, depending on which dish you order, you may not even notice, since your mouth will be on fire.

article_title: The Best New Restaurants in Austin
name: Boni’s
address: 1805 South 1st Street, Austin, Texas 78704, United States
notes: The team behind South First staple Lenoir opened a tapas bar on the same property in March. Boni’s, named after co-owner Jessica Maher’s Spanish great-grandfather, is housed in the 1930s bungalow on-site, and serves cocktails (think gin and tonic goblets and seasonal margaritas), wine, espresso, and tapas like oysters, a ham plate, and head-on shrimp. The interior holds a piano, family photos, and vintage bar furniture while the outside patio has a backyard feel with small shaded tables.

article_title: The Best New Restaurants in Austin
name: Leona Botanical Cafe & Bar
address: 6405 Brodie Lane, Sunset Valley, Texas 78745, United States
notes: Leona Botanical Cafe & Bar is a new five-acre beauty in Sunset Valley from the teams behind the Thai food truck Dee Dee and Mexican restaurant Veracruz All Natural. Leona marks Dee Dee’s first-ever brick-and-mortar location, serving their beloved pad kapow alongside coffee from the Australian-based Proud Mary, with drinks like a banana milk hojicha and Thai pumpkin latte. Rotating pastries come from local favorites like Rockman Bakery and Sour Duck Market. With a lush garden, ample outdoor seating, and a full bar, Leona is one of the most gorgeous and serene ways to grab a cup of joe.

article_title: The 38 Best Restaurants in Austin, Spring 2026
name: Himalaya Kosheli Nepali & Indian
must_try: Momos — the chewy, pan-fried Nepalese dumplings.
notes: Austin’s South Asian food scene has a small but strong contingent of North Indian and Nepalese restaurants. Of those, this casual Barrington Oaks restaurant is a standout: It serves the greatest hits from the Indian subcontinent, including momos, chow mein, chilis, dals, Himalayan goat curry, and biryanis. Inside, find a cozy, diner-like interior with booths flanking the far wall and 1980s-style lighting. Takeout orders can be placed online.

article_title: The 38 Best Restaurants in Austin, Spring 2026
name: Usta Kababgy
must_try: The lahmajun (meat flatbread) is a filling, ripe-for-any-time comfort food.
notes: With locations in North Austin and Round Rock, this casual Middle Eastern restaurant focuses on delectable halal grilled meats. The menu boasts a bevy of kebab options, such as lamb-and-beef adana and chicken, as well as shawarma bowls, wraps, falafels, and dolmas. Inside, find booth seating and friendly service. Online to-go ordering is available.

article_title: The 38 Best Restaurants in Austin, Spring 2026
name: House of Three Gorges
notes: The casual North Austin Sichuan restaurant, which has vowed to “keep Austin spicy” since it opened in 2020, offers some of the best regional Chinese in the city. Find specialties like red braised pork belly, spicy jumping fish, and brisket in a tangy radish soup; the mapo tofu, silky chunks of tofu bedded in a savory sauce, is also a diner favorite. The small dining room is typically packed on weekends, so consider getting there early, or take your order to go. Know before you go: Feel like staying in? Order your meal to be delivered through Uber Eats and DoorDash.

article_title: The 38 Best Restaurants in Austin, Spring 2026
name: Korea House
notes: Dining at the casual Korean restaurant in North Shoal Creek is a perfectly homey experience. The traditional menu boasts stews (budae jjigae and the seaweed-y miyukguk), kimbap, and kimchi jeon. Then, there’s the fun grill-it-yourself barbecue setup with all sorts of meats, such as galbi and tender duck breasts, and a wide variety of banchan. Order from its selection of wines, cold sake, select beers, or its yuja ade, a Korean honey citron tea combined with sparkling water, lemon, and mint. The physical space is beautiful, with a pond outside and many group-friendly tables, though to-go ordering is also available. Vibe check: Korea House is a place for the community: co-owner Mrs. Kim offers monthly free meals to those who need them.

article_title: The 38 Best Restaurants in Austin, Spring 2026
name: Bufalina Due
notes: Owner Steven Dilley and his pizzeria teams continue to impress diners at this Brentwood restaurant, which has an East Austin location with even more pasta options. The Neapolitan pies are simple but skillfully executed. Diners can’t go wrong with the exemplary margherita and the various special pies, which have included zucchini or potato pizzas, or appetizers like the Caesar salad or burrata with juicy peaches. Wine nerds will dig wine director Rania Zayyat’s intriguing and affordable bottle selection. Best for: This is a wonderful date night option, with fancy pizza, great wine, and cozy quarters. Both locations accept takeout orders as well.

article_title: The 38 Best Restaurants in Austin, Spring 2026
name: Paprika ATX
address: 6539 North Lamar Boulevard, Austin, Texas 78752, United States
notes: This beloved trailer expanded to a restaurant (with air conditioning) in 2025, and taco lovers rejoiced. Paprika exudes feel-good vibes in its compact new space, serving classic tacos like carnitas, tinga de pollo, nopalitos, and champiñones, along with tortas, quesadillas, and specials. Start with the juicy suadero or carnitas wrapped in a warm corn tortilla with spicy salsas, and don’t forget an agua fresca — they rotate often but are always refreshing. Best for: Paprika is a popular spot to take out-of-towners for the best tacos in Austin.

article_title: The 38 Best Restaurants in Austin, Spring 2026
name: Fonda San Miguel
address: 2330 W North Loop Blvd, Austin, TX, 78756, US
notes: This high-end restaurant in Allandale is a classic for a reason: Since the 1970s, Austin co-founders Tom Gilliland and late chef Miguel Ravago are credited with focusing on fine-dining Mexican food whose origins can be traced to regions like Oaxaca, Puebla, Veracruz, and Yucatan. Classic regional Mexican dishes include an array of chile rellenos, cochinita pibil, and wagyu steak tacos. Entering the restaurant is a truly transformative experience — the beautiful dining rooms decked out with lush plants and Mexican artwork will make you feel like you’re in a gorgeous Mexican villa. Best for: Special occasions. Fonda is gorgeous with excellent food, but cost-appropriate.

article_title: The 38 Best Restaurants in Austin, Spring 2026
name: Foreign & Domestic
notes: Chef-owners Sarah Heard and Nathan Lemley are still knocking it out of the park at this North Loop restaurant, which has been a neighborhood staple for 15 years — so much so that there would probably be local upheaval if any of its staple dishes were removed. The gruyere popovers with charred onion butter, homemade charcuterie, and rotating seasonal dishes like goat mafaldine and duck cassoulet have devout followings. Adventurous eaters should seek out the tasting menu, which makes skillful use of less common cuts of meat. With its small patio, intimate chef’s counter, and table seating, Foreign & Domestic works just as well for spontaneous meals as it does for special occasions — and it even offers online to-go ordering. Know before you go: Foreign & Domestic is a small restaurant with an open kitchen, so reservations are a good idea. Opt for the chef’s counter for a front seat to the magic.

article_title: The 38 Best Restaurants in Austin, Spring 2026
name: Komé
notes: Co-owners Také and Kayo Asazu offer their takes on homestyle Japanese fare at this laid-back North Loop restaurant that offers excellent quality at a reasonable price. The menu offers a little bit of everything: starters like brie tempura, creative rolls like the sunshine roll with salmon, mango, and avocado, and luxe cuts like otoro nigiri, when available. Drinks-wise, there’s surprisingly good house wine, beer, and sake. The interior is beautiful, with natural wood and calming blue tiles and artwork, and there’s a small outdoor patio, too. Know before you go: Stop by during the day for satisfying lunch-only options like filling ramen or loaded donburi bowls — the rich hokkai-don with salmon sashimi, ikura, and scallops

article_title: The 38 Best Restaurants in Austin, Spring 2026
name: Allday Pizza [Tarrytown]
notes: This summer, Allday closed its East Sixth trailer behind Daydreamer to focus on its two brick-and-mortar locations in Tarrytown and Hyde Park — and the move seems to be for good measure. In an era when everyone’s charging what seems like too much for a slice, Allday delivers and is worth every penny. The Austin upstart turns out New York–style slices and 18-inch pies made from 48-hour cold-fermented Barton Springs Mill flour dough. Standouts include the Sweet Sausage, topped with ricotta cream, mozzarella, crumbled sausage, soppressata, red onion, and Calabrian honey, and the Industry Plant, a bright, vegetarian pie layered with mozzarella, verde sauce, arugula, lemon, and Grana Padano. Know before you go: Sometimes it’s advertised on a menu, sometimes it’s not, but you’re gonna want to try out that pickle pizza.

article_title: The 38 Best Restaurants in Austin, Spring 2026
name: Uchiko
notes: Omakases may have taken over the country, and Uchiko may have expanded into a multi-state empire, but chef Tyson Cole’s second restaurant in Rosedale is still serving some of the best Japanese fare in Austin 15 years after its opening. Exceptional bites like simply garnished madai, the classic hama chili, and the cult-favorite milk and cereal dessert make up the very fabric of Austin dining. Despite the mainstays, the menu stays fresh with new and seasonal offerings, too, and a variety of seating means it’s easy to switch things up. Uchiko offers table service, a sushi counter, patio seating, a bar with some of the most delicious people-watching in town, and, surprisingly, takeout and delivery. Check out the happy hour for a slightly more wallet-friendly experience. Vibe check: Uchiko is the epitome of Austin fancy. The space is simple but gorgeous, and contains patrons wearing anything from shorts to suits.

article_title: The 38 Best Restaurants in Austin, Spring 2026
name: P Thai’s Khao Man Gai & Noodles [North Loop]
notes: The Eater Award-winning casual counter-service North Loop restaurant is a premier destination for comforting Thai Chinese dishes courtesy of chef Thai Changthong. The namesake khao man gai is the Thai iteration of Hainanese chicken, with tender chicken paired with chicken-fat rice, a sweet chili sauce, cucumbers, and chicken broth. Don’t skip the noodle soups or the daily specials. Takeout orders can be placed online, but if you can, dine in so you can take cute photos of your plates atop the restaurant’s gingham-print table covers. Know before you go: Take vegetarian friends elsewhere — all P. Thai’s dishes are made with meat.
hours_match_name: P Thai’s Khao Man Gai & Noodles [North Loop]

article_title: The 38 Best Restaurants in Austin, Spring 2026
name: Crown & Anchor Pub
address: 2911 San Jacinto Blvd, Austin, TX, 78705, US
notes: For almost 40 years, this casual North Campus pub has been slinging affordable bar fare like fried pickles, Frito pie, and some of the city’s best burgers to hungry college students and sports fans who can enjoy more than 30 beers on tap. The vibes here are immaculate, and walking in can feel like a time warp, as patrons shoot pool and chat with friendly bartenders, often with nary a phone in sight. Though the pub is utterly unpretentious, it takes food seriously — the beef is sourced locally, the fries are crispy, and everything is properly seasoned. Open 365 days a year, Crown & Anchor is a great place to take out-of-towners to showcase the Lone Star State’s motto: Friendship. Must-try dishes: The juicy burger — don’t forget the fries.

article_title: The 38 Best Restaurants in Austin, Spring 2026
name: KG BBQ
notes: Austin veritably brims with barbecue restaurants, but this MLK Michelin Recommended food truck from pitmaster Kareem El-Ghayesh stands out because of the incredible Egyptian-Texan menu and minimal wait times. Look for pork ribs glazed with pomegranate seeds and za’atar, kebabs, turmeric rice studded with brisket and tahini, and smoked meats, available by the pound or in rice bowls and wraps. KG is located at Oddwood Brewing, which has indoor and outdoor areas (think picnic-table vibes). Those in a hurry can also order takeout online. Best for: Families — Oddwood has an on-site playground, and KG makes a great macaroni and cheese.

article_title: The 38 Best Restaurants in Austin, Spring 2026
name: Dai Due
notes: This Cherrywood Texan restaurant is so regionally minded that even beer and wine are exclusively from the state. Equally satisfying for a lingering brunch or blowout feast at dinner, the Texas-forever menu under chef Jesse Griffith has heavier dishes like dry-aged wagyu rib-eyes, quails stuffed with boudin noir, grilled chicken hearts, and coffee-cured antelope leg filets. Diners can go lighter, too, with dishes like mixed greens salads and grilled cabbage. The decor vibe is industrial farmhouse — wood, brick, black accents, walls of preserves — and the outdoor patio has a vine canopy with twinkle lights. Best for: Showing out-of-town visitors why Texas is great.

article_title: The 38 Best Restaurants in Austin, Spring 2026
name: Este
notes: This Cherrywood restaurant from the team behind Suerte is the ultimate destination for quality coastal Mexican food in Austin — that’s precisely why the team, led by Fermín Núñez, won an Eater Award in 2023. Every dish guarantees big flavor: There’s messy camarones “el ricas” (buttery spicy grilled shelled shrimp), creamy salsa-drenched pan-seared halibut, and fresh raw bar offerings. Don’t sleep on the large, nearly plate-sized tostadas that come with its salsa array, which may be some of the best chips and salsa in town. The indoor dining room is cool and coastal, and the outdoor patio has a view of the large garden that supplies fresh produce for the restaurant group. Know before you go: Este can be pricey, but it runs happy hour for lunch service Friday through Sunday, including margaritas and excellent fish tacos.

article_title: The 38 Best Restaurants in Austin, Spring 2026
name: Jeffrey’s
address: 1204 W Lynn St, Austin, TX, 78703, US
notes: Jeffrey’s is a landmark Austin restaurant for a few reasons. It opened in 1975 as one of the first fine-dining restaurants in the city. In 2013 it got a facelift as an early project of Larry McGuire and Tom Moorman, now MML Hospitality, and cemented the group’s status as the adjudicator of cool. Today, the Clarksville restaurant offers an opulent fine-dining experience, with ice-cold martinis, fancy steaks, and caviar service delivered to deep, cozy booths or the upscale bar area. Know before you go: Want a taste of the rich life on a budget? Visit on a Monday for all-night happy hour with the same immaculate vibes.

article_title: The 38 Best Restaurants in Austin, Spring 2026
name: Olamaie
notes: This fancy Downtown Southern restaurant received a Michelin star for its sensational dining experience. Top Chef contestant chef de cuisine Amanda Turner and executive chef Michael Fojtasek collaborate on inventive plates, such as smoked cabbage and the blackened dayboat fish with blue crab sauce and Carolina Gold rice. Settle in on the pretty patio, or in the elegant dining room, or at the small bar, and pair your full dinner with one of its fantastic drinks, which include the Soup du Jour, a punch that rotates with the seasons, and the Book and a Blanket, a combination of chamomile mate gin, blanc quinquina, apple honey, lemon, and Suze liqueur. Vibe check: Think old-school Southern elegance. Located in a gorgeous old house, this is one of the fanciest restaurants in town, with top-notch service to match.

article_title: The 38 Best Restaurants in Austin, Spring 2026
name: Fabrik
address: 1701 East Martin Luther King Jr Boulevard, Unit 102, Austin, TX 78702
notes: At this fine-dining tasting menu restaurant in the Blackland neighborhood, co-owner and chef Je Wallerstein shows the delicate power of high-end vegan cuisine. The seven-course tasting menu is full of creative plates that pull from her Japanese, Italian, and Nordic training. This can mean seasonal dishes like springy focaccia with vegan butter and bean dip, and potatoes paired with smoked ricotta and mushrooms. There’s an optional wine pairing, or nonalcoholic options like kombucha or spritzes. The restaurant is sleek but small, so reservations are essential. Best for: This is a special occasion restaurant with impeccable service. Feel free to take sworn carnivores to show them the power of vegetables.

article_title: The 38 Best Restaurants in Austin, Spring 2026
name: Birdie’s
notes: This Chestnut neighborhood wine bar offers a friendly, casual dining experience with James Beard Award-recognized food and beverages. Every month, co-owner and chef Tracy Malechek-Ezekiel creates a new seven-course prix fixe menu of simple dishes done well. Expect handmade pasta, minute steak with a rotating vegetable like smoked cabbage, and wine-friendly seasonal appetizers like fried green tomatoes. Don’t skip the creamy soft serve drizzled in olive oil for dessert or the after-dinner drinks from co-owner Arjav Ezekiel. Know before you go: Though there are a few reservations available, the restaurant is mostly walk-in. There will almost certainly be a line to get in — but it will be minimal when the restaurant opens at 5 p.m. Stalk their Instagram, where they often post about the rare times the line is short.

article_title: The 38 Best Restaurants in Austin, Spring 2026
name: Nixta Taqueria
notes: Chef Edgar Rico has to be one of the country’s most lauded taco chefs, winning the James Beard Awards’ emerging chef of 2022 and Michelin Guide’s Texas Young Chef Award. Rico works magic into every single one of Nixta’s tacos and tostadas, pulling influences from fine dining, Mexican-American cuisine, the on-site garden, and his wife and co-owner Sara Mardanbigi’s Iranian background. The vegetarian options truly showcase his creativity: the beet tartare is an artist’s statement piece, with tender cubed jewels of beets perked up by avocado crema and a squeeze of lime. Even a bean and cheese taco is made rich with duck fat refried beans and a hunk of chewy panela cheese. For a more in-depth experience, check out Nixta’s omakase service, which includes a six-course meal for $95 per person with optional beverage pairings ($45 per person for non-alcoholic drinks, $65 for wine). For something quick, order takeout — though you’ll miss the lively garden patio. Must-try dishes: The ultra-rich duck carnitas taco and the beet tartare tostada even beet haters will marvel at.

article_title: The 38 Best Restaurants in Austin, Spring 2026
name: Ensenada ATX
notes: This friendly orange truck in the Arbor Food Park in Central East Austin has a small but transformative menu. Ensenada serves Baja-style fresh seafood perfect for hot days: think crispy fish tacos and a cóctel de camarón topped with ripe avocado (the avocado is technically optional, but shouldn’t be). The trailer is run by Liz Everett and Stephanie Everett Martin, a mother-daughter team from Ensenada, both of whom provide some of the kindest, most personalized service in Austin. Know before you go: Ensenada’s raw dishes — the cóctel de camarón and a ceviche tostada — are only available on the weekend.

article_title: The 38 Best Restaurants in Austin, Spring 2026
name: Better Half Coffee & Cocktails
notes: This all-day cafe was named Eater Austin’s restaurant of 2018, and still, the team, which includes co-owners Matt and Grady Wright and Matthew Bolick, continues to deliver fantastic service and convivial vibes. The West Fifth space feels appropriately Austin with its large patio shaded by an old live oak tree, great coffee, and a first-rate lineup of beer, wine, and cocktails. The laid-back dishes steal the show: Think breakfast sandwiches layered with crispy hash brown patties, a filling fried chicken biscuit, and the iconic cauliflower tots. Dinner includes things like a Taiwanese-style fried chicken sandwich; smoked butter chicken crispy rice; and the same earthy beans and greens dish you can order in the morning, topped with a runny fried egg. Bonus: There’s a large parking lot on-site. Best for: Casual first date. Brunch with friends. Coworking. Literally any occasion.

article_title: The 38 Best Restaurants in Austin, Spring 2026
name: Franklin Barbecue
notes: There’s a reason Franklin has sold out of brisket every day since opening in 2009. In a city known for smoked meats, Franklin stands out. Yes, the lines are long — but yes, the brisket is worth it, with a peppery bark that melts in your mouth. Franklin sticks to a classic menu, offering snappy sausage, ribs with a bit of chew, and moist turkey, alongside potato salad, coleslaw, and pinto beans, but it’s all executed perfectly every time. Know before you go: You can skip the line with a preorder — but be warned that waiting in line is half the experience.

article_title: The 38 Best Restaurants in Austin, Spring 2026
name: Lao’d Bar
must_try: Get as much of the menu as you can, but don’t skip the nam khao or the rib-eye skewer.
notes: The 2024 Eater Award-winning Far East Austin bar is chef Bob Somsith’s fully realized vision of fiery Laotian American cuisine, which he started during his food truck days. Lao’d Bar is all about full-on spices, from the nam khao (crispy fried rice with red curry) to its papaya salad, to the meaty skewers with dipping sauces (try the chicken with yellow curry and pineapple jicama slaw). First-timers should not miss the coconut escolar ceviche — escolar tuna cured with coconut water, coconut cream, and punchy vinegar — served in a fresh coconut bowl; it’s never too soon to order a second round. The converted space has garage doors for patio season and stellar tropical cocktails like the frozen guava-lanche margarita.

article_title: The 38 Best Restaurants in Austin, Spring 2026
name: Veracruz All Natural
open_for: Breakfast, lunch, dinner at some locations
must_try: Decide between the migas or a migas poblanas taco.
notes: At this essential Austin food truck, sisters Reyna and Maritza Vazquez make some of the best tacos in the city, served on handmade corn and flour tortillas with fresh salsas (the latter piled in a grab-as-many-as-you-need cooler). Go for breakfast, when tacos are loaded with varying combinations of eggs; meats like chorizo, sausage, and bacon; cheese; beans; and vegetables, or opt for its lunch options, which include the Doña Reyna Taco, a grilled chicken taco with mole negro, onion, and queso fresca. (Diners may also be surprised by its vegetarian options, which include vegan queso made with cashews.) It’s not a complete order without a juice or one of its aguas frescas — try the La Bomba with orange juice, pineapple, and spinach — and the migas taco, which is loaded with thick, crispy chips. Most locations include indoor areas, and all have outdoor dine-in areas and online ordering.

article_title: The 38 Best Restaurants in Austin, Spring 2026
name: Canje
must_try: The boldly spicy jerk chicken. It’s unforgettable.
notes: The East Austin Caribbean restaurant from the talented Emmer & Rye restaurant group was named Eater Austin’s best new restaurant in 2022, and it continues to stack accolades, including a James Beard Award recognition. Canje pays tribute to executive chef and pastry expert Tavel Bristol-Joseph’s Guyanese roots with buttery roti, wild boar pepper pot, and jerk chicken. Since it’s a Bristol-Joseph project, don’t overlook the desserts, especially the tres leches cake and the puzzlingly good Cassareep ice cream, which features hints of dark chocolate; dollops of sweet, tangy passion fruit jam; as well as crunchy puffed rice, salty pepitas, and delicate flowers. The upscale tropical vibe delightfully seeps into the fruity but balanced drinks, its large bar, and the small outdoor patio.

article_title: The 38 Best Restaurants in Austin, Spring 2026
name: Joe’s Bakery & Coffee Shop
notes: Founded in 1962 by Joe and Paula Avila, this historic Mexican bakery is a pillar of the East Austin community, now run by the Avilas’s granddaughter Regina Estrada. Diners will first encounter the exceptional daytime menu, with wonderfully tender and juicy barbacoa breakfast, excellent migas, filling lunch tacos like chicken and beef picadillo tacos served with diner’s choice of flour, corn, or crispy shelled tortillas, and rice and beans, and a hard-to-beat $12 rotating daily special served Tuesday through Friday with tea. The restaurant has also long been committed to providing resources and services to its community. Estrada is an advocate for East Austin, ensuring people are informed about local politics, and she even directly registers voters. Squeeze in at a table next to the tempting bakery case or on one of the outside tables. Vibe check: Very old-school. Joe’s has been open for over 60 years and was named a James Beard Foundation’s America’s Classic restaurant in 2023.

article_title: The 38 Best Restaurants in Austin, Spring 2026
name: Dee Dee
address: 1906 E Cesar Chavez St, Austin, TX, 78702, US
notes: After closing in 2025, beloved Thai restaurant Dee Dee has made a triumphant return — with new menu items, to boot — as part of the absolutely gorgeous Leona space in Sunset Valley. Familiar dishes include om gai, a spicy chicken soup, and the tofu stir fry, plus new bites like dumplings and Thai fried chicken. The new five-acre location reflects chef Lakana Sopajan-Trubiana’s own dedication to gardening, and provides a setting to linger in, with lots of native plants, a water feature, fire pits, a playscape, and an indoor area with coffee drinks and a full bar. (There’s also a location of taco favorite Veracruz on-site). It’s a casual spot that appeals to all crowds. Must-try dishes: The spicy, veggie-loaded tofu stir-fry, the comforting om gai, and the refreshing papaya salad

article_title: The 38 Best Restaurants in Austin, Spring 2026
name: Mercado Sin Nombre
notes: Off Pleasant Valley in East Austin, this coffee shop brews an excellent cup of joe that’s light on astringency and heavy on complex flavors. The signature Atole Cortado pairs espresso with masa-infused oat milk and a side of atole, a warm masa-based drink flavored with cinnamon and vanilla. There are seasonal offerings too like this summer’s Beet the Heat, an americano with golden beet juice, burnt honey, chile de árbol, brightened by atole. Though Mercado Sin Nombre is very serious about its coffee, the real star is the lean, masa-obsessed food menu. Biscuits, burritos, pancakes, and sandwiches all with masa as a focal point will have you daydreaming about them for years to come. Know before you go: The food can sell out fast here, so you may want to get there a few hours before closing.

article_title: The 38 Best Restaurants in Austin, Spring 2026
name: Odd Duck
notes: Odd Duck was a leader in the farm-to-table revolution in Austin when it opened in 2009, and is still making some of the best food in the city with a creative, ever-changing menu of small plates from chef Bryce Gilmore. Dishes at the casual Zilker restaurant might include braised goat pizza with pecan mole, spicy redfish chalupa, and tres leches made with cocoa nibs and grapefruit, all served within a rustic, homey dining room with floor-to-ceiling windows that let in a muted natural light. Don’t leave without one of its cocktails, which include palomas on draft, frozen margaritas, and an earthy Six Beet From the Edge, made with mezcal, tequila, Peychaud’s, rosemary-beet syrup, and lemon. Diners can also build their own Old Fashioned, choosing from a list of the restaurant’s top-shelf spirits, which is then mixed with Odd’s signature spiced syrup and Angostura bitters. Know before you go: Reserve in advance; seating options include indoor and outdoor tables or a spot at the wraparound bar to watch the open kitchen at work.

article_title: The 38 Best Restaurants in Austin, Spring 2026
name: La Barbecue
address: 2401 East Cesar Chavez Street, Austin, Texas 78702
notes: The city has an embarrassment of world-class smoky meats, and this East Austin spot has earned plenty of honors: a Michelin star, a spot on Texas Monthly’s best barbecue list, and a rave review from TikTok critic Keith Lee. The late LeAnn Mueller, of the legendary Texas barbecue family, launched the barbecue joint in 2012, and it’s now run solely by Mueller’s wife, Ali Clem. Go for the brisket, sausages, and any of the inventive sandwiches, and be sure to order the superb shells and cheese made with smoked vegetables for a spicy kick. Tailor your tray for a true La Barbecue experience, and chow down amid classic barbecue vibes indoors or on the patio. Know before you go: Be prepared to wait in line for at least an hour, but you can skip it by ordering ahead.

article_title: The 38 Best Restaurants in Austin, Spring 2026
name: Intero
address: 2612 East Cesar Chavez Street, Austin, Texas 78702
notes: Chef and co-owner Ian Thurwachter uses beautiful fresh produce from local farms and whole-animal cooking to create memorable Italian dishes like roasted duck breast over risotto, springy pastas like mafaldine with rosemary-tinged Bolognese, and pizzas at this restaurant on East Cesar Chavez. The menu changes constantly, but the pastas and anything with duck are great bets. Co-owner and pastry chef Krystal Craig is a chocolatier, so save room for desserts such as brown butter cake with cinnamon gelato — or at least take home some of her handmade chocolates. Best for: Intero is a pro date move — tasty local fare, cute dining room and patio, and nice without being too nice.

article_title: The 38 Best Restaurants in Austin, Spring 2026
name: Justine’s
notes: This East Austin French restaurant enshrines indulgence with rich dishes, late hours, and a festive atmosphere. Open until midnight on weekends, Justine’s serves French food like steak frites, escargot, duck confit, and ratatouille, along with a hefty burger topped with Gruyere cheese. There’s a blood-red dining room, but most seating is on the patio, which is draped in fabric and lit by chandeliers in the winter. Vibe check: Justine’s is absolutely a scene, and a popular spot for visiting celebrities who always think they are the first to discover this “hidden gem.”

article_title: The 38 Best Restaurants in Austin, Spring 2026
name: Bouldin Creek Café
notes: Beloved by residents of 78704 and beyond, this vegetarian cafe in Bouldin Creek from owner Leslie Martin offers a varied menu of diner fare paired with locally roasted coffee. The cafe provides a taste of old-school Austin in its design (a quirky Slackers vibe inside and out) and food, which features loaded vegetable taco plates with sides of crispy plantain chips. Breakfast is served all day with plenty of egg-centered dishes, like a potato leek omelet or breakfast tacos, but vegans can opt for tofu scramble instead. There is online to-go ordering, but at a place like this, it’s better to soak up the vibes. Best for: A casual breakfast catch-up with friends

article_title: The 38 Best Restaurants in Austin, Spring 2026
name: Amy’s Ice Creams
address: 3100 S Congress Ave, Austin, TX, 78704, US
must_try: The Mexican vanilla ice cream that started it all.
notes: An Austin institution for more than 40 years, Amy’s serves thick, creamy ice cream out of 14 fun-loving shops in the area (including one that beckons locals and travelers touching down at Austin-Bergstrom International Airport). Amy’s is an endearing scoop of the oft-mourned “Old Austin,” its shops decorated with weird creations from employees, some of whom perform tricks as they serve. An ever-changing roster of flavors and topping combinations, plus customizable cooling treats like milkshakes, ices, freshly baked waffle cones, and sundaes with hot fudge, seal this as one of the most iconic ice cream shops in the city. The Mexican Vanilla, which uses a unique Madagascar vanilla blended with a Mexican orchid, might as well be Austin’s official dessert. It’s an essential stop on a warm day, which is pretty much always in this town.

article_title: The 38 Best Restaurants in Austin, Spring 2026
name: LeRoy & Lewis [Restaurant]
must_try: Tender beef cheeks and that sinfully juicy burger.
notes: Pitmaster Evan LeRoy, Sawyer Lewis, and their team made a name for themselves with this Michelin-starred, new-school barbecue restaurant in Garrison Park. Think intensely flavorful meats, including beef cheeks, smoked chicken, and pulled hog, plus dishes like chicken-fried steak specials and burgers. It may be a meat-centric spot, but don’t skip the vegetables, especially the cauliflower burnt ends. Meats may run out at lunch, but there’s a full bar, dinner specials, and a nice patio, so don’t be afraid to visit later in the day.

article_title: The 38 Best Restaurants in Austin, Spring 2026
name: Distant Relatives
notes: The McKinney food truck at Meanwhile Brewing has grown into an essential Austin barbecue spot — there’s a reason Eater named it one of the best restaurants in the country and in the city in 2021. James Beard Awards semifinalist pitmaster Damien Brockway and his team showcase the foodways of the African American diaspora. There are succulent chicken legs paired with a chile-vinegar dip, pulled pork with tangy tamarind-molasses barbecue sauce, and superb sides like burnt ends mixed with black-eyed peas and barbecue jumbled rice. Grab something crisp and bubbly from Meanwhile, like its iced tea and lemon kolsh, Fore!, or its Italian-style pilsner. Know before you go: This truck’s host site at Meanwhile Brewing has a giant shaded outdoor area with picnic tables, a soccer field, and more food trucks, making it an excellent place for an outing with kids. There’s a small indoor seating area, too, for when the weather doesn’t cooperate.

article_title: The Best Restaurant and Bar Patios in Austin
name: Tzintzuntzan
address: 2302 West North Loop Boulevard, Austin, Texas 78756, United States
notes: If brunch in a sculpture garden is the goal, Tzintzuntzan is the spot for you. The patio at this breakfast and lunch spot — sibling to nearby Fonda San Miguel — features beautiful sculptures, a cactus garden, and a relaxing fountain, and the interior is equally lovely. Grab some mole poblano biscuits, a cocktail, or velvety coffee and enjoy a soothing escape from the real world.

article_title: The Best Restaurant and Bar Patios in Austin
name: Barrett’s Too
address: 103 E North Lp Blvd, Austin, TX, 78751, US
notes: Open from early until late, this vegan-friendly casual patio bar in North Loop is a great spot for extended hangouts. The bar serves cocktails and coffee (and doesn’t charge extra for nondairy milk) along with tacos and other casual fare. The patio has picnic tables, Adirondack surrounding fire pits, colorful murals, and a kid-friendly area for little ones to explore.

article_title: The Best Restaurant and Bar Patios in Austin
name: Draught House Pub & Brewery
notes: With worn wood tables and hearty pints, this ultra-casual Rosedale brewpub wouldn’t feel out of place in the Shire. Grab a beer, cider, or draft cocktail to enjoy at one of many shaded outdoor tables, though there is an indoor area as well. On-site kitchen Little House serves pub fare like fish and chips, burgers, and sausages.

article_title: The Best Restaurant and Bar Patios in Austin
name: Mozart's Coffee
address: 3825 Lake Austin Blvd., Austin, TX 78703, USA
notes: The spacious Lake Austin cafe wins for beautiful scenery (Water! Birds! Fish!). Though the coffee, Italian sodas, and baked goods take second place to the view, they’re really quite good. There are indoor areas with big windows, but why not get right next to the water?

article_title: The Best Restaurant and Bar Patios in Austin
name: The Butterfly Bar
notes: The Butterfly Bar is named that because it is an actual butterfly sanctuary. The Blackland bar has eclectic decor, cocktails, beers, wines, and Patrizi’s food truck which serves some of the best pasta in town — plus there are outdoor cats. What more could you need? It’s owned by nonprofit theater company the Vortex, so there are often performances and shows to enjoy, and drink purchases support local art.

article_title: The Best Restaurant and Bar Patios in Austin
name: Sour Duck Market
address: 1814 E Martin Luther King Jr Blvd, Austin, TX 78702, USA
notes: Of the Bryce Gilmore restaurants, this casual Blackland cafe, which is the center of the restaurant group’s baking operations, is the one with the best patio situation. The large outdoor space has misters, fans, shaded areas, sunny areas, and a chalkboard trailer for kids to play in. Try the wagyu smash burger, which is a steal during happy hour, along with excellent pastries, coffee, and cocktails.

article_title: The Best Restaurant and Bar Patios in Austin
name: Birdie's
address: 2944 E 12th St Unit A, Austin, TX 78702, USA
notes: Need a patio for a nice date night out? The no-reservations counter-service wine restaurant in Chestnut has received nods from Michelin, the James Beard Awards (for excellent food and beverage service), and Eater Austin for its excellent pastas, salads, and bistro fare that prioritizes locally sourced ingredients. The patio out back has seating options for groups, intimate dates, or solo evenings out. Get there early to avoid the line.

article_title: The Best Restaurant and Bar Patios in Austin
name: Nixta Taqueria
notes: Nixta may feel like a garden party, but this counter-service taco restaurant in East Austin has racked up recognition from Eater Austin, the James Beard Awards, and Michelin. Chef Edgar Rico serves luxe tacos, like duck carnitas, plus dishes that call on his wife and co-owner Sara Mardanbigi’s Iranian heritage, like rice custard with cardamom, turmeric, pistachios, and cinnamon. There’s a tiny indoor dining room but most seating is on the patio, which is adorned with colorful murals and flanked by the restaurant’s garden.

article_title: The Best Restaurant and Bar Patios in Austin
name: Better Half Coffee & Cocktails
notes: The Clarksville-adjacent all-day restaurant has a patio and menu that are highly adaptable for any occasion: first date, networking meeting, or post-bike brunch. Try one of the best breakfast sandwiches in town with an iced coffee under the shade of a centuries-old live oak tree, or come late and meet new people over cocktails and burgers and hearty salads on the communal benches, painted a friendly shade of pink. There’s a small indoor area, and Better Half also has its own parking area, which is incredible for a place this close to downtown.

article_title: The Best Restaurant and Bar Patios in Austin
name: Oria
address: 1530 Barton Springs Road, Austin, Texas 78704, United States
notes: Oria’s breezy patio feels like a trip to the Mediterranean with a neutral palette, plants everywhere, and gauzy curtains; it’s the patio of choice for anyone looking for the perfect photo op. The food is pretty typical Mediterranean fare — mezze, whole roasted branzino, and sorbets — but pita from the wood-fired oven is a fun addition. If photos aren’t a priority but fulfilling a late-night hummus craving is, the kitchen is open until midnight or later every day.
opening_hours: Sunday-Thursday 4pm-10pm Friday-Saturday 4pm-11pm Contact (512) 220-0204 contact@oriarestaurants.com; Sunday-Thursday 4pm-10pm; Friday-Saturday 4pm-11pm

article_title: The Best Restaurant and Bar Patios in Austin
name: Tamale House East
notes: Looking for a lush, tranquil escape before heading out on East Sixth? The historic Tex-Mex restaurant on East Sixth offers plentiful outdoor space in its plant-filled courtyard. It’s known for tamales, sure, but there’s also excellent breakfast tacos, enchiladas, and margaritas.

article_title: The Best Restaurant and Bar Patios in Austin
name: Kemuri Tatsu-ya
notes: Enjoy Eater Award-winning fare on the patio at this funky Japanese-Texas izakaya in the Holly neighborhood. Find unique dishes here like chili cheese takoyaki and a barbecue boat with serrano-miso barbecue sauce. The back area features plenty of covered and uncovered tables, and there’s a rambling indoor seating area, too, with walls covered in Japanese beer signs and deer heads.

article_title: The Best Restaurant and Bar Patios in Austin
name: Holiday
notes: This Eater Award-winning Govalle bar has three outdoor areas, perfect for sipping fantastic cocktails like a frozen Mexican martini or one of co-owner Erin Ashford’s punch creations. With a certain kind of West Texas charm, Holiday is lovely and approachable, featuring white chairs and natural wood, cushy sofas, and misters for the super hot days. The food menu is also strong, with snacks like crab deviled eggs or entrees like a crispy chicken schnitzel.

article_title: The Best Restaurant and Bar Patios in Austin
name: Justine’s
notes: Follow in the steps of every celebrity that comes through town and head to this French restaurant known for its late hours, hedonistic pleasures, and vivacious spirit. In the winter, the patio is covered with a tent and maximally decorated to become its own luxe destination. The menu offers dishes like escargot, duck confit, and a rich burger. A word of warning: Make a reservation or be prepared to wait.

article_title: The Best Restaurant and Bar Patios in Austin
name: Jacoby's Restaurant & Mercantile
address: 3235 E Cesar Chavez St, Austin, TX 78702, USA
notes: This rustic-chic Govalle restaurant has one of the most relaxing patios in town. Enjoy views of a quiet and pretty section of the Colorado River with refreshing cocktails and burgers that feature beef from the owner’s ranch. There’s an indoor dining room as well.

article_title: The Best Restaurant and Bar Patios in Austin
name: Perla's Seafood and Oyster Bar
address: 1400 S. Congress Suite B100, Austin, TX 78704, USA
notes: The vibe at this Bouldin Creek seafood restaurant is clean, polished, and rich, with signature blue and white umbrellas and fancy fonts. It’s one of Austin’s original high-end patio dining experiences, and still one of the best. Oysters, Champagne cocktails, and fresh seafood a la plancha are worth the wait, plus there’s a prime view of South Congress.

article_title: The Best Restaurant and Bar Patios in Austin
name: San Ginés [Austin]
notes: Take a mental trip to Spain with this all-day restaurant known for its churros. The back patio is gorgeous, with lots of plants, Spanish language tunes, umbrellas, comfy couches, large work tables, and a few fire pits. It’s open from coffee and pastries in the morning to salad and cava with lunch to evening cocktails. If the weather doesn’t cooperate, the indoor bar and seating area is equally vibey.

article_title: The Best Restaurant and Bar Patios in Austin
name: Lenoir
notes: The longtime favorite Bouldin Creek restaurant’s shady wine garden is an expansion of its vintage-chic interior. Another great spot for a nice date night, Lenoir is known for a great wine selection, unreal fluffy Pullman bread, and an ever-rotating menu of dishes made for hot weather. The wine garden serves the full menu, so try all of the Michelin-recommended fare.
