article_title: The Best Restaurant and Bar Patios in Austin
name: Radio Coffee & Beer
notes: The South Lamar coffee shop and bar is popular for its triple threat of excellent coffee, great food trucks, and casual patio. Take your pick of lounging spots: the shaded outdoor space, the patio, or the grassy lawn. Good luck choosing between beloved taco truck Veracruz All Natural, Shortwave Diner, and Michelin-recognized Briscuits for food. There is a second location in the McKinney neighborhood and a third in Rosewood which also have great patios.

article_title: The Best Restaurant and Bar Patios in Austin
name: Cosmic Coffee + Beer Garden
notes: This sprawling South Austin space is lush with sustainable plants and water features. The cafe serves coffee and cocktails (try one of the frozens) and is home to great food trucks like Pueblo Viejo for excellent tacos. There’s a second location in East Austin close to downtown with a good patio (and great tacos) too.

article_title: The Best Restaurant and Bar Patios in Austin
name: Leona Botanical Cafe & Bar
address: 6405 Brodie Lane, Sunset Valley, Texas 78745, United States
notes: South Austin’s latest patio destination has everything: an inventive cocktail list, excellent food options from Dee Dee Thai and Veracruz Tacos, a play area for kids, and a beautiful, sprawling outdoor area with fire pits and a stream running through the space. There are over 80 species of plants in the garden, which means the flowers and foliage will change with the seasons. Cocktails come from Caer Maiko Ferguson, and draw on South Asian and Mexican ingredients to complement the food, as in a Red Curry Lion’s Tail and a Oaxacan Old Fashioned. Order migas tacos and coffee for breakfast, and stay for cocktails and stir-fry (and mango sticky rice) for dinner.

article_title: The Best Steakhouses in Austin
name: Bartlett’s
notes: Forget fighting Downtown parking or waiting for reservations. The steaks at this underrated North Shoal Creek restaurant include filet mignon and a rib-eye marinated in a pineapple-sesame-ginger sauce. Those in search of the more elusive prime rib will be excited to see it on the lunch and dinner menu. Slide into a dimly lit booth or saddle up to the traditional wooden bar for classic martinis to accompany your beef.

article_title: The Best Steakhouses in Austin
name: Hopfields
address: 3110 Guadalupe St suite #400, Austin, TX 78705, USA
notes: While the menu at this French Heritage neighborhood gastropub doesn’t offer multiple beef options, the steak frites are a standout. It offers a choice of filet mignon drenched in butter and Dijon or a hanger steak au poivre, both served with a heap of the famous Hopfields’ fries. Make a reservation to sit in the back dining room or grab a seat and cozy up to the bar, where you can order drinks and food to your heart’s desire. Don’t miss the escargot dumplings.

article_title: The Best Steakhouses in Austin
name: Dai Due
notes: What’s a butcher shop without offering steak on its Texas-forever menu? This Cherrywood restaurant, which recently received a Michelin Bib Gourmand, prides itself on sourcing its meats locally and seasonally. The dining room and patio may be slightly barebones, but the bar seating overlooks the open kitchen, with plenty of action to observe. Sip a Texas-based wine to pair with your expertly cooked steak. Tip: Call ahead or check the website to see what steaks are available that day.

article_title: The Best Steakhouses in Austin
name: Jeffrey's
address: 1204 W Lynn St, Austin, TX 78703, USA
notes: This Clarksville institution is ideal for a romantic dinner date or special occasion, so plan to make reservations. Start with a classic martini before mulling over the slew of prime beef steak options of angus or dry-aged wagyu. Snag an intimate booth and save room (and extra time) for a decadent souffle.

article_title: The Best Steakhouses in Austin
name: ALC Steaks
address: 1205 N Lamar Blvd, Austin, TX, 78703, US
notes: Don’t be fooled by the baby strip mall location. This restaurant has been grilling steaks with a Southwestern twist for over 30 years. Though the name might’ve changed (it was formerly known as Austin Land & Cattle Co.), the casual vibes sure haven’t. Pass by the guitars on the walls and stroll across the saltillo tile floors to the dining room, where meats come to the table in all shapes and sizes. There are rib-eyes, New York strips, T-bones, tomahawks, porterhouses, and filet mignons, with a slew of topping and sauce options.

article_title: The Best Steakhouses in Austin
name: Aris
notes: Houston-based restaurant group and James Beard Award semifinalist Sof Hospitality opened its fifth restaurant in Clarksville, bringing a slice of the Mediterranean to traditional Texas steakhouses. Aris’s dry-aging techniques are on full display thanks to a glass case located directly next to the dimly lit curved banquettes. Use this as an opportunity to inform your pick from one of the many beefy options, which include a 34-ounce porterhouse for two and an A5 Miyazaki Japanese wagyu striploin. Complement steak mains with festive vegetable-focused sides, like honey harissa carrots or broccolini and asparagus with labneh.

article_title: The Best Steakhouses in Austin
name: Lonesome Dove Austin
notes: The Austin outpost of Fort Worth’s 25-year-old Lonesome Dove Western Bistro brings Tim Love’s signature live-fire cooking to a more laid-back, semi-casual setting. There is still a nod to the restaurant’s game-heavy roots, but the focus here lands more squarely on steaks, with hand-cut options including beef tenderloin and a massive wagyu tomahawk ribeye. Everything hits the table with gorgonzola black garlic butter, adding a rich, savory finish.

article_title: The Best Steakhouses in Austin
name: Hestia
notes: Executive Chef Kevin Fink and partner Tavel Bristol-Joseph (both from Emmer & Rye, Canje, and Ezov) oversee this Downtown grill house, which is aptly named for the “Greek goddess of the hearth.” Every dish at this one-Michelin-starred restaurant is touched by its impressive 20-foot hearth, which uses a fusion of fire techniques from around the world. This includes its Texas wagyu sirloin and rib-eye steaks. Many difficult decisions must be made: Choose between the sleek dining room or the airy patio. Then, relinquish some control and let the chef lead the way with the menu, or choose your adventure from the a la carte menu.

article_title: The Best Steakhouses in Austin
name: Bob’s Steak & Chop House - Austin Downtown
notes: This Dallas-based steak chain offers two locations: one in the heart of Downtown and one at the Omni Barton Creek Resort & Spa. Downtown is more refined, while the Barton Creek locale is more rustic, with historic flags adorning the walls. Both serve super classic and large portions of prime steaks, including porterhouses and tomahawk cuts, pork chops, seafood, and sides such as grilled veggies and macaroni and cheese.

article_title: The Best Steakhouses in Austin
name: Restaurant Francois
address: 401 West 3rd Street, STE 100, Austin, Texas 78701, United States
notes: The team behind Red Ash Italia and J Carver’s Oyster Bar & Chophouse embraces joie de vivre at this Downtown newcomer while still maintaining a sharp steakhouse focus. The space is ideal for date night, with a sultry 1930s supper club feel that includes glowing globe lights, brass accents, and warm, low lighting. Large dry-aged rib-eyes and porterhouses are priced by the ounce and built for sharing. The deep, funky richness from 35 days of aging pairs well with au poivre sauce. The steaks are the stars here, but don’t sleep on the rest of the menu: lean into martini flights, French-y entrees like beef bourguignon, and decadent lobster pasta. And make sure to save room for the soufflé of the day.

article_title: The Best Steakhouses in Austin
name: VanHorn’s
notes: Big Apple glamour takes center stage at this New York-inspired steakhouse, where the team behind Bill’s Oyster and Teddy’s Restaurant & Bar pairs wood panels and red leather booths with stiff martinis. The menu sticks to the classics, with the focus squarely on the steaks, including prime dry-aged cuts sourced from Pat LaFrieda Meat Purveyors. Expect all things beef, including filet mignon and New York strips, as well as a 45-day dry-aged porterhouse seared to a proper crust and bursting with deep flavor. Throwback sides, like creamed spinach or a wedge salad, round out the meal.

article_title: The Best Steakhouses in Austin
name: Dean’s Italian Steakhouse
notes: White tablecloths and low lighting set a classic steakhouse mood in this polished Downtown dining room, which mixes Italian flair with a modern edge. The menu goes all in on beef, featuring everything from USDA Prime dry-aged cuts to Texas and Japanese A5 wagyu, alongside showstoppers like a 36-ounce tomahawk rib-eye and richly marbled strip loins. While guests can customize their meal with various sauces and surf-and-turf add-ons, the steaks hold their own without much help. With a focus on high-quality, expertly cooked cuts (and prices to match), it’s an optimal spot for an expense account dinner — or a night when someone else is picking up the tab.

article_title: The Best Steakhouses in Austin
name: Murray’s Tavern
notes: Travis Tober’s East Austin hotspot leans into old-school tavern style with a serious respect for steak. Murray’s Tavern serves a tender, rare prime rib after 4 p.m., Monday through Saturday, and a classic 8-ounce New York strip all day. Both are right at home alongside Grandma Murray’s Rob Roy, a scotch cocktail that’s as no-nonsense as the bar’s vibe. Don’t miss the $35 steak frites and martini night on Wednesdays.

article_title: The Best Steakhouses in Austin
name: Garrison
address: 101 Red River St, Austin, TX 78701, USA
notes: Nestled into the bottom of Downtown’s Fairmont Austin, this Michelin-recommended high-end hotel restaurant features a bunch of steaks in its serene dining room. From the strip loin to the giant 24-ounce porterhouse served with ramp butter, all meats are cooked in the post-oak-fired grill. While there’s no set dress code, per se, people do tend to spiff up for a special occasion. For the ultimate indulgence, start your steak dinner with the ube blini and Black River Caviar.

article_title: The Best Steakhouses in Austin
name: Carve American Grille
notes: The Austin mini-chain offshoot of Perry’s, which has locations at Lantana Place and one up north in the Grove, is all about meat, which naturally includes steaks. Serving carefully chosen USDA Upper Choice or higher Midwestern beef, Carve’s steaks include bacon-wrapped, cherry-smoked filet mignon, 22-ounce bone-in rib-eye with caramelized onions, and butcher’s custom-cut steaks that are also available raw to take home. Go wild with steak toppers of fire-roasted oysters or a lobster tail.
opening_hours: Daily Happiest Hour Every day from 3 PM-6:30 PM; Monday through Friday from 11 am to 5 pm.

article_title: The Best Steakhouses in Austin
name: Justine's Brasserie
notes: Known for its festive outdoor winter tent and romantic vibes, Justine’s also serves a classic French steak frites dish. A 12-ounce grass-fed, wood-grilled rib-eye is plated with crispy fries, and a choice of sauce completes the fun atmosphere at this Govalle restaurant. Start with the escargots, and choose a funky French bottle of red to round out the meal.

article_title: The Best Steakhouses in Austin
name: Jacoby's Restaurant & Mercantile
address: 3235 E Cesar Chavez St, Austin, TX 78702, USA
notes: This Govalle Southern restaurant raises its own brand of beef on the Jacoby Ranch, which is used for steaks that are later dry-aged from 21 to 28 days. The chicken-fried steak and burger are staples, but the daily butcher’s cut, paired with mashed potatoes, broccolini, and black garlic fried onions, should not be missed. Make brunch or dinner a breezy occasion by dining on the spacious, rustic patio.

article_title: The Best Steakhouses in Austin
name: District Kitchen + Cocktails
address: 5900 W Slaughter Ln D, Austin, TX 78749, USA
notes: South Austinites don’t have to trek North for luscious cuts of beef. Both locations of this New American restaurant serve up various steaks, including wagyu New York strips, rib-eyes, filet mignons, and massive tomahawk rib-eyes. Choose your own adventure with sauces to top that beef, including chimichurri, Diane (cognac, jus, cream, butter, mushrooms), and N’awlins, a combination of Worcestershire, lemon, butter, cream, and thyme.

article_title: The Best Pizza in Austin
name: Chicago Slice
address: 900 East Braker Lane, STE 105, Austin, Texas 78753, United States
notes: Price: $$ If you’re a Midwesterner looking for true tavern-style pizza, North Austin’s Chicago Slice, which opened in May 2025, is here for you. A quick tavern-style primer: tangy sauce, bubbly browned cheese and any combo of toppings are all generously spread right up to the very edges of an ultra-thin crackery crust, then cut into compact, grid-style square slices (leaving four flavor-packed, bite-sized triangle corner pieces to fight your family for, IYKYK). And Chicago Slice does it right. They’ve got a great deep dish, too, which tastes just like tradition, as well as delicious hot wings. Pizza and beer may be a classic combo, but Chicago Slice’s top-tier craft cocktails will have you wondering why you haven’t been pairing Old Fashioneds with your pepperoni-and-mushroom pie this whole time. Try the Dove, which pairs tequila with elderflower, grapefruit, and a splash of soda.

article_title: The Best Pizza in Austin
name: Pedroso’s Pizza
notes: Price: $$ Once a trailer, now a modest brick and mortar in Crestview, Pedroso’s does it all, and they do it really freakin’ well. Their classic NY slices are some of the best in the city, but they’ve also got saucier, thicker American-style pies, thin-crust Trenton tomato pies, Sicilian-style pan pizzas and the similar, underrated GOAT of the menu, the grandma-style: Expect some of the most flavorful, olive-oil-crisped thick crust you’ve ever had in a perfect ratio with cheese, sauce and toppings. Pedroso’s also earns top scores for vibes. It’s a classic no-frills neighborhood joint with a counter for ordering and pickup, seating in the form of a few picnic tables on astroturf, and a bizarro local-favorite dive bar, the Christmas-all-year-round-themed Lala’s Little Nugget, snuggled next door.

article_title: The Best Pizza in Austin
name: Little Deli & Pizzeria
notes: Price: $ Considered one of Austin’s most underrated pizza spots, Little Deli and Pizzeria draws fans in from all over town for its Jersey-style pies and subs. With a second location in Windsor Park, the original Crestview restaurant offers a variety of pies each day, plus stromboli, soups, and sandwiches galore, including a diner-favorite Reuben and another stuffed with pastrami. Check the specials of the day, which most recently included a pesto pie with sausage and spinach pizza, in addition to its Italian lentil and tomato basil soups. Be sure to grab a slice of one of its homemade cakes. Curbside pickup orders can be placed over the phone or in person. Both locations are open for dine-in services, and outdoor areas are available.

article_title: The Best Pizza in Austin
name: Bufalina Due
notes: Price: $$ Sister to Bufalina, this East Austin restaurant slings expertly executed Neapolitan pies that have earned raves from critics and diners alike. Try different combinations, like the margherita; the fresca, a refreshing pie topped with mozzarella, prosciutto, arugula, lemon, and Parmesan; or the potato pie, which features crème fraiche, lots of garlic, and a cured egg yolk. Best of all, Bufalina focuses on wine, with plenty of natural varietals. Finish strong with the olive oil cake or the pear sorbet. Orders can be placed online for curbside pickup. The restaurant is open for indoor and outdoor dine-in services.

article_title: The Best Pizza in Austin
name: Pinthouse Pizza [Burnet]
notes: Price: $$ Pinthouse brews up some of the most popular beers in Austin – shoutout Electric Jellyfish IPA – and its four friendly, bustling locations are go-to spots for families, big social groups, after-work happy hours, and just about any other kind of casual gathering. But the pizza here is not just of the “meet your needs” variety. Pinthouse’s crowd-pleasing pies are chewy, with slightly puffy dough and the slightest char. Creative and well-balanced topping combos on both the permanent pies and the rotating specials (candied jalapeno and Texas-raised chicken? Fennel pollen and charred broccolini?) are something to look out for too. The carefully sourced ingredients – local when possible, from afar when that’s smarter are what make Pinthouse special. Example: the recent appearance of perfect, crispy, curly cupped pepperonis on their pies is thanks to a switch to the iconic, storied Ezzo Sausage Company based in Columbus, OH. Vegan and gluten-free options abound here, and the cauliflower-based GF crust is especially good.

article_title: The Best Pizza in Austin
name: Home Slice [North Loop]
notes: Price: $$ Home Slice was the NY-style pizza place in Austin when it opened in the heart of South Congress in 2005. Plenty of tasty contenders have cropped up in the last two decades, but Home Slice’s pizzas remain big, savory and satisfying, with a nicely curated selection of specialty pies (the No. 6, with giant discs of sausage, dollops of ricotta cheese, and roasted red peppers, offers one of the best flavor combos). Debate rages over whether a pizza with fried eggplant on top (The No. 3) is truly “New York-style,” but what’s more important is that it tastes good – especially if you add some of that ricotta cheese. These days, you can dine in under the dim-red lighting of the cozy original location or swing by the More Home Slice counter next door for a quick slice, but we recommend spreading out at the more expansive North Loop location, where children are likely to be frolicking on the lawn beyond the patio. Honestly, as Austin changes, this Home Slice stays feeling kinda like home.

article_title: The Best Pizza in Austin
name: Feral Pizza
notes: Price: $$ You have to plan a little to get a pie from Feral Pizza, a trailer tucked away in Hyde Park that’s only open from 4 p.m. to 8 p.m. Thursday through Sunday, but it’s worth it. The sourdough crust on these NY-style pizzas is sharply tangy, and crispy on the bottom. Order from a menu of specialty pizzas with appropriately wild names, like the Gatecreeper, with three meats and a drizzle of garlic oil, or the Panic Attack, an amped up pepperoni with pretty dollops of ricotta. Give their formidable grandma-style pizza a try, too. On Thursdays, Feral fires up classic ultra-thin, grid-cut tavern-style pies.

article_title: The Best Pizza in Austin
name: Allday Pizza
notes: Price: $$ Since opening its two locations in Tarrytown and Hyde Park in 2023, Allday has established itself as one of the best spots in Austin for a big, perfectly foldable NY slice with a flavorful, chewy crust and some great specialty topping combos. Even better, Allday offers a brilliant, customizable option called “The Sampler,” which allows you to mix and match 6 slices to create a beautiful and delicious rainbow of a frankenpie. Don’t forget to include a slice of the must-try, off-menu (but always available) fried pickle pie, which has become an instant Austin classic.

article_title: The Best Pizza in Austin
name: Lil Nonna’s
notes: Price: $$ The name of this pizza food truck helps spell out a lot. Parked at Tweedy’s, Li’l Nonna’s aims to be that comforting spot you stop at for food that feels like a warm hug. What’s different, though, is Li’l Nonna’s offers its 14-inch New York-style pizzas and 12-inch gluten-free pizzas with various vegan and vegetarian toppings. Go wild, and pile your pizza high with vegan mozzarella, beet-based pepperoni, seitan sausage, and smoky tempeh. For those who believe pineapple belongs on pizza, try out the Piña Jalapeño, topped with a combination of pineapple, pickled jalapeños, roasted garlic, organic tomato sauce, and homemade vegan mozzarella. Place takeout and DoorDash delivery orders online.

article_title: The Best Pizza in Austin
name: Oddwood Brewing
notes: Price: $$ This lively MLK brewery fires up fantastic personal-sized, 10-inch thin-crust pies with loads of toppings, including meats (anchovies to Italian sausages), vegetables (broccoli to olives), and cheeses (vegan to ricotta). The menu features more than pizza, with buildable strombolis, salads, Chicago beef sandwiches, and kids’ meals with vegetarian corn dogs, chicken tenders baskets with tater tots, and fruit cups. Pickup orders can be placed online; indoor and outdoor dine-in areas are available. And if a craving strikes for something other than pizza, KG BBQ, the Egyptian-Texas food truck, is also available onsite.

article_title: The Best Pizza in Austin
name: Marye’s Gourmet Pizza
address: 3663 Bee Caves Rd #4G, Austin, TX 78746, USA
notes: Price: $$ Pizza is just the starting point at this Westlake Hills pizzeria. Marye’s offers a full menu, featuring starters like meatballs, cheesy bread, and the lemon-feta hummus; sandwiches; salads; and rice and grain bowls. Pizza and flatbreads come with tons of different options, including specialty pies like the Que (barbeque sauce, mozzarella-provolone blend, chicken, red onions, jalapeños, cilantro, smoked gouda), and the choice to build your own, with vegan cheeses and gluten-free options for diners with dietary restrictions. Those looking for a takeout option to serve fresh later can purchase one of Marye’s “take and bake” dishes — frozen calzones, pizza rolls, or pans of lasagna that can be cooked later in the comfort of their homes. Takeout orders, curbside pickup, and deliveries can be placed online. Go between 11 a.m. and 3 p.m. for the weekday lunch special, which offers an 8-inch pizza, half a salad, and a drink for $18 (note: the pizza toppings change daily).

article_title: The Best Pizza in Austin
name: Baldinucci Pizza Romana
notes: Price: $$ Come hungry to Baldinucci Pizza Romana, where the Baldinucci family, inspired by the Long Island pizzeria their father founded in 1977, bakes pies that are big, beautiful, and downright delicious. This Westlake spot makes pizza two ways, New York-style and Roman. Both are top-notch, but the Roman style is really special: thick, square pies with formidable, airy crusts that are crispy outside and fluffy inside. And those beautiful crusts are piled high with toppings, from the standard American pepperoni to Italian specialities like mortadella with pesto, and thin-sliced potatoes with provolone and rosemary. For a balance of sweet and savory, try the fig and arugula, which is drizzled with balsamic glaze. And for those with dietary restrictions, gluten free, vegan, and vegetarian options abound.

article_title: The Best Pizza in Austin
name: East Side Pies
notes: Price: $$ For a crispy, flatbread-like crust and smart, balanced topping combos, turn to any of East Side Pies’ three locations. Pies like the ricotta-sauced, bacon-and-mushroom-topped Moontower and the garlicky, pesto-sauced Olivia (with a pile of Mediterranean toppings including artichokes and three types of olives) are uniquely craveable. The best toppings at this low-key Austin classic are the roasted cherry tomatoes (featured on the Epoch, named and created for the beloved coffee shop), jalapenos, and artichokes, but you can’t go wrong with any of the specialty pies. Take it to go by ordering online.

article_title: The Best Pizza in Austin
name: Small’s Pizza
address: 1023 Springdale Road, BLDG 1, Austin, Texas 78721, United States
notes: Price: $$ This New Haven-style pizzeria launched as an “apizza” (pronounced “ah-beets”) pop-up in 2021 before transitioning into its counter-service digs in Far East Austin. Still, it sticks to its Connecticut roots, serving its signature whole pies, which are asymmetrically sliced, deeply charred, and absolutely irresistible. Choose between classic toppings like pepperoni or anchovies, or a very New Haven choice like sausage and peppers. Looking for something different? Try Small’s Pizza of the Week concoctions (the garlic mashed potato is especially unique). You can’t go wrong. Fun fact: This style of pizza is called “apizza” (pronounced “ah-beets”) in Connecticut.

article_title: The Best Pizza in Austin
name: Via 313 [East 6th Restaurant]
notes: Price: $$ Once just a single food truck on East 6th dishing some of the only quality Detroit pies in Austin, Via 313 has grown into a behemoth of a local chain, with several dine-in locations across the greater Austin region, including ’burbs like Cedar Park, as well as spots in Houston, San Antonio, and even out west in Utah and Colorado. They’ve got thinner-crust tavern-style pizza, too, but the star here remains the honking 4-square pies on a pillowy bread base baked with a rim of crispy golden cheese. Meateaters can’t go wrong with the Detroiter, which features both standard flat pepperonis and delicious, crispy-cupped natural-casing peps.

article_title: The Best Pizza in Austin
name: Palm Pizza
address: 1701 E Cesar Chavez St, Austin, TX, 78702, US
notes: Price: $$ The folks at Palm not only make their own dough, they also make their own mozzarella cheese — and, boy, does it make a difference. This charming green-accented pizza joint on E. Cesar Chavez, run by the folks behind Bufalina and Bufalina Due, knows how to balance the flavors on its NY-style pies. The namesake, the Palm, is a fine example: It’s got that tasty mozz, tangy red sauce, and a heap of milky, also-homemade stracciatella that plays very, very well with thick parm shavings and a few fresh basil leaves. Other good options include a sausage and pepper white pie and a pepperoni and mushroom spiced up with pickled jalapeno and serrano ranch drizzle, not to mention plenty of hefty deli sandwiches and occasional pasta specials. You can order by the slice or the pie, either for takeaway or to dine at their adorably retro collection of outdoor picnic benches on fake grass (with, yes, a few palm trees scattered around).

article_title: The Best Pizza in Austin
name: The ABGB
notes: Price: $$ The vibes at this indoor-outdoor South Austin venue/brewery/pizza joint/hangout spot are great: a little rock n’ roll and also welcoming to anybody and everybody. The beer and the food are just as good. Pair a Hell Yes Helles with a wagyu beef-topped pie, or an Industry Pilsner with a Malabar Spinach slice, which has a pecan pesto sauce, some briny veggies, and a lemon herb yogurt. If you’re a clam pizza fan, ABGB has one of the best in Austin. And try some deviled eggs as a starter — they’re “maw-maw’s special recipe.”

article_title: The Best Pizza in Austin
name: Side Eye Pie
address: 3901 Promontory Point Dr, Austin, TX 78744, USA
notes: Price: $$ The co-founder of the now-closed pizza truck Dough Boys opened his own new truck last summer, which is now parked at Meanwhile Brewing. Side Eye slings fun Neapolitan pies, including the Jalapeño Business (bianco red sauce, basil, pepperoni, pickled jalapeños, and a drizzle of hot honey), the Local Trip (garlic cream sauce with herbs, fontina cheese, and local oyster and crimini mushrooms), and the Pretty Fly for a White Pie (garlic cream sauce topped with fontina, mozzarella, parmesan, and whipped ricotta). Take these personal-sized pies into the brewery or dine out on the patio. Don’t forget the beer. Order online from the South Congress location or the truck at Meanwhile.

article_title: The Best Lunch Restaurants in Austin
name: Épicerie
notes: Tucked into a quiet corner of Rosedale, Épicerie offers a relaxed midday retreat with a French Louisiana twist. The lunch menu here strikes a cozy balance between rustic and refined, with fried shrimp po’boys and refreshing Caesar salads. The side patio, shaded and cheerful, feels like a neighborhood secret. Lunch runs from 11 a.m. to 3 p.m. daily. Pro tip: Impress your dining companions by ordering beignets for the table.

article_title: The Best Lunch Restaurants in Austin
name: Komé
notes: The lunch menu at the North Loop Japanese restaurant is somewhat different from its dinner offerings. There are teishoku (lunch combinations) with mains like karaage, katsu, and hangar steak, we well as sushi lunch specials and rolls, bento boxes, and donburi and chirashi bowls. It’s available from 11 a.m. to 2 p.m. on weekdays and until 3 p.m. on weekends. There are indoor and outdoor dine-in services.

article_title: The Best Lunch Restaurants in Austin
name: Uncle Nicky's
notes: Set along a busy stretch in Hyde Park, Uncle Nicky’s brings Euro-café flair to the neighborhood with pastas and sandwiches stuffed with meatballs, porchetta, and chicken Parmesan, all of which pair well with its crisp salads. The interiors are clean and casual with counter service and a small patio. Save room for a pastry or gelato; don’t forget to fulfill your espresso fix. The all-day/lunch menu starts at 11 a.m., and diners can also visit its location in South Lamar.

article_title: The Best Lunch Restaurants in Austin
name: Josephine House
notes: Just off West Lynn in Clarksville, Josephine House is where weekday brunch doubles as an elegant lunch escape. The daily menu, available from 9 a.m. to 2:30 p.m., includes a carrot-ginger muffin, a burger layered with Gruyère and a farm egg, and more polished takes on daytime classics. Whether seated inside the sunlit cottage or under the ivy-covered pergola, the whole experience feels like a pause button for the day.

article_title: The Best Lunch Restaurants in Austin
name: Sour Duck Market
address: 1814 E Martin Luther King Jr Blvd, Austin, TX 78702, USA
notes: Located between Cherrywood and East Austin proper, Sour Duck Market is an easygoing spot where the line moves quickly, and the lunch hits the mark. Think breakfast sandwiches, double cheeseburgers, fish ‘n’ chips, and seasonal salads, including the recent version with field greens, maple mustard vinaigrette, shaved radish, strawberries, fennel, mole granola, and queso fresco. The beer garden patio is expansive and shaded, perfect for lingering, but diners can settle inside for the air-conditioning on hot days.

article_title: The Best Lunch Restaurants in Austin
name: Better Half Coffee & Cocktails
notes: Better Half blurs the line between coffee shop, diner, and all-day hangout in the West Fifth corridor. Weekday lunch, served from 8 a.m. to 3 p.m. in a counter-service set-up, brings heavy hitters like a double cheeseburger, cauliflower tots, and lighter bites, including salads. The outdoor seating, dotted with pastel pink picnic tables, stays lively but never too loud, ideal for midday meetings or solo battery recharges.

article_title: The Best Lunch Restaurants in Austin
name: Walton's Fancy & Staple
notes: A longtime player in the Warehouse District, Walton’s anchors a corner of West Sixth with old-school deli charm. Flaky quiches, well-stacked turkey sandwiches, and an eye-catching pastry case anchor a menu that consistently satisfies. Inside, the antique décor and floral touches make it feel timeless, in the best way. Open daily from 7 a.m. to 4 p.m.

article_title: The Best Lunch Restaurants in Austin
name: Uroko
notes: East MLK’s Uroko, inside the Springdale General complex, takes a minimal approach to maximal flavor. Build a hand roll lunch with options like avocado with yuzu hama toro, spicy tuna, and beef tataki. The sleek, counter-style space is calm and efficient, well-suited to a quiet midday break. Lunch is served Monday through Saturday from 11 a.m. to 2 p.m. and until 3 p.m. on Sundays.

article_title: The Best Lunch Restaurants in Austin
name: Austin Rotisserie & Sandwicherie
notes: In Downtown’s bustling Fareground food hall, Austin Rotisserie stands out with its focused French menu and unfussy execution. Rotisserie chicken comes plated with golden potatoes or layered into baguette sandwiches alongside herbed mayo and greens. Indoor and shaded outdoor seating make this spot ideal for everything from lunch-hour meetings to post-trail bites. Grab a café au lait macaron for later. Open daily from 11 a.m. to 9 p.m.

article_title: The Best Lunch Restaurants in Austin
name: Fresa’s South First
notes: In the Bouldin Creek neighborhood, Fresa’s nails the lunch brief with two flavors of wood-grilled chicken (achiote and citrus and Yucatan spice), and serves a variety of vibrant bowls and tacos that travel well from plate to patio. The space feels like a mini vacation, with palm trees, bright tile, and breezy tables. Lunch is available daily from 11 a.m. to 10 p.m. A North Lamar drive-thru makes lunchtime even easier, while there’s another location in Westlake Hills.

article_title: The Best Lunch Restaurants in Austin
name: Bouldin Creek Cafe
notes: Bouldin Creek Café has long defined South First with its South Austin quirk and sprawling, meat-free menu. Standouts include the tofu BLT and rotating veggie stir-fries, served in a mural-covered space that stays busy. It’s open from 8 a.m. until 10 p.m. (11 p.m. on weekends), and it works at nearly any hour. The coffee runs strong enough to power through the afternoon slump.

article_title: The Best Lunch Restaurants in Austin
name: Habanero Cafe
address: 501 W Oltorf St, Austin, TX 78704, USA
notes: In a modest house on Oltorf near South First, Habanero Café delivers classic Tex-Mex with no frills and full flavor. From 11 a.m. to 3 p.m., find daily lunch specials like enchiladas, crispy taco plates, and chicken mole done right. The dining room is small but bustling — expect a short wait and plenty of regulars.

article_title: The Best Lunch Restaurants in Austin
name: Frazier’s Long & Low
notes: On East Riverside, Frazier’s offers diner fare with a dive bar personality. It opens at noon daily, serving up smash burgers, chili cheese dogs, and cold beer with a side of ’90s nostalgia. The backyard patio is roomy and relaxed, ideal for casual meetups or playing hooky from work. It’s unapologetically laid-back.

article_title: The Best Lunch Restaurants in Austin
name: Pueblo Viejo Riverside
address: 2410 E Riverside Dr Ste H8, Austin, TX, 78741, US
notes: This East Riverside taco spot — one of several Pueblo Viejo locations across Austin and its only brick-and-mortar — is a go-to for fast, full-plate lunch. Whether craving al pastor tacos or a quesadilla plate with rice and beans, the portions are solid, and the salsas pack heat. There’s covered seating on-site, or take your order to go. Open daily from 7 a.m. to 10 p.m.

article_title: The Best Lunch Restaurants in Austin
name: Golden Boy
notes: Chef Nick Middleton and the team behind Y Comida run this Fareground food hall stall on Congress, serving a tight menu built around bao, bowls, and salads. The lineup includes gochujang beef bao, miso butter salmon bowls, and a Buffalo chicken salad that leans indulgent without going overboard. Golden Boy keeps lunch-focused hours, open daily from 11 a.m. to 3 p.m.

article_title: The Best Lunch Restaurants in Austin
name: Uchiba
notes: Uchibā adds a refined midday option to the Downtown lineup, with lunch served daily from 11:30 a.m. to 2:30 p.m. Highlights include the sushi oma with hama chili, the tuna hand roll, and the ba lunch — a set featuring sesame ramen salad, a salmon hand roll, and two pieces of nigiri alongside a choice of karaage, pork dumplings, or a hot fried chicken bun. A slightly limited sushi and izakaya dinner menu is also available for lunch (sorry, the diner-favorite mushroom dumplings aren’t available during lunch). The dining room blends dark wood, soft lighting, and clean lines for a polished and inviting look. It’s the kind of midday meal that feels like an occasion without breaking the bank.

article_title: The Best Lunch Restaurants in Austin
name: LeRoy & Lewis
notes: Garrison Park’s Michelin-starred LeRoy & Lewis rewrites the barbecue rules with bold choices like smoked beef cheeks, cauliflower burnt ends, and smoked burgers. Lunch runs Wednesday through Monday from 11 a.m. until sellout — and be warned: sellout comes quick. The flatiron, beef cheeks, and brisket specifically go fast, so showing up early pays off. Grab a tray from the cafeteria-style line, pair it with a frozen Big Red Sangria, and call it a power lunch.

article_title: The Best Lunch Restaurants in Austin
name: Tiny Boxwoods
notes: In Brykerwoods, Tiny Boxwoods turns lunch into a garden party in its adorable, light-filled dining room. From 11 a.m. to 2:30 p.m., you’ll find salads with roasted chicken, avocado toast, and the famous chocolate chip cookies that definitely do count as a meal. The setting is polished but breezy, with whitewashed brick, soft lighting, and a patio that always seems to have just the right breeze.

article_title: The Best Lunch Restaurants in Austin
name: Bartlett’s
notes: Lunch is served daily from 11 a.m. to 4 p.m. at this North Shoal Creek steakhouse, with a menu that includes prime rib sandwiches, sashimi tuna salad, and steak frites. Inside, it has dark wood, leather booths, and quiet sophistication — an ideal backdrop for business lunches, long conversations, or simply hiding out for an hour. The martinis don’t hurt, either.

article_title: The Best Lunch Restaurants in Austin
name: Fish Shop
address: 1401 East 6th Street, #201, Austin, Texas 78702, United States
notes: This East Sixth seafood joint brings a laid-back, California coastal sensibility to the workday lunch crowd, open weekdays starting at 11:30 a.m. The menu is comforting and indulgent, with the “secret” Filet O’ Fish sandwich and the Le Beef burgers drawing regulars. Those with more time can treat lunch like an occasion, pairing oysters or other raw seafood with a properly made martini. It’s an easy spot to blur the line between a quick bite and a midday escape.

article_title: The Best Lunch Restaurants in Austin
name: Paprika ATX
address: 6539 North Lamar Boulevard, Austin, Texas 78752, United States
notes: The brick-and-mortar version of Paprika ATX recently opened near its original food truck location in the Highland neighborhood, and it’s already drawing long lunchtime lines that, thankfully, move quickly. The interior is sparse, but strong air conditioning and an upbeat crew make waiting easier. Suadero is a must-order, rich and deeply flavorful, while quesitacos pile on extra cheese for a yet more indulgent bite. Paprika is open Tuesday through Saturday starting at 11 a.m., and Saturdays often bring al pastor specials that give another reason to show up early.

article_title: The Best Italian Restaurants in Austin
name: Rocco’s Neighborhood Joint
address: 5001 Airport Boulevard, Austin, Texas 78751, United States
notes: Restaurateur Wade McElroy (Love Supreme Pizza and Teddy’s Cocktail Bar) and the former executive chef at Sammie’s, Nicholas Ford, opened the doors to Rocco’s Neighborhood Joint in September on Airport Boulevard. The spirit of the spot is meant to channel casual Italian eateries you might find in the Northeast with a small menu of antipasto, salads, pastas, and Italian-only wines. This cozy spot has reservations for large parties of six to eight, everyone else will get seated on a first-come, first-served basis.

article_title: The Best Italian Restaurants in Austin
name: Umarell
address: 4413 Guadalupe Street, Austin, Texas 78751, United States
notes: After working at a high-end Emmer & Rye restaurant, chef Mike Graffeo wanted to create a place true to his New Jersey roots. This casual counter-service spot in Hyde Park serves all the red sauce classics: a big-as-your-head chicken parm sandwich, rigatoni and meatballs in a tomato cream sauce, and end piece lasagne fried in garlic oil until crispy. Don’t miss the decor details like the car-themed bathroom or the photo from the 1940s featuring Graffeo’s grandfather and great-grandfather with friends in Newark. Grab a stool at the counter, cram the family into a cushy booth, or order online.

article_title: The Best Italian Restaurants in Austin
name: Asti Trattoria
address: 408 E 43rd St Ste C, Austin, TX, 78751, US
notes: This longtime Hyde Park restaurant attracts the all-too-rare intergenerational crowd with Italian favorites including pizza and hand-made pasta, plus “could’ve fooled me” gluten-free versions. For any-time-of-day pasta, try the creamy carbonara; brunch-goers should not miss the tart Italian toast, made on focaccia with ricotta and lemon curd, either. The indoor dining room has a long bar for those looking to get a quick bite, and there’s a patio with twinkle lights for couples with time to linger. Pro tip: The restaurant also accepts to-go orders online.

article_title: The Best Italian Restaurants in Austin
name: L’Oca d’Oro
notes: This Mueller restaurant offers sustainable, locally sourced dishes like super-fresh mozzarella, sourdough focaccia, and an ever-rotating menu of pastas with both classic options (rigatoni alla ’nduja with spicy sausage) and inventive takes (fusilli with mustard cashew cream). If lasagna is on the menu, get it. Solo diners can park at the small bar with wine or a cocktail, friends can enjoy the Mueller bustle on the patio, and there’s room for large parties in the dining room.

article_title: The Best Italian Restaurants in Austin
name: Patrizi’s
notes: House-made pastas anchor the menu of this food truck at the Vortex theater and bar in Cherrywood. Have a very Austin date night on the whimsical patio or inside the funky indoor space with chewy fettuccine in Karah’s Diavolo, a lemon-olive-oil-garlic-rosemary-chile mix topped with an egg yolk. Sides like beef and pork meatballs and roasted beets round out the menu. There’s often a lengthy wait on weekends, though if you’re ordering to-go, you can order online. A second location in Austin Lake Hills is more of a counter-service restaurant with a larger menu of sandwiches and pizzas.

article_title: The Best Italian Restaurants in Austin
name: Cipollina
address: 1213 W Lynn St, Austin, TX, 78703, US
notes: This smallish, cozy restaurant in the heart of Clarksville focuses on Italian dishes with an Austin twist. For lunch, try the meatball sub on a crusty baguette. For dinner, go for a crispy thin-crust pizza, or try the Bolognese made with brisket, bacon, tomato, and tagliatelle, topped with a snowfall layer grana padano. There are a few tables for outdoor dining, but most seating is indoors — though Cipollina does offer online takeout orders.

article_title: The Best Italian Restaurants in Austin
name: Quattro Gatti
notes: A hidden gem by the Capitol on Congress Avenue, Quattro Gatti is a great option for downtown diners seeking Italian dishes. The menu includes pizzas blasted in a wood-fired oven for a perfectly chewy crust, traditional pastas, and hefty entrees. Don’t skip the creamy gelato for dessert. Despite the regal feel of the building, which dates back to 1876, the golden-yellow dining room is cozy and warm, and there is an outdoor patio to take in the downtown scene.

article_title: The Best Italian Restaurants in Austin
name: Sammie’s Italian
notes: This Jersey-proud red sauce joint from vibe-conscious hospitality group McGuire Moorman Lambert offers Italian American classics in an upscale atmosphere downtown. Look for dishes like chicken Parmigiana, spaghetti and meatballs from the chef’s family recipe, and smothered pork chops with maitake mushrooms and a marsala brandy cream sauce. Dine on white tablecloths indoors, enjoy a meal al fresco, or order takeout online.

article_title: The Best Italian Restaurants in Austin
name: Red Ash
notes: The scents of wood-fire-grilled steaks and red snapper a la plancha lure diners into this modern two-story downtown Italian restaurant. But even with many tables in the restaurant, reservations should be made well in advance. Locally sourced meats and an assortment of seafood are roasted on a custom-built wood-burning grill and oven. The restaurant offers a variety of hand-made pastas, such as spaghettini alle vongole and tagliolini with fresh blue crab, too.

article_title: The Best Italian Restaurants in Austin
name: Il Brutto
notes: Only dine indoors at Il Brutto if you have to; the restaurant, set in the hubbub of East Sixth, boasts a large shady patio for people-watching plus occasional live music, the ideal backdrop for ultra fresh hand-made pastas. The lamb ragu pappardelle is a great order (even to-go), or snack on charcuterie and wood-fired pizzas over drinks. Don’t miss out on the rich desserts, like a ricotta semifreddo, before leaving.

article_title: The Best Italian Restaurants in Austin
name: Bufalina
notes: For over a decade, Bufalina has been one of Austin’s go-tos for blistered Neapolitan pizza. It also slings fantastic house-made pastas such as pappardelle with ragu and orecchiette with sausage and preserved lemons. Sip a glass of natural wine from the extensive list outside while waiting (and on weekends, there will almost certainly be a wait) for a table inside to watch the wood-fire oven in action. There’s a second location up in Brentwood, which has a slightly smaller menu. Both locations offer limited takeout orders.

article_title: The Best Italian Restaurants in Austin
name: Intero Restaurant
notes: Both the dark indoor dining room and cute outdoor patio at this Holly restaurant offer great settings for a date night. Solo diners can also unwind over plates of pasta or hefty meat dishes at the long bar. Intero strives to avoid waste, utilizing every part of its ingredients wherever possible. The menu includes hand-made pasta like ricotta gnocchi with duck confit, plus lots of seasonal local vegetables. Co-owner Krystal Craig also makes incredible chocolates, so be sure to order some to take home after dinner. Sibling restaurant Poeta is another great choice for Italian fare.

article_title: The Best Italian Restaurants in Austin
name: Artipasta
notes: Opened by immigrants from the city of Mantova in the Lombardy region of Italy, this Italian food truck offers some of the most true-to-Italia dishes in Austin. Try pastas like the indulgently rich lasagne or casarecce with sage and almond pesto. There’s a second location up in the Linc that has an indoor dining area and a patio. Takeout orders can be placed online.

article_title: The Best Italian Restaurants in Austin
name: Trattoria Lisina
notes: It’s worth the trek out to Driftwood for a quick getaway from reality at this Hill Country restaurant on a sprawling vineyard. Wander the on-site garden, which recalls the Italian countryside, before entering the spacious trattoria, which has a bustling open kitchen and large fireplace. Lisina, owned by the same family that operates Mandola’s, offers a menu full of Italian favorites like crispy pizzas and fresh pastas. Try the pappardelle al cinghiale (in wild boar ragu). Takeout orders can be placed online, but half of the reason to come here is the sunset views from the patio.

article_title: The Best South Lamar Restaurants and Bars in Austin
name: Odd Duck
address: 1201 S Lamar Blvd, Austin, TX 78704, USA
notes: Enjoy fancy Texan cuisine in a welcoming environment from notable Austin chef Bryce Gilmore and his team. The restaurant has been killing it since 2013, and while some aspects of the place may seem dated (small plates served on mismatched china), the food and service have only gotten better. Gilmore works closely with local farmers to showcase seasonal ingredients on a constantly evolving menu. Diners might find a Thai-influenced sweet potato salad with crisped pork belly, or birria de chivo (braised goat) pizza with pecan mole and queso Oaxaca (there will always be a seasonal cast-iron cornbread, and it is a must-order). There’s a sizable patio, tables, and a long bar wrapping around the open kitchen that’s perfect for solo diners. But make reservations—even after a decade, Odd Duck books up.

article_title: The Best South Lamar Restaurants and Bars in Austin
name: Asahi Imports
address: 3005 S Lamar Blvd Suite B-105B, Austin, TX 78704, USA
notes: The South Austin location of the low-key Japanese market is the place to go for tasty onigiri, bento boxes, and soft serve. The Japanese konbini-style aisles make for a fun browsing and to-go experience (this is the place to grab lunch for today and tomorrow). Speed up your visit by ordering onigiri or bento boxes for takeout.

article_title: The Best South Lamar Restaurants and Bars in Austin
name: Uchi
notes: Tyson Cole opened this Japanese restaurant on South Lamar back in 2003, when sushi was still new to landlocked Austin. It’s been a hit ever since, earning the chef a James Beard Award and spawning an empire across the country. The restaurant serves fresh, sustainable, and responsibly fished ingredients in dishes like its signature hama chili (yellowtail with ponzu, thai chili, and orange) and wagyu beef hot rock. For discounted prices on select food and drink options, get there early for the happy hour, aka the sake social. Incredibly, you can get the award-winning sushi for takeout or delivery, but its elegant interior or more casual patio makes it a great choice for date night.

article_title: The Best South Lamar Restaurants and Bars in Austin
name: Soto South
address: 1100 South Lamar Boulevard, Suite 2115, Austin, Texas 78704
notes: When Uchi is impossible to get into, or you’re looking for a different approach to Japanese cuisine, head to this restaurant in the Lamar Union development. The restaurant has well-crafted nigiri, sashimi, and sushi, alongside larger dishes like uni pasta and lobster tempura, as well as lunch specials. (The wine list is nice, too.) The dining room is unremarkable, with a sushi bar, tables, and a small patio, so ordering takeout is not a big loss here.

article_title: The Best South Lamar Restaurants and Bars in Austin
name: Gibson Street Bar & JewBoy Sliders
address: 1109 S Lamar Blvd, Austin, TX 78704, USA
notes: With an ample patio, the Zilker bar is perfect for both intimate dates and large groups. The casual bar serves well-made classic cocktails, and numerous television screens make it a favorite for watching sports. Even better: there’s a location of El Paso-Jewish slider truck JewBoy here. Takeout orders for JewBoy burgers, mini latkes, and fries can be placed online or in person, and eaten indoors or outdoors at Gibson.

article_title: The Best South Lamar Restaurants and Bars in Austin
name: Ramen Tatsu-Ya & Tiki Tatsu-Ya
address: 1234 S Lamar Blvd, Austin, TX 78704, USA
notes: Some of the state’s best ramen can be found over at Ramen Tatsu-ya. Choose from ramen made with pork bone broth, chicken shoyu broth, or the almond-milk-and-mushroom combination for a vegan option, and add a spicy bomb if you’re adventurous. There are also small bites like gyoza and karaage, and everything is available for dine-in or to go. When you’re done slurping ramen, see if you can snag a reservation or a rare walk-in seat at its neighboring sibling cocktail bar Tiki Tatsu-ya, which has so many lights, sound effects, and outrageous drinks that it feels like cocktail Disneyland.

article_title: The Best South Lamar Restaurants and Bars in Austin
name: It’s Italian Cucina
address: 1500 S Lamar Blvd Ste 110, Austin, TX, 78704, US
notes: Find handmade pastas and other Italian dishes from this under-the-radar Zilker neighborhood restaurant. The white-tablecloth spot with a grand piano (!) serves up Northern Italian pasta (try the gnocchi alla vodka), bruschette, risotto, and much more, all rounded out by a stellar wine list and original cocktails. Not feeling the dining room or patio? Order online for pickup or delivery.

article_title: The Best South Lamar Restaurants and Bars in Austin
name: El Raval
address: 1500 S Lamar Blvd #150, Austin, TX 78704, USA
notes: The Zilker tapas restaurant underwent an overhaul courtesy of new owner and chef Laila Bazahm. Taking its cues from its namesake neighborhood of Barcelona, the flavorful menu spans This tapas restaurant has a flavorful menu of small dishes (think pulpo a la brasa, pan con tomate, and cheese selections), crispy-bottomed paellas (including seafood, duck, and vegetable), and desserts like crema catalana (all available to go). Cocktails also shine here, with well-executed classics along with red and white sangria. The name is a nod to Barcelona’s iconic, diverse neighborhood of the same name. Chef Laila Bazahm was raised in the Philippines and traveled around Southeast Asia and Spain before landing in Austin.

article_title: The Best South Lamar Restaurants and Bars in Austin
name: Blue Aspara
address: 1904 South Lamar Boulevard, Austin, Texas 78704, United States
notes: When it opened in 2025, this new Cambodian food truck immediately became a star on the Austin food scene. The menu includes fragrant dishes like fish amok (steamed fish in coconut curry) and the Nom Pahng sandwich (ground chicken or pork in a baguette with vegetables, topped with a heavenly coconut Sriracha sauce). If there’s a wait for your food (likely), Blue Aspara is conveniently parked outside a plant shop and next to revered coffee truck Desnudo, or order ahead to skip the line.

article_title: The Best South Lamar Restaurants and Bars in Austin
name: Brown's Bar-B-Que
address: 1901 S Lamar Blvd, Austin, TX 78704, USA
notes: The Zilker barbecue truck at Corner Bar is known and loved for its ribs, brisket, chicken, and pulled pork. Don’t forget the sides: The homemade potato salad and coleslaw are worth attention. Diners can take their orders to go or sit at one of the truck’s adjacent picnic tables with their barbecue spreads and, if they know what’s good for them, a large sweet tea.

article_title: The Best South Lamar Restaurants and Bars in Austin
name: Taqueria de Diez (South Lamar)
address: 2110 South Lamar Boulevard, STE C, Austin, Texas 78704, United States
notes: The original location of this Taqueria de Diez is a kind of taco speakeasy downtown, but the secret’s out with this expansion to South Lamar. Taqueria de Diez serves simple but awesome tacos, quesadillas, and burritos, like a succulent al pastor and meaty carne asada. It’s also a great option for any late-night taco cravings, open until 1 a.m. Thursday through Saturday. Follow the ordering instructions on the bright red signs to eat inside or on the tiny patio, or order ahead to-go.

article_title: The Best South Lamar Restaurants and Bars in Austin
name: Proud Mary Coffee
notes: An import from Australia, Proud Mary is paradise for coffee snobs. The cafe offers fancy pour overs and espresso, along with brunch fare like a sausage roll, ricotta hotcakes, and avocado toast. The interior is light and airy, with soaring ceilings and light wood, and there’s a small, rustic outdoor patio. Order takeout if necessary—it can get busy.

article_title: The Best South Lamar Restaurants and Bars in Austin
name: Loro Austin
notes: The Zilker pan-Asian-ish Texas smokehouse restaurant in the South Lamar neighborhood is a good crowd-pleasing destination — after all, it’s led by Uchi’s Tyson Cole and Franklin Barbecue’s Aaron Franklin. There’s brisket served with a chile gastrique (that’s restaurant-speak for a sweet-and-sour sauce), smoked turkey breast with yuzu chutney, and rice bowls alongside nice frozen cocktails. Dine in a booth in the huge A-frame, on the giant patio, or order for delivery or takeout.

article_title: The Best South Lamar Restaurants and Bars in Austin
name: Kiin Di
address: 2414 S Lamar Blvd Suite A, Austin, TX 78704
notes: Once a food truck at Corner Bar, beloved Thai restaurant Kiin Di has opened in its own space with a larger menu. Find fare like roti with a curried dipping sauce, gai tod (traditional Thai fried chicken served on its own or with ginger rice and pickled cucumbers), and stir-fried noodles. First-timers would also do well to order the old-school pad thai and Thai boxing chicken wings served with dry spices (like spicy chile lime) or a sticky and spicy tamarind sauce. If you don’t want to eat in the indoor dining room, order to-go.

article_title: The Best South Lamar Restaurants and Bars in Austin
name: Matt's El Rancho
address: 2613 S Lamar Blvd, Austin, TX 78704, USA
notes: Dip into Austin history at this classic South Lamar Tex-Mex restaurant, where the margaritas are plentiful and the iconic Bob Armstrong dip is the ideal queso. Here, you can find such big-plate fare as the “deluxe” dinner with chile con queso, a beef taco, pork tamale, smothered enchilada, rice, and refried beans, all served in a vintage-feeling dining room. Matt’s is known for its patio surrounding a big fountain, but there’s also a rowdy dining room and takeout is available.

article_title: The Best South Lamar Restaurants and Bars in Austin
name: El Naranjo
address: 2717 S Lamar Blvd STE 1085, Austin, TX 78704, USA
notes: The chef and co-owner of the South Lamar Oaxacan restaurant, Iliana de la Vega, won a James Beard Award in 2022 for good reason: Her food is outstanding, from the perfect mole negro de Oaxaca with duck breast to the weekly ceviche. Find it all in a bright, modern dining room with sunflower-yellow walls. Located at the bottom of an apartment complex, the restaurant might seem unimpressive from the outside, but you’ll be truly transported once you take a bite.

article_title: The Best Coffee in Austin
name: Leona Botanical Cafe & Bar
address: 6405 Brodie Lane, Sunset Valley, Texas 78745, United States
notes: Leona Botanical Cafe & Bar is a new five-acre beauty in Sunset Valley from the teams behind the Thai food truck Dee Dee and Mexican restaurant Veracruz All Natural. Leona marks Dee Dee’s first-ever brick-and-mortar location, serving their beloved pad kapow alongside coffee from the Australian-based Proud Mary with drinks like a banana milk hojicha and Thai pumpkin latte. Rotating pastries come from local favorites like Rockman Bakery and Sour Duck Market. With a lush garden, ample outdoor seating, and a full bar, Leona is one of the most gorgeous and serene ways to grab a cup of joe.

article_title: The Best Coffee in Austin
name: Radio Coffee & Beer
notes: South Austin’s cafe and bar features good coffee, lots of outdoor space, and a whole lot of parking. For food, there are famed food trucks: Veracruz All Natural (tacos), Dee Dee (Thai), and Briscuits (barbecue and biscuits). In the evenings, it has booze too, and live music. It has a second location in East Austin on Montopolis Drive.

article_title: The Best Coffee in Austin
name: Sightseer Coffee
notes: This coffee roaster opened up shop this tiny Bouldin Creek Cafe within Suger Mama’s Bakeshop. Everything is made using its beans, from the classic espressos and lattes to its more inventive seasonal and house creations. The Teaches of Peaches is fantastic, a cold brew with peach nectar and mint syrup and a spicy Taijin rim.

article_title: The Best Coffee in Austin
name: Mercado Sin Nombre
notes: The beloved farmers market stand expanded with this lovely alleyway coffee shop in the Holly neighborhood. The menu is short but sweet, making use of its Mexico-sourced beans in standard coffee drinks as well as special ones. Look no further than the atole cortado — aka the masa milk shorty — where espresso paired with a candied orange slice is accompanied by a sidebar of the masa beverage. The masa-based foods are great too, including biscuits, pancakes, and house-made Twinkies.

article_title: The Best Coffee in Austin
name: Flitch Coffee
notes: The east side coffee shop is set up in a vintage trailer with plenty of outdoor seating. Beans come from local roasteries like Creature Coffee Co., among others, along with bites from Pueblo Viejo and Sour Duck.

article_title: The Best Coffee in Austin
name: Sa-Tén
notes: The Japanese-ish Govalle cafe is serving up espresso drinks alongside the saifa (its take on Vietnamese coffee), plus cold brew. There are also larger Japanese daytime entrees like katsus and loaded toasts. It has a large, industrial space with exposed cinder block walls, rustic tables, and loads of plants, while there outdoor tables offer some sunlight. Takeout is available online.

article_title: The Best Coffee in Austin
name: Desnudo Coffee
notes: The East Austin coffee trailer has worked up a frenzy of interest in its coffee beans sourced directly from local farmers, which it uses in espresso drinks and nice lattes like the shoyu butterscotch or the brown sugar miso. There’s also a downtown cart location. It sells its beans online.

article_title: The Best Coffee in Austin
name: Patika
address: 100 Congress Ave Ste 125, Austin, TX, 78701, US
notes: Alas, Patika closed down its flagship cafe on South Lamar but thankfully, its spirit lives on with its downtown location within an office building lobby. There are its thoughtful coffees (the vanilla latte remains one of the best in town) as well as house-made baked goods and hearty daytime dishes. Takeout orders are placed online or in person.

article_title: The Best Coffee in Austin
name: Cenote
address: 1405 East 7th Street, Austin, Texas 78702, United States
notes: A longtime East Side favorite has returned. After briefly closing in the spring of 2025, Cenote returned in September, this time with new digs on Seventh Street. The new location has all of the old favorites like chilaquiles, migas, breakfast sandwiches, and strong coffee, but also has a full bar this time around too. Any visit here, cannot go without the horchata cold brew, which is so good you can take it right before bedtime and won’t even regret feeling wired.

article_title: The Best Coffee in Austin
name: The Brew and Brew
address: 500 San Marcos St Ste 105, Austin, TX, 78702, US
notes: The East Austin coffee shop has an elegant menu of espresso, filter coffee, and cold brews from local roasters. It is light and warm, and friendly baristas can guide the uninitiated through the latest and greatest trends in the coffee world. It serves sandwiches and bagels for the daytime, while the evening menu has burgers, fries, and chicken sandwiches. Plus there’s a full bar and loads of beers and wines. Takeout orders can be placed online or in person.

article_title: The Best Coffee in Austin
name: Figure 8 Coffee Purveyors
notes: This small Central East Austin coffee shop serves up its own roasted beans, along with other craft roasters. It sells shippable coffee beans, which patrons will find themselves standing next to bags of while waiting for their name to be called. It has a second location in North Loop.

article_title: The Best Coffee in Austin
name: EL TIGRE COFFEE
notes: El Tigre Coffee serves up small-batch coffee from Brazil and Honduras in a vintage Airstream trailer. The operation is simple, with some tacos and pastries and benches out front, but this is perfect for an easygoing stroll on a brisk morning. Duck into Keep Looking next door, an indie newsstand with a curated selection of zines, international magazines, and vintage clothing.

article_title: The Best Coffee in Austin
name: Trianon Coffee
address: 3654 Bee Caves Rd Ste A, West Lake Hills, TX, 78746, US
notes: The classic Westlake coffee shop offers all sorts of coffees from single-origin to microlots, and bites from Rosen’s Bagels, Tacodeli, and Quack’s. The colorful shop has comfy armchairs, tables for two, a high top for laptop warriors, and a private room for groups or meetings. Or take it to go in person or order from Uber Eats and GrubHub.

article_title: The Best Coffee in Austin
name: Kyoko Coffee
notes: This cute Japanese-inspired coffee truck (if one can call a Honda van a truck) describes itself as “a whole vibe,” and more than lives up to the promise. Some coffees have wacky names like Cause of Death and Kyoko Dreams of Ube. It also offers left-of-center options like Miso Pironcillo Umai and Umami Mocha, plus standard drinks like a cortado, cappuccino, and lattes. It also has golden and ube milk to spike that cuppa with something different and sweet little cookies for a treat. Visit it at the Highland Collective.

article_title: The Best Coffee in Austin
name: Barrett’s Coffee
address: 713 W Saint Johns Ave, Austin, TX, 78752, US
notes: The Crestview coffee shop and roastery serves up an array of espresso drinks, including a chocolate-orange blossom latte, iced coffees, matcha, and chai. This is the kind of shop one can park at for awhile, with a large, industrial indoor space and a few outdoor tables. Coffee bean and cold-brew growler deliveries can be ordered online but for those who want a cup to go, it must be gotten in person.

article_title: The Best Dive Bars in Austin
name: Sam's Town Point
notes: Way South Austinites don’t have to go far for a classic dive bar experience with live music, cold drinks, and a laid-back backyard vibe that’s away from the hustle and bustle of Downtown. This low-key bar is known for its chill atmosphere, friendly bartenders, and plenty of good tunes. There’s also a sprawling outdoor area with picnic tables.

article_title: The Best Dive Bars in Austin
name: Deep Eddy Cabaret
address: 2315 Lake Austin Blvd, Austin, TX 78703, USA
notes: One of Austin’s oldest dive bars, Deep Eddy Cabaret, has been serving cold beer and good conversation since 1951. This no-frills spot near Deep Eddy Pool is known for its jukebox, neon signs, pool table, and laid-back atmosphere. Despite being in an area not known for dive bars, Deep Eddy Cabaret remains a classic hangout for those seeking old-school Austin charm. It has a rich history and a loyal crowd of regulars.

article_title: The Best Dive Bars in Austin
name: Donn's Depot
notes: A dive bar in an old train station in Clarksville seems just about right. There’s live music and often free two-stepping dance lessons. It’s fantastically fun, and a photo shoot in the bathroom is a must. Even Dua Lipa visited when she was in town for ACL.

article_title: The Best Dive Bars in Austin
name: The 04 Lounge
notes: The Dawson neighborhood bar off South Congress Avenue is small and dark with a side patio. There are several pool tables and arcade games. The drinks are cheap. The regulars are friendly. It’s a quintessential Austin dive bar to its core. ‘Nuff said.

article_title: The Best Dive Bars in Austin
name: Dirty Bill's
address: 511 Rio Grande St, Austin, TX 78701, USA
notes: This place is not a misnomer – it’s a dingy but welcome respite amid West Sixth Street bachelor/bachelorette parties. The intimate Downtown bar, adorned with string lights, stickers, and a signature gnome, is a popular industry haunt due to its lively atmosphere and simple drinks. Often crowded with patrons, the narrow space is always buzzing with energy - whether it’s people dancing, drinking, or simply enjoying themselves. Oh, and it sells fun shirts too.

article_title: The Best Dive Bars in Austin
name: Hole In The Wall
notes: This unpretentious dive bar and live music venue has been serving cheap drinks for over 50 years. Located on the Drag, it’s a popular spot among University of Texas students and punk and rock music fans. It features three bars, two stages (one for karaoke), and a small outdoor patio. The mix of local and touring acts and the casual atmosphere make it a favorite among music lovers and those who just love to drink.

article_title: The Best Dive Bars in Austin
name: Little Longhorn Saloon
notes: This Allandale bar has a storied reputation. On Sunday afternoons, Little Longhorn offers the classic Austin experience of Lone Star beers, live music, and a unique game called Chicken Shit bingo, where participants place bets on where a chicken will do its business. The drinks might not be fancy, but the music and dancing are always lively. While there’s tailgating outside on Sundays, bargoers mostly hang out in the cozy interiors.

article_title: The Best Dive Bars in Austin
name: The Aristocrat Lounge
notes: The bright pink walls and poodle decorations are a beacon for those seeking this Burnet Road dive bar. Formerly the Poodle Lounge, Aristocrat provides a comfortable atmosphere, affordable drinks, and a straightforward good time. It features multiple pool tables, a jukebox, a shuffleboard, and a small back patio with custom furniture crafted from old truck beds. Don’t forget to check out Yeni’s Fusion, the Indonesian food truck parked out front.

article_title: The Best Dive Bars in Austin
name: Casino El Camino
notes: With burgers, eardrum-shattering metal from the jukebox, and affordable drinks served across a sticky bar, this is pretty much a textbook Downtown dive. Located on Sixth Street, it offers a dimly lit, no-nonsense escape from the usual Downtown chaos, complete with cult-favorite bloody marys. Outdoors, a small patio is inexplicably decorated like a Mayan temple—perfect for enjoying that gigantic burger.

article_title: The Best Dive Bars in Austin
name: Lala's Little Nugget
notes: It’s always Christmas time (as in it’s permanently decorated in festive Yuletide cheer) at this Crestview bar. Lala’s, which has been around since 1972, has lived many lives and was saved from an uncertain fate after an abrupt closure in 2015. Like any good dive bar, though, it has all the essentials. There are no-nonsense drinks, a great jukebox, pool tables, and a small outdoor patio, but the action happens mostly inside. Enjoy its sister bar — Lala’s South Pole in South Austin on Davis Lane — that’s equally divey-yet-Christmas-y.

article_title: The Best Dive Bars in Austin
name: The White Horse
notes: This lively dive bar and honky-tonk in East Austin is known for its nightly live country music and spacious dance floor. The bar offers cheap whiskey and attracts a mix of old-school two-steppers and young hipsters, embodying both classic and modern Austin charm. Those looking for a break from dancing can enjoy pool tables and a relaxed outdoor patio with the Bomb Tacos food truck.

article_title: The Best Dive Bars in Austin
name: Barfly's
notes: With its budget-friendly beers and well drinks served by genuinely friendly bartenders, this North Loop bar is a win for both the wallet and spirit. There are pool tables, a loud jukebox, and floorboards that have seen better days. No notes. Don’t trip on all those stairs, though. The ones in the back leading down to the patio are even more rickety than the ones leading up to the entrance.

article_title: The Best Dive Bars in Austin
name: Carousel Lounge
notes: This Windsor Park circus-themed dive offers set-ups, beer, and wine. Open since 1963, it features an eclectic mix of live music, including rock and jazz sets, all of which are played beneath a giant pink elephant mural. It’s cash-only, like any good dive should be, but there’s no outdoor space.

article_title: The Best Dive Bars in Austin
name: The Silver Medal
notes: This Coronado Hills bar is laidback with cheap drinks, simple food (the burger is excellent), and a resident cat named Orange Cat, who isn’t allowed inside but still sneaks in. The exterior may be a bit dingy, but that’s to be expected from a dive bar located in a motel parking lot. Check out Twofer Dog Night on Thursday, or take advantage of the $12 special, which includes a chili dog, burger, and a Bud. Bonus: There are veggie options.

article_title: The Best Dive Bars in Austin
name: King Bee
notes: A true East Side staple, King Bee swings wildly between energetic nights where folks are elbow-to-elbow chaos and the quiet, intimate ones, where there’s fewer than five people inside, making for a great intimate occasion with a dear friend or your thoughts. Very rarely is there a middle ground. The drinks are cheap, the bartenders swift, and the clientele typically awesome. The Bee has had a lot of food trucks parked out back over the years. Currently, it’s the most unexpected — Little Nishi sushi.

article_title: The Best Dive Bars in Austin
name: Posse East
notes: Typically, the oldest bars end up being the diviest and Posse East is no exception. Since 1971, the bar has been slinging beer and wine — none of the hard stuff — to generations of UT students and colorful Austinites. The long-running institution has everything you could ask for, like worn-in booths, fatty burgers, and wild nights. Game days, when the place is a sea of burnt orange, are even wilder.

article_title: The Best Dive Bars in Austin
name: Frazier’s Long & Low
notes: Frazier’s has a couple of beers on draft and a small backbar of liquor. While the Pleasant Valley haunt’s only been open since 2019 — not quite old enough to have that worn-in dive bar patina — it still has the right vibe and the right prices. What regulars will tell you, though, is that the real reason to go is the burger: simple, juicy, satisfying, tasty ode to beef that’s worth much more than they’re selling it for.

article_title: The Best Breweries in the Austin Area
name: Hopsquad Brewing Co.
notes: Major League Soccer fans flock to this North Burnet for the rowdy pregame atmosphere, which sits conveniently down the road from Q2 Stadium. The core beer lineup is available to-go in cans, and among the favorites are the Zapatista Mexican lager and (especially) the Architetto Ruffini Italian pils. Pizza and other dough-based handhelds are available from the adjacent Dough Squad trailer, with to-go ordering, available plus outside seating. The brewery often opens early on Sundays for Formula 1 fans, and with a giant indoor screen, it’s the Austin viewing headquarters for fans of the Premier League team Newcastle FC. Non-beer options like margaritas and mules are also available.

article_title: The Best Breweries in the Austin Area
name: Austin Beerworks
notes: Perhaps the most successful manifestation of craft brewing in Austin, this North Burnet brewery has moved from strength to strength mostly by doing one big thing right: understanding what Central Texas beer drinkers want. While Austin Beerworks makes all sorts of styles, the core focus on flavorful warm weather beers is best manifested in the classic Pearl Snap German pils and the Peacemaker ale, which is so crushable that it is humorously available in 99-can packs. At the taproom, there’s a workhorse food truck (Can-Tina) serving burgers, nachos, salads, and tacos, a number of experimental small batch taps, and a large seating area that remains packed most weekends. The brewery also expanded recently with a second location in Northwest Austin, which has a kid’s playground, wine and cider options, and even a small dog park. Both locations have online ordering.

article_title: The Best Breweries in the Austin Area
name: The Brewtorium
notes: The Skyview brewpub succeeds by blending the old and the new. There’s a focus on traditional European beers like the schwarzbier (a dark lager) and the Czech pilsner, but that doesn’t mean you can’t order a hazy IPA or a barrel-aged sour instead. There’s also a welcoming beer hall setup, plus a small outdoor area. Expect Euro-pub fare like pretzels, bratwurst, and German-style pizza, paired with more American comforts like mac and cheese and smash burgers (all available to-go). Austin FC games are shown on the big screen here, and there is even axe throwing available.

article_title: The Best Breweries in the Austin Area
name: Pinthouse Pizza
notes: The brewery/pizzeria mini-chain has become a mainstay of both family dinners and beer nerds by offering a convivial, communal atmosphere paired with a comfort food menu of pizza, nachos, soft pretzels, and (critically) award-winning beers. The multi-location brewery is best known for the ubiquitous Electric Jellyfish IPA, a hazy New England-style beer that’s served everywhere in Austin. A wide assortment of other stellar IPAs come and go seasonally from the tap wall, and the Magical Pils and Bearded Seal stout are similarly best in class.

article_title: The Best Breweries in the Austin Area
name: Batch Craft Beer & Kolaches
notes: The Mueller brewery/bakery/restaurant has proven to be wildly popular with both the neighborhood set and beer aficionados. While the kolache bakery is the understandable draw for some, there’s a very eclectic lineup of beers ranging from “PB&J kolache-inspired” fruit beer to a New Zealand black IPA. A wide variety is the point: the brewery prides itself on experimentation, with a mix of clean and wild beers to satisfy disparate palates. There’s also coffee here, an outdoor play area for kids, offerings from food trucks (currently Parish Barbecue and Bummer Burritos), and, of course, the beloved sweet, savory, and breakfast kolaches (which do run out as the day progresses). The indoor area also has a small stage for live music.

article_title: The Best Breweries in the Austin Area
name: Oddwood Brewing
notes: While the VHS tapes and indie rock soundtrack lend the MLK brewery a laid-back, retro vibe, Oddwood’s beers are smart and serious. The brewers clearly enjoy offering some sours, but most weeks, the tap list is heavy on IPAs and hoppier lagers. An extensive range of pizzas, salads, and stromboli help patrons soak in all of those beers, as does the stellar Egyptian barbecue food truck KG BBQ. For non-beer-drinkers, there’s a reasonable wine list here, too. For the kids (or for you), there is a free-play arcade room here with NBA Jam, Ms. Pac-Man, and a small but mighty outdoor play area. Check the website for events like live comedy and even a horror movie night.

article_title: The Best Breweries in the Austin Area
name: Central District Brewing
notes: For those attending Convention Center events or en route to a concert at Stubb’s, this Red River venue in downtown Austin is a simple, pleasant spot for a craft beer. Beers are cleverly named after song titles: on a recent visit, there were references to the Incubus and Florence & The Machine on the menu. The menu is helpfully designed in easy-to-parse sections like light, hoppy, sour, and dark, and the quality is really quite a bit better than a conference-goer might expect from an unassuming stop. Try the Texas Craft Brewers Guild’s award-winning Kayaker’s kolsch to start. Most seating is indoors, but there are a couple of tables outside.

article_title: The Best Breweries in the Austin Area
name: Zilker Brewing Co.
notes: The East Sixth brewery has become an East Austin staple by producing crowd-pleasing beers — mostly in lighter styles — and canning quite a few for backyard and picnic consumption. While the interior of the taproom is fairly small due to the central location, there’s a generous patio to perch on, complete with fried chicken (and tempeh) sandwiches and tots from the on-site truck Spicy Boys. The staff is friendly, and the brand’s light and refreshing style will appeal to a variety of tastes. Try the Parks and Rec pale and the Icy Boys, an outstanding rice lager collaboration with Spicy Boys.

article_title: The Best Breweries in the Austin Area
name: Lazarus Brewing
notes: Pilsners and lagers dominate the list here, showing an appreciation for both Austin’s weather and the art of doing the basics right. There’s also a solid coffee program and a menu of tacos and tortas, making the space a vital all-day hang destination. Lazarus opened a second location on Airport Boulevard with 15 taps, wine and coffee options, a menu focusing on Mexican food, and a pirate ship play area for kids. Both locations offer online ordering and indoor and outdoor areas, although the East 6th Street location is decidedly more suave.
