article_title: The Best Breweries in the Austin Area
name: The ABGB
notes: The Galindo brewery has a lot of accolades, including winning multiple Great American Beer Festival golds (including the best large brewpub of the year), all done by keeping a laser focus on the oft-underrated pilsner and helles styles. The whole concept fits South Austin perfectly: a large beer garden, a nice indoor stage for live local music from notable names several nights a week, and a killer lineup of flavorful but crushable lighter beers. A menu of casual bar food, including creative pizzas, sandwiches, and wings is also available, and there are other cocktails, too. Perhaps the quintessential Austin beer garden, and great for groups. There are keg orders available.

article_title: The Best Breweries in the Austin Area
name: Jester King Brewery
notes: The Hill Country brewery’s evolution from a beer nerd paradise to an all-around gathering spot appears to be complete. Once solely focused on farmhouse and wild beers, the brewery now offers several “clean” beer selections in the lager and pilsner categories, alongside curated picks of wines and ciders. The kitchen side still has the usual selection of pizzas and baked goods, and the weekend-only food truck offers bar fare like burgers and fries. There are also walking trails and quite a few goats and other animals on the property. Takeout orders can be placed online. [Disclosure: Eater contributor Tom Thornton is an investor in Jester King.]

article_title: The Best Breweries in the Austin Area
name: Live Oak Brewing Company
notes: What can you say about Austin’s oldest brewery, except that it’s a classic and the city is lucky to have it? Now almost 30 years old, the Del Valle brewery’s old-guard approach has always been to make German beer styles properly and priced reasonably. As such, both the Pilz and hefeweizen are among the best in the country. There’s a small German food truck with pretzels and sausages, a disc golf course should you be interested, and UT Longhorn football games are shown in the taproom.

article_title: The Best Breweries in the Austin Area
name: St. Elmo Brewing Company
notes: While best known for its crisp Carl Kolsch, the East Congress brewery brews a wide array of styles with range: there are solid IPAs, a Japanese rice lager, and even a fruity sour optionFood from Spicy Boys is available, and there are coolers of tallboy cans to go. There is also a second location with a large play area for kids, and both locations have indoor and outdoor seating.

article_title: The Best Breweries in the Austin Area
name: Meanwhile Brewing
notes: The brewery’s McKinney neighborhood aesthetic — industrial, lots of warehouses — gives drinkers no idea of what’s around the corner with its massive space. Upon arrival, there’s a fancy indoor taproom, a large stage, a soccer field, and perhaps the largest brewery patio area in the Austin city limits. The food trucks are outstanding, with tacos from Pueblo Viejo, barbecue from the award-winning Distant Relatives, pizza from Side Eye Pie, and ice cream from Besame. As for the beer, the two signature offerings are the German-style Pilsner and the Secret Beach San Diego IPA, but with over 20 beers on tap, there is an array of choices. This is a great place for group outings or a brewery visit with kids, although finding a table on weekends can be challenging. A variety of non-beer options (including frozens) are also available at the bar.

article_title: The Best Breweries in the Austin Area
name: Fast Friends Beer Company
notes: Fast Friends has great beer and spacious indoor and outdoor areas with kid-friendly games. There’s a wide array of beer styles, like a hoppy East Coast IPL, a dark amber ale, and an easy drinking Czech pilsner. Wine, cider, seltzers, and nonalcoholic beverage options are also available. The high-ceiling space has a large patio and an open kitchen focused on pizzas (including vegan options), sandwiches on crusty bread, fresh salads, and roasted vegetables.

article_title: The Best Breweries in the Austin Area
name: Vista Brewing
notes: Much like Jester King, Vista Brewing is best viewed as a destination brewery: the drive out to the 20 acre property in Driftwood is part of the fun. Don’t skip the food, which makes use of local ranchers and an on-site farm for dishes like pickled veggies, kids fare, and juicy burgers, which are just $15 with a beer on Thursdays. The taproom keeps a rotating selection of around 15 taps flowing, with standouts like the Adair kolsch and Driftwood pale leading the way. There are barrel-aged beers to sample, too, and several seasonal beer selections. Live music is also featured most Fridays and Sundays. The sprawling outdoors is a great dose of Texas Hill Country, but there are indoor areas for when the weather doesn’t cooperate.

article_title: The Best Breweries in the Austin Area
name: Blue Owl Brewing
notes: If you’re into sour beer, Blue Owl is the place you need to be. The brewery specializes in sour and tart-leaning beers poured right in the heart of East Cesar Chavez’s hustle and bustle. Grab a flight if you want to try a wide range of styles; seasonal releases are plentiful, and the Sour Cranberry Ale w/ Ginger & Oats is a fall and winter favorite. There is pizza from Bola along with small bites like cheese boards from the on-site kitchen.

article_title: The Best Breweries in the Austin Area
name: Central Machine Works
notes: Central Machine Works is a massive beer hall converted from a machine shop on the East Side, with tons of space both indoors and out. The brewery leans into pilsners, lagers, ales, and IPAs, plus cocktails like a paloma on draft, and a menu of pizzas, sandwiches, nachos, and pretzels. The huge lawn is a hit with families, and the busy events calendar means you’re likely to catch live music or a festival on any given weekend.

article_title: The Best Breweries in the Austin Area
name: Hopsquad Brewing Co.
notes: Hopsquad sits just down the road from Q2 Stadium, making it an easy stop before or after an Austin FC match. The taproom pours a rotating lineup of IPAs, Mexican lagers, pilsners, and seasonal beers, plus the signature boozy Verde frozen lemonade — an ode to the FC. There’s also a tight slate of cocktails like margaritas, espresso martinis, and even THC options. Out back, Dough Squad turns out Neapolitan-inspired pies, empanadas, and corn dogs, while Botanita Tres Reyes sells Mexican food.

article_title: The Best Splurge-Worthy Austin Restaurants
name: Tsuke Edomae
address: 4600 Mueller Blvd Apt 1035, Austin, TX, 78723, US
notes: One of the city’s hottest omakases that is highly difficult to get to tickets for is this Mueller Japanese restaurant led by chef Michael Che. If you’re one of the lucky ones, you’ll experience a thoughtful 21-course sushi journey for $135 with such top-notch quality ingredients. Book reservations online; there are indoor dine-in services.

article_title: The Best Splurge-Worthy Austin Restaurants
name: Intero
address: 2612 East Cesar Chavez Street, Austin, Texas 78702
notes: The Holly restaurant is ever-so-considerate about its comforting Italian food, from hearty large meats like wagyu beef shoulder or full pasta plates such as the ricotta gnocchi with duck confit. Everything is thoughtfully sourced and prepared. Book reservations online; there are indoor and outdoor dine-in services.

article_title: The Best Splurge-Worthy Austin Restaurants
name: Este
notes: Seafood lovers can rejoice in the bold and playful menu at the Blackland Mexican restaurant. Chef Fermín Núñez crafts bold, fantastic, flavorful, and fresh dishes, such as the camarones “el ricas,” which are messy fun in the best way, and a decadent seafood tower featuring clams preparados and morita-drawn butter lobsters. Book reservations online; there are indoor and outdoor dine-in services.

article_title: The Best Splurge-Worthy Austin Restaurants
name: Canje
notes: A hit from the Emmer & Rye team, this vibrant East Austin location celebrates Caribbean flavors with standout dishes like wild boar pepperpot and Wagyu beef suya. The cocktail menu is just as transportive, featuring drinks like the Frozen Guava Colada and Mango Palmer. While there’s no formal tasting menu, the rotating specials and “suppa” mains make each visit feel curated. Book reservations online; there are indoor and outdoor dining areas.

article_title: The Best Splurge-Worthy Austin Restaurants
name: Lutie’s
address: 4100 Red River St, Austin, TX, 78751, US
notes: Reservations are essential at this tiny garden-forward restaurant at the dreamy Commodore Perry Estate. The New American restaurant from lauded Austin chef couple Bradley Nicholson and Susana Querejazu, has familiar regional dishes like Texas beef with marrow and sweet potato, while the chickpea and green corn salad and kouign-amann ice cream remain signature items. Book reservations online; there are indoor and outdoor dining areas.

article_title: The Best Splurge-Worthy Austin Restaurants
name: Fabrik
address: 1701 East Martin Luther King Jr Boulevard, Unit 102, Austin, TX 78702
notes: Cuisine inspired by the seasons is the calling card for this vegan fine dining spot situated in the Blackland neighborhood. Through seven courses, chef and owner Je Wallerstein executes incredibly thoughtful dishes in an effort to “showcase the diversity of plant-based cuisine.” The house-made garlic “butter” is considered a standout. As far as tasting menus are concerned, it’s at a bit more comfortable $105 per person. Book reservations online; there are indoor dine-in services.

article_title: The Best Splurge-Worthy Austin Restaurants
name: Fish Shop
address: 1401 East 6th Street, #201, Austin, Texas 78702, United States
notes: This East Austin restaurant is a neighborhood go-to for seafood lovers. Launched by the duo behind burger pop-up Le Beef, chef Justin Huffman (an alum of Uchi and French brasserie Justine’s) and his wife Nicole Rossi bring raw bar finesse to the table with oysters, conservas, and crudos, alongside classic delicacies like Dungeness crab and the couple’s wagyu quarter-pounder, the Le Beef Burger. The cozy, coastal-inspired space pairs seafood towers with natural wines and draught Guinness, making it a splurge-worthy destination that feels both elevated and approachable. Book reservations online; indoor seating only.

article_title: The Best Splurge-Worthy Austin Restaurants
name: Toshokan
notes: The Japanese restaurant is hidden within East Austin’s Holey Moley venue, making it one of the most fun and memorable higher-end omakase experiences around. Led by chef Saine Wong, the $160 14-course meal showcases his global culinary experiences in delicious dishes. Book reservations online; there are indoor dine-in services.

article_title: The Best Splurge-Worthy Austin Restaurants
name: Barley Swine
address: 6555 Burnet Rd Ste 400, Austin, TX, 78757, US
notes: This Michelin-starred Brentwood restaurant, one of Austinite chef Bryce Gilmore’s three, is where the team serves experimental New Texan dishes with a seasonal multi-course tasting menu for $115 that includes bites like pig face carnitas and sunflower and miso layer cake. The rotating menu is like a love letter to Texas soil and season, and nods to the establishment’s sustainability practices, which include sourcing ingredients from Gilmore’s River Field Farm, adding depth to the experience. Book reservations online; there are indoor and outdoor dining areas.

article_title: The Best Splurge-Worthy Austin Restaurants
name: Craft Omakase
address: 4400 North Lamar Boulevard, Suite 102, Austin, Texas 78756
notes: Within a year of opening, the Rosedale omakase restaurant — launched by Uchiko alum Charlie Wang and Nguyen Nguyen — earned a Michelin star, quickly becoming one of the city’s finest Japanese tasting menu destinations. The $175 22-course meal centers on minimalistic presentation, pristine nigiri, small plates, and excellent hospitality. Book reservations online for indoor dine-in services.

article_title: The Best Splurge-Worthy Austin Restaurants
name: Garrison
notes: Though the windowless ambiance at this restaurant within the downtown Fairmont hotel may not be to everyone’s taste, the kitchen is doing everything right. The menu rotates frequently and ranges from super-fancy tater tots with black truffle and aerated gruyere to caviar with brioche to large steaks. Expect five-star fine dining service as well. Book reservations online; there are indoor dine-in services.

article_title: The Best Splurge-Worthy Austin Restaurants
name: Olamaie
notes: Executive chef Michael Fojtase’s Olamaie is another Austin favorite to claim a Michelin star. The modern downtown restaurant reimagines Southern classics – smoked cabbage with an Alabama barbecue sauce, blackened dayboat fish served with a blue-crab sauce, Carolina Gold rice, and chives, and grilled pork chops. Chef de cuisine Amanda Turner’s weekly six-course dinners are still a must-book for $150. Book reservations online; there are indoor and outdoor dining areas.

article_title: The Best Splurge-Worthy Austin Restaurants
name: III Forks
notes: III Forks is a lavish Lavaca Street marble-and-mahogany-trimmed downtown steakhouse that leans into classic luxury with USDA Prime steaks, cold-water lobster, Oscar-style crab, black truffle-and honey-infused butter, handcrafted cocktails, and a wine list boasting over 500 selections. While there’s no formal tasting menu, mains like the $142 Tomahawk Ribeye and $179 Bone-in Wagyu Ribeye make it a destination for indulgent dining. Happy hour runs daily from 5 to 7 p.m.. Book reservations online; indoor dining only.

article_title: The Best Splurge-Worthy Austin Restaurants
name: Hestia
notes: Named after the Greek goddess of the hearth, this Michelin-starred downtown restaurant — which comes from the much-lauded team behind Emmer & Rye — centers on a live-fire menu featuring dishes like dry-aged wagyu rib-eye steaks, oyster mushrooms, and chicken with peach lacquer and pickled muscadine grapes. Desserts come from Tavel Bristol-Joseph. The restaurant’s ongoing commitment to elevated experiences, including the recently added Champagne Philipponnat Wine dinner, continue to wow. There’s also a tasting menu available for $195 with wine pairings for an additional $150. Book reservations online; there are indoor and outdoor dining areas.

article_title: The Best Splurge-Worthy Austin Restaurants
name: Otoko
address: 1603 S Congress Ave, Austin, TX, 78704, US
notes: Still one of Austin’s most exclusive dinners, Kyoto-meets-Tokyo in this multi-course journey led by chefs Yoshi Okai and Domonic Candy. The 12-seat Japanese restaurant, located at the South Congress Hotel in the Travis Heights neighborhood, offers two options: sushi omakase for $250 per person and classic omakase for $200 per person, making it a bucket-list experience. Watertrade, an intimate Japanese-style cocktail bar is attached, though guests cannot book a bar experience for the same day they dine in Otoko. Book reservations online; there are indoor dine-in services.

article_title: The Best Splurge-Worthy Austin Restaurants
name: Jeffrey’s
address: 1204 W Lynn St, Austin, TX, 78703, US
notes: This Clarksville fine-dining institution was reimagined by the entrepreneurs at McGuire Moorman Hospitality in 2013 (now McGuire Moorman Lambert), transforming it into an ideal venue for special occasions, with wine dinners hosted throughout the year and the option for private dining in the Napoleon Room and The Apartment. The menu changes often but consistently spotlights a legendary martini cart, dry-aged steaks, and caviar service. Book reservations online; there are indoor dine-in services.

article_title: The Best Splurge-Worthy Austin Restaurants
name: Uchi Austin
address: 801 S Lamar Blvd, Austin, TX, 78704, US
notes: Still iconic. The Zilker restaurant’s modern take on Japanese cuisine has rightly earned its national acclaim. While you can’t go wrong with the exquisite à la carte menu, choose the adventurous market-priced omakase menu to truly experience the chef’s expertise with fresh cuts of fish, inventive flavor pairings, and desserts that surprise. Book reservations online; there are indoor and outdoor dining areas.

article_title: The Best Splurge-Worthy Austin Restaurants
name: El Naranjo
notes: Helmed by James Beard Award-winning chef Iliana de la Vega, this South Lamar Mexican restaurant serves up refined Oaxacan cuisine that’s soulful and sophisticated. Duck mole negro and tlayuda oaxaqueña are signature dishes; most of the menu is gluten-free. Book reservations online; there are indoor and outdoor dining areas.

article_title: Where to Eat and Drink Around the University of Texas at Austin
name: Musashino Sushi Dokoro
address: 2905 San Gabriel St #200, Austin, TX 78705, USA
notes: Plenty of Austin’s best sushi chefs have cut their teeth at this longtime Japanese staple, which relocated just to the north of West Campus in 2016. It still offers some of the city’s best nigiri. There are indoor and outdoor dine-in services.

article_title: Where to Eat and Drink Around the University of Texas at Austin
name: Goodall’s
address: 1900 Rio Grande St, Austin, TX 78705, USA
notes: Located at the historic northern downtown Hotel Ella, which was built in 1900 and renovated in 2013, Goodall’s is the ultimate brunch or dinner spot with its elevated Southern fare and classy cocktails – ideal when the parents are in town. There are indoor and outdoor dine-in services.

article_title: Where to Eat and Drink Around the University of Texas at Austin
name: China’s Family Restaurant
address: 1914 Guadalupe St suite b, Austin, TX 78705, USA
notes: On the Drag, China’s Family Restaurant offers some of Austin’s best Sichuan cuisine, with a menu that offers bold, traditional dishes. Standouts include mapo tofu, dan dan noodles, yuxiang shredded pork, and spicy eggplant with garlic sauce. The no-frills space keeps the focus squarely on the food.

article_title: Where to Eat and Drink Around the University of Texas at Austin
name: Clay Pit Contemprary Indian Cuisine
notes: Housed in a rumored-to-be-haunted building, this warm but upscale Indian restaurant offers both staples (like tikka masala, vindaloo, and goat curry) and new classics: Seek out the curried mussels, tandoori salmon, and rack of lamb infused with Kashmiri spices. At lunch, lighter curry bowls offer a quicker, more casual option.

article_title: Where to Eat and Drink Around the University of Texas at Austin
name: Dirty Martin’s Place
notes: Generations of students have gathered at Dirty Martin’s Place, which has served the Drag since 1926 and still boasts a no-fills, retro interior that preserves the joint’s history. The menu features classic burgers and hearty sandwiches complemented by hand-spun milkshakes.

article_title: Where to Eat and Drink Around the University of Texas at Austin
name: El Patio Restaurant
notes: This family-owned North Campus Mexican A North Campus staple since 1954, El Patio Restaurant serves a consistent Tex-Mex menu built on tradition. The no-frills dining room keeps the focus on hearty, satisfying meals — enchiladas and chalupas are the highlight — a formula that has brought diners back for generations.

article_title: Where to Eat and Drink Around the University of Texas at Austin
name: Texas Chili Parlor
notes: Diners at Texas Chili Parlor can choose from a wide array of chili options — some with pork, some with and without beans — fueling the city’s long-running debate whether or not beans belong in chili. The restaurant has been a staple since 1976, predating the time when chili was officially the state dish; a mention in a Guy Clark song has only emphasized its enduring charm. Diners can customize their bowls to the spice level of their choosing, and the chili-smothered enchiladas remain a standout.

article_title: Where to Eat and Drink Around the University of Texas at Austin
name: Tweedy’s Bar
notes: The spirit of the old Spider House lives on in Tweedy’s Bar. Vintage decor and rock ’n’ roll energy shape the space, created by the team behind Hotel Vegas and Yellow Jacket Social Club. Cocktails, like classic daiquiris, margaritas, and bloody marys, as well as beer and wine, anchor the menu, while nearby food trucks Li’l Nonna’s, Yalla Burger, Zucchini Kill, and Guppy’s offer sustenance.

article_title: Where to Eat and Drink Around the University of Texas at Austin
name: Via 313
notes: Detroit-style pizza finds a home at Via 313 in North Campus. The square pies with thick, crisp crusts come loaded with toppings, like the Detroiter with double pepperoni, while the Caesar salad offers a lighter but equally satisfying option. The laid-back atmosphere makes it a dependable stop for groups of friends.

article_title: Where to Eat and Drink Around the University of Texas at Austin
name: Black’s Barbecue Austin
notes: At Black’s, barbecue lovers can get a taste of Lockhart without leaving Austin. Brisket, sausage, and ribs are smoked to perfection and served alongside classic, hearty sides that complement the meats. This location brings legendary Lockhart meats to a convenient spot near campus, making it a go-to for both students and locals. First-time visitors must try the standout sausage.

article_title: Where to Eat and Drink Around the University of Texas at Austin
name: Hopfields
notes: French-inspired fare and an impressive drink list draw crowds to Hopfields. The front bar feels relaxed and communal, while the dining room offers a more sophisticated spot to enjoy refined European plates. The signature burger and the mussels and frites are favorites, but the escargot dumplings are a must-order. Ditto the fries.

article_title: Where to Eat and Drink Around the University of Texas at Austin
name: Honey Moon Spirit Lounge
notes: The elegant interiors at Honey Moon Spirit Lounge set a romantic tone for a night out in the Heritage neighborhood. The menu leans New American, with dishes like steak frites and seasonal desserts. A lively cocktail program makes it a strong contender for a celebratory dinner or date night, with drinks like the Yuzu Sexy (vodka, ginger, yuzu, lemon, lavender, and herb salt).

article_title: Where to Eat and Drink Around the University of Texas at Austin
name: FoodHeads
notes: At FoodHeads, sandwiches are the star, whether you order from the wide-ranging menu — the Lami, with grilled salami and mozzarella on toasted multigrain bread with spinach, tomato, and balsamic reduction, is a highlight — or build your own. Colorful decor and a sunny patio make it a welcoming choice for breakfast or lunch.

article_title: Where to Eat and Drink Around the University of Texas at Austin
name: The Carillon
notes: Tucked inside the AT&T Hotel and Conference Center, the Carillon serves one of Austin’s most underrated dining experiences. The seasonal menu features dishes like Gulf seafood crudo, compressed watermelon and ricotta salads, and lamb chops with smoked pistachio butter, blending inventive techniques with familiar flavors. The calm, elegant dining room makes it ideal for celebrations or a special night out, but for a more workaday meal, a three-course prix fixe meal offers a curated tasting for $55 per person.

article_title: Where to Eat and Drink Around the University of Texas at Austin
name: Vaquero Taquero
notes: Dig into Rio Grande Valley tacos and quesadillas, which feature fillings like al pastor and bistec, as well as vegetarian options like nopales and rajas poblanas. The straightforward setup keeps attention on the vibrant flavors coming out of the kitchen. Bonus: The Downtown location also doubles as a music venue and cantina.

article_title: Where to Eat and Drink Around the University of Texas at Austin
name: Scholz Garten
address: 1607 San Jacinto Blvd, Austin, TX 78701, USA
notes: A beer garden dating back to 1866, Scholz Garten claims the title of Austin’s oldest restaurant. Today, it continues to serve sausages, schnitzel, and pretzels alongside cold drinks. Its spacious patio remains a festive gathering place for football games and community events.

article_title: Where to Eat and Drink Around the University of Texas at Austin
name: Crown & Anchor Pub
address: 2911 San Jacinto Blvd, Austin, TX 78705, USA
notes: Since opening in 1987, Crown & Anchor Pub has earned a loyal following of students, neighbors, and longtime regulars. Burgers and chili dogs anchor the menu, best paired with a rotating lineup of local and craft beers. There are also darts and pool tables to keep the night going.

article_title: Where to Eat and Drink Around the University of Texas at Austin
name: Posse East
notes: At Posse East, the action centers on game days, when crowds pack the patio and dining room for wall-to-wall sports coverage. Football fans can nosh on burgers, fries, and, of course, queso. Cold pitchers, strong drinks, and wallet-friendly prices fuel the lively atmosphere.

article_title: Where to Eat and Drink Around the University of Texas at Austin
name: Aster’s Ethiopian Restaurant
notes: Sop up all those wott sauces with spongy injera at this longtime Ethiopian Central Austin restaurant that’s perfect for carnivores and vegans alike. Takeout orders can be placed online; there are indoor dine-in services.

article_title: Where to Eat and Drink Around the University of Texas at Austin
name: Sour Duck Market
address: 1814 E Martin Luther King Jr Blvd, Austin, TX, 78702, US
notes: Part cafe, part beer garden, Sour Duck Market draws regulars with burgers, pastries, and brunch favorites. The patio and communal seating encourage long visits, whether for weekend gatherings or weekday lunches. Seasonal touches keep the menu fresh and engaging. Don’t skip the smoked chicken, and always take home a baked good, even if you’re stuffed.

article_title: Where to Eat and Drink Around the University of Texas at Austin
name: Salty Sow
notes: At Salty Sow, modern Southern cooking (think pork belly tacos and duck fat fries) meets a lively atmosphere, fueled by a happy hour that ranks among the best near campus.

article_title: Where to Eat and Drink Around the University of Texas at Austin
name: Este
notes: Seafood-focused Mexican cooking sets Este apart in East Austin: Ceviche and shrimp aguachile deliver bright, layered flavors, while the crispy swordfish tacos are the epitome of crunch. (Although it’s not seafood, don’t skip the mushroom quesadilla.) The bungalow-style dining room provides a stylish but relaxed backdrop. The onsite sister restaurant, Bar Toti, also offers an unreasonably good burger if seafood’s not your thing.

article_title: Where to Eat and Drink Around the University of Texas at Austin
name: Dai Due
notes: Seasonal ingredients sourced across Texas define Dai Due. Game meats, pork chops, and charcuterie boards — which can feature anything from antelope salami to Wagyu beef bologna to chicken liver mousse — show chef Jesse Griffiths’s commitment to local products. A rustic butcher shop setting paired with attentive service and Texas wines makes it a memorable dining experience.

article_title: 11 Fantastic Thai Restaurants in Austin
name: Titaya's Thai Cuisine
address: 5501 N Lamar Blvd Ste C101, Austin, TX 78751, USA
notes: North Lamar’s longtime Thai favorite delivers on bold flavors and an expansive menu. Standouts include Ta-Lay Dancing, a spicy stir-fry of shrimp, squid, green mussels, and vegetables, alongside curries and noodles. With its bustling dining room and steady following for over 15 years, Titaya’s is a cornerstone of Austin’s Thai scene.

article_title: 11 Fantastic Thai Restaurants in Austin
name: Madam Mam’s
notes: The Thai mini-chain is a favorite amongst Austinites for its super-spicy fare. The menu, created by owner Chatfuang Mam, is a reflection of her food education, culinary knowledge, and generational methods. She was once married to Sap Apisaksari, but after the divorce, she kept the restaurant, and he opened Sap’s. There are several locations throughout the greater Austin area: this one in West Campus and others in North Shoal Creek, Bee Cave, and Cherry Creek in South Austin. They all have massive menus with tasty options, including curries, soups, noodles, and Thai Coco Queso, served with wonton chips.

article_title: 11 Fantastic Thai Restaurants in Austin
name: Thai Thani
notes: This food truck on a busy Cherrywood street, serves curries, stir-fries, noodles, and standout halal chicken wings. The wings are crispy and well-seasoned, and the curries and noodles pack rich flavors. The casual setup makes it easy to grab a quick, but satisfying meal.

article_title: 11 Fantastic Thai Restaurants in Austin
name: Sway
address: 3437 Bee Caves Rd, West Lake Hills, TX 78746, USA
notes: In Westlake, the last-remaining Sway location offers a more polished take on Thai cuisine. The menu leans into creative interpretations, like spicy jungle curry and son-in-law eggs, served in a sleek dining room built for group dinners and celebrations. There’s also a great patio with views of Downtown Austin.

article_title: 11 Fantastic Thai Restaurants in Austin
name: Kiin Di
address: 2414 S Lamar Blvd Suite A, Austin, TX 78704
notes: In Zilker, Kiin Di operates out of a food truck parked at Corner Bar, serving creamy soft-shell crab curry, Thai-style fried chicken, and pad Thai. Co-owners and chefs Panyada Chaikantha, Bee TP, and Chris Matthana focus on balanced flavors that shine in every dish. The casual bar patio complements the food with a lively, street-food energy.

article_title: 11 Fantastic Thai Restaurants in Austin
name: Thai Fresh
address: 909 W Mary St, Austin, TX 78704, USA
notes: The Bouldin Creek spot from chef and owner Jam Sanitchat does triple duty as a restaurant, cafe, and bakery that focuses on Texas-sourced sustainable ingredients. The cozy South Austin spot offers traditional dishes like papaya salad and green curry fried rice. The adjacent market stocks herbs, spices, and curry pastes for home cooking. Bright, eclectic, and community-focused since 2008, Thai Fresh is a place to linger.

article_title: 11 Fantastic Thai Restaurants in Austin
name: Sap's Fine Thai Cuisine
address: 4514 West Gate Blvd, Austin, TX 78745, USA
notes: The Westgate Thai restaurant from Sap Apisaksari is a result of his divorce from Chatfuang Mam, who kept their original restaurant, Madame Mam’s. Expect curries, noodle soups, and fiery stir-fries like the spicy long beans, each prepared with attention to heat and balance. Fans keep returning to the roomy, casual spot for consistently tasty dishes and variety.

article_title: 11 Fantastic Thai Restaurants in Austin
name: Tuk Tuk Thai Cafe
notes: South Manchaca’s Tuk Tuk Thai Cafe is a family-run restaurant beloved for its homestyle cooking. The menu features curries, stir-fries, and soups, with house specials like hor mok, a steamed fish in red curry, stealing the spotlight. Warm service and a cozy, lived-in space give it the feel of a neighborhood fixture.

article_title: 11 Fantastic Thai Restaurants in Austin
name: Little Thailand
address: 4315 Caldwell Ln, Del Valle, TX 78617, USA
notes: OK, yes, this Thai restaurant is technically in Del Valle, but the trek is well worth it for some of the greater Austin area’s absolute best Thai food. Signature dishes include red curry with roast duck and pad gra prow, a fragrant stir-fry of meat and vegetables. It’s family-owned and refreshingly no-frills.

article_title: 11 Fantastic Thai Restaurants in Austin
name: P Thai’s Khao Man Gai & Noodles [North Loop]
notes: In North Loop, P Thai’s began as a small food stall before evolving into a counter service restaurant centered on khao man gai, the Thai-Chinese chicken and rice dish served with soup. Diners can choose poached, fried, or a combination of both (recommended), each paired with fragrant chicken-fat rice and crisp cucumber. Plates come with an array of sauces, including a sharp fermented bean and ginger chili sauce alongside sweet chili.
hours_match_name: P Thai’s Khao Man Gai & Noodles [North Loop]

article_title: 11 Fantastic Thai Restaurants in Austin
name: Kai Zabb Thai
address: 7221 Mcneil Drive, Austin, Texas 78729, United States
notes: Parked in a Highland food truck park, Kai Zabb Thai comes from the team behind hot chicken shop Dang Hot 89. The basil-green trailer specializes in fried chicken served over rice with sauces like fiery lime-and-chili or hearty panang curry, with choose-your-own spice levels. The chicken stays crisp and juicy, with dressings served on the side to lock in the crunch. Thai iced tea and honey-drizzled fried bananas balance the heat and round out the meal.

article_title: The Best Tex-Mex Restaurants in Austin
name: Chuy’s
address: 1728 Barton Springs Rd, Austin, TX, 78704, US
notes: Though it’s now a national chain, Chuy’s got its started at this colorful and funky restaurant on Barton Springs Road over 40 years ago. Along with the kitschy decor, expect serious Tex-Mex, tangy and creamy jalapeño dip, and some of the best fajitas in town (Chuy’s version is marinated with Texas treasure Shiner Bock beer). There are indoor and outdoor tables at this Chuy’s, which offers curbside pickup of orders, and four other locations in Austin proper with various pickup and delivery options.

article_title: The Best Tex-Mex Restaurants in Austin
name: Ramos Tex-Mex Restaurant
address: 14611 N Mopac Expy #103, Austin, TX 78728, USA
notes: This Wells Branch restaurant’s menu reads like a dictionary of Tex-Mex favorites. Chimichangas stuffed with roasted chicken, green chiles, and smothered in sauce; tender pork marinated in red chile; steak a la Mexicana; fajitas; tortilla soup; fried ice cream; and numerous combination plates. Takeout orders can be placed online, but eating inside the strip mall spot gives that hidden gem feel, with green booths, mix and match chairs, and a bar for solo eaters.

article_title: The Best Tex-Mex Restaurants in Austin
name: Eldorado Cafe
address: 3300 W Anderson Ln #303, Austin, TX 78757, USA
notes: This North Shoal Tex-Mex restaurant is full of old-school tastes and vibes thanks to its team and the owner, Joel Fried, who happens to be a former Tacodeli chef. There are cheesy enchiladas, piquant short ribs, and a spicy, flavorful array of salsas. The dining room features multi-colored walls, Home Depot tile floors, and a colorful stained glass feature over the open kitchen. When the weather is nice, the restaurant sets up a few outdoor tables too. To-go orders are available online.

article_title: The Best Tex-Mex Restaurants in Austin
name: Enchiladas y Mas
address: 1911 W Anderson Ln, Austin, TX 78757, USA
notes: There is one reason to go to this Crestview Tex-Mex restaurant, and it’s in the name: stunning enchiladas of all types, including enchiladas rancheras; enchiladas con queso; and beef, chicken, or vegetarian enchiladas. An order of chile con queso is never out of place on the table; the margaritas, served in a no-frills dining room, will knock you clear into next week. It can get super crowded in this spot for dinner so for those seeking a quick meal should stop by at lunch.

article_title: The Best Tex-Mex Restaurants in Austin
name: Vivo
address: 6406 N Interstate Hwy 35 Ste 2343, Austin, TX 78752, USA
notes: The Linc Tex-Mex restaurant is steeped in San Antonio traditions, which means puffy tacos (beef picadillo, shredded chicken, shrimp, and beans and vegetables are among the filling options) alongside enchiladas and fajita tacos on the menu. However, Vivo is mostly known for its colorful, creative margaritas, including options with watermelon and cucumber. The dining room nods to the colorful culture of coastal Mexico, and the boisterous patio is always a good time. If for some wild reason you don’t want to stay, you can order it to-go or for delivery online.

article_title: The Best Tex-Mex Restaurants in Austin
name: La Mancha Tex Mex Tavern
notes: This casual Tex-Mex tavern in Rosedale offers dependable fare like queso, enchiladas, sopapillas, and more in a colorful setting. La Mancha is best known for its powerful margaritas, available in many fruity iterations like mango or sangria. The walls of the indoor dining room are covered with kitschy movie posters and light-up letters, and outside, there are several picnic tables for outdoor dining. To-go ordering is available for guests in a pinch or allergic to maximalist decor.

article_title: The Best Tex-Mex Restaurants in Austin
name: Amaya's Taco Village
address: 5804 N Interstate 35 Frontage Rd, Austin, TX 78751, USA
notes: The Tex-Mex restaurant with two locations (this one in North Loop and the other down in Southpark Meadows) knows tacos, serving sturdy tortillas filled with eggs, fish, chicken, beef, or even rib meat. The dining room is a great place for a party, with twinkle lights and vibrant decor alongside tables that easily push together. So is the little outdoor patio, for that matter. And it does offer takeout online.

article_title: The Best Tex-Mex Restaurants in Austin
name: La Cocina de Consuelo
notes: Consuela’s dream was to open a kitchen, and at the tender age of 63, she did in a converted house. Come to the Rosedale restaurant now run by her niece Letty Garcia for the amazing breakfast burrito bursting with whatever you want. The potato enchiladas with chile chipotle sauce are also great — orders for anything on the menu can be placed online. The vibe is very DIY, reflecting crafty projects like painted tables and the sculptural use of kitchen utensils as wall decor.

article_title: The Best Tex-Mex Restaurants in Austin
name: Mi Madre's
notes: The Cherrywood restaurant is a pillar for Tex-Mex in the neighborhood, offering massive burritos, sizzling chicken or steak fajitas, and perfect crispy tacos with picadillo. If that sounds good, any and all of it can also be ordered online. The cantina decor inside and on the multiple colorful outdoor patios, however, is half the reason to come here.

article_title: The Best Tex-Mex Restaurants in Austin
name: Maudie's Cafe
notes: The Austin chain reigns supreme for all things Tex-Mex in the city. The chile con queso is a solid pick, as well as the molten cheese-layered nachos, tender fajitas, and carne guisada tacos. Fair warning: The curvy walls in here might make you feel a little funny if you have too many margaritas. Let those flashing lights on the walls mesmerize, and then grab a breath of fresh air on the patio. Takeout orders can be placed online.

article_title: The Best Tex-Mex Restaurants in Austin
name: Tamale House East
notes: The East Austin restaurant is part of Austin’s folkloric Tex-Mex history for good reason. The family dishes out steadfast migas, enchiladas, tacos, and its namesake tamales (regulars can easily get it all to go online, too), as they have been for over 60 years. Raise a toast to the portrait of Frida Kahlo that hangs over the bar and enjoy the vintage decor from any of the restaurant’s haphazard tables or patio seats.

article_title: The Best Tex-Mex Restaurants in Austin
name: Joe's Bakery & Coffee Shop
notes: If a condo displaces Joe’s, the Austin we know will end. Although Joe’s may seem unassuming, the daytime Holly Tex-Mex institution has racked up recognition from the James Beard Awards and Michelin. Joe’s typically incurs waits and lines, with fancy folks jammed up against those catching a quick meal between construction gigs. The dishes — breakfast pork chop, barbacoa plates, enchilada combos, and migas among them — are huge, affordable, and arrive quickly. Takeout orders can be placed in person or over the phone only — who needs the internet.

article_title: The Best Tex-Mex Restaurants in Austin
name: Matt's El Rancho
address: 2613 S Lamar Blvd, Austin, TX 78704, USA
notes: The appeal of this historic South Lamar restaurant lies in its menu of Tex-Mex specialties and the massive patio centered around a fountain. Its size makes it a good spot for groups and family outings—there’s room for 500 people. Though the tostadas compuestas and the beef enchilada are stellar, everyone’s really there for the Bob Armstrong dip and a Mexican martini. Order online if you must, but the vibe is half the fun of Matt’s.

article_title: The Best Tex-Mex Restaurants in Austin
name: Habanero Mexican Cafe
address: 501 W Oltorf St, Austin, TX 78704, USA
notes: This little daytime Dawson Tex-Mex restaurant has all the staples (fajitas, quesadillas, enchiladas), but the specialties are truly boss. The El Patron huevos rancheros come with two fried eggs smothered in ranchero sauce, all next to a pile of fajitas and steaming refried beans. This place is a classic casita-style restaurant, with bright red chairs, ornamental iron works, and a line to pay at the fron,t complete with impulsive little treats.

article_title: Rock Out at These Austin Live Music Bars With Drinks and Food
name: Sahara Lounge
notes: This place likely has the liveliest buffet you’ll ever experience. Sahara Lounge’s Africa night on Saturdays is an absolute must to experience Afrobeat dance tunes and a buffet lineup typically featuring spiced meatballs and chicken, bean stew, vegetable rice, and tortilla chips, starting at 7 p.m. Zoumountchi and the Sahara All-stars take the stage a half hour later, and the hearty meal from the buffet will fuel your dancing well into the evening. There’s a patio outside for alfresco dining, but with the stage inside, the lounge comes alive indoors under its rainbow string lights.

article_title: Rock Out at These Austin Live Music Bars With Drinks and Food
name: Cheer Up Charlies
notes: Under the parachute tent or inside the bar, an evening spent at Cheer Up will be unforgettable — and no night here is the same. Expect the unexpected with DJ sets by drag queens, middle school-inspired dance parties, and musical acts covering every kind of sound. Asador, the taco truck out front, reliably serves both meat and veggie tacos with carnitas or cauliflower inside the tortillas.

article_title: Rock Out at These Austin Live Music Bars With Drinks and Food
name: The Continental Club
address: 1315 S Congress Ave, Austin, TX 78704, USA
notes: Inside one of Austin’s most legendary venues in operation since 1955, get transported back to the rockabilly era with classic rock, blues, soul, and more every night of the week. Keep it simple at this Travis Heights spot with a brew or a vodka soda. After the show, plan on walking a block north for a slice of pizza from Home Slice’s late-night takeaway window, More Home Slice.

article_title: Rock Out at These Austin Live Music Bars With Drinks and Food
name: Kick Butt Coffee
address: 5775 Airport Blvd Ste 725, Austin, TX, 78752, US
notes: What happens when caffeine and karate combine? Kick Butt Coffee. This underrated spot continues to fly under the radar as it hosts open mics, live bands, and even comedy shows with the occasional barbecue in the parking lot. If you catch a show while the coffee shop’s kitchen is open, expect the usual suspects like muffins, bagels, and sandwiches, along with pizzas and pretzels to go with an order of kick-butt coffee.

article_title: Rock Out at These Austin Live Music Bars With Drinks and Food
name: 29th Street Ballroom
address: 2906 Fruth Street, Austin, Texas 78705, United States
notes: With a light renovative touch to the beloved Ballroom, the managers behind Tweedy’s, Hotel Vegas, Kinda Tropical, Chess Club, The Little Darlin’, and Yellow Jacket Social Club joined forces for a live music bar revival. Giving our city a mid-sized stage for mid-sized touring bands to perform on, 29th Street Ballroom fills a missing middle aspect to our venues, opening the door up to more kinds of musical acts. Sharing a food truck-filled parking lot with Tweedy’s Bar next door, patrons can come for dinner and a show.

article_title: Rock Out at These Austin Live Music Bars With Drinks and Food
name: The Hole in the Wall
notes: A legendary dive bar known for being the humble beginnings of big local acts like Spoon, Black Joe Lewis, and Shakey Graves, chances are decent that a new band you see on stage in the front window of the Hole in the Wall will go on to be legends. Find a spot in the alleyway for a break from the dive bar inside and check out the backside business, Amity’s Coffee and Karaoke. While Jewboy Cantina is no more, leaving the bar without a bite to grab for patrons, the Hole in the Wall now has a space for belting karaoke yet again to satisfy that hunger to be on stage.

article_title: Rock Out at These Austin Live Music Bars With Drinks and Food
name: Coconut Club
notes: If any bar has good vibes in spades, it’s the outdoor rooftop dance party that is Coconut Club. Located two stories in the air in Downtown, under a giant neon red sign with the bar’s name, a never-ending rotation of DJs come through to deliver bouncing beats meant for dancing the night away. A vodka soda here just tastes better somehow. Downstairs, jerk chicken from its drag brunch provider, Traphouse Caribbean, tastes pretty darn good too.

article_title: Rock Out at These Austin Live Music Bars With Drinks and Food
name: Elephant Room
address: Swift Building, 315 Congress Ave., Austin, TX 78701, USA
notes: Duck into this basement jazz bar in Downtown to be transported back to the days when speakeasies were more than a trend. The Elephant Room is an effortlessly cool and cozy spot under a dress shop on Congress, with jazz bands filling the delightfully dingy space to play improvised tunes and/or classic jazz standards. Prohibition-era-inspired cocktails, like its take on a Manhattan and a fiery Old Fashioned, make the rounds across the long bar. Sip a martini to keep it classy, or enjoy a beer on tap. For a bite, wander over a block south to Burger Bar, which is open until 2 a.m.

article_title: Rock Out at These Austin Live Music Bars With Drinks and Food
name: Lamberts
notes: In addition to a menu of slow-smoked meats, local beers, and classic cocktails, this longtime Downtown barbecue eatery from McGuire Moorman Lambert Hospitality group offers live music upstairs every weekend. There is a small patio outside, and you might catch some tunes wafting over from ACL Live, but to catch live music in this club, head upstairs to its bar and lounge to see any acts coming through before or after some barbecue from downstairs.

article_title: Rock Out at These Austin Live Music Bars With Drinks and Food
name: Hotel Vegas
notes: On a strip known for bar hopping, consider staying the night for some shows at Hotel Vegas. With bars and stages inside and out, there’s a chance that if you don’t like what’s on for one stage, you’ll like what’s happening on the other. Along with Hotel Vegas’s Volstead Lounge next door, the live music bar complex acts like a mini festival with multiple acts on multiple nights from its patio to the stages inside the sister bars. Clandestino, its own street food truck parked on the patio, adds to the festive vibe in the backyard with street tacos, quesadillas, and of course, chips and queso headlining the menu for a Tex-Mex bite between belting along with the bands on stage.

article_title: Rock Out at These Austin Live Music Bars With Drinks and Food
name: Cosmic Saltillo
notes: The popular South Austin coffee bar’s Eastern outpost finds a way to fit live music into its impressive lineup of coffee, cocktails, and tacos tucked inside a warehouse that will give anyone landscaping envy. Every Thursday, a stellar band sets up shop and serenades the two-story patio bar,s. Grab a drink from the bar inside, caffeinated or not, and order a plate of queso fries to snack on from any level, inside or out, at this out-of-this-world venue.

article_title: Rock Out at These Austin Live Music Bars With Drinks and Food
name: The White Horse
notes: The White Horse is an oasis of two-stepping, cheap beer, and Bomb Tacos in a sea of overpriced cocktail “experience.,” This bar comes to the rescue for all modern-day lovers of old-school country. Live music on the stage inside can be expected every night, and on Thursdays, Fridays, and Saturdays at 7 p.m., country-curious folks can learn two steps from the bar’s in-house instructor, Beth Coffey of Dancin’ Austin. Still not convinced? Willie Nelson and Kasey Musgraves’s joint music video was filmed here.

article_title: Rock Out at These Austin Live Music Bars With Drinks and Food
name: Banger's Sausage House & Beer Garden
notes: Dine on sausages and sip on draft beers while enjoying live music in this spacious Downtown beer garden on the weekends. While there’s plenty of space inside to find out why a sausage joint is always a hot dining spot, be sure to grab a table in the yard or on its porches to see bands put the banger in Banger’s Sausage House and Beer Garden.
hours_match_name: Banger's Sausage House and Beer Garden

article_title: Rock Out at These Austin Live Music Bars With Drinks and Food
name: Güero's Taco Bar
address: 1412 S Congress Ave, Austin, TX 78704, USA
notes: Looking for tacos, margaritas, and music in the heart of South Congress? Hit up the lively patio at this Tex-Mex restaurant, place a large order for their chili con queso with Güero’s own ranchero sauce, and catch bands playing Thursday through Sunday. While the stage is out in its backyard, diners room can still hear the acts inside, albeit muffled. Those who choose to sit out on the sidewalk can catch the spontaneous live acts of street performers on South Congress. The schedules and talent levels of street performers may vary.

article_title: Rock Out at These Austin Live Music Bars With Drinks and Food
name: C-Boy's Heart & Soul
address: 2008 S Congress Ave, Austin, TX 78704, USA
notes: This funky South Austin venue is the perfect place to start or end the night with live classic soul and R&B music every day of the week. Find space inside to watch the acts that come across its indoor stage, or catch a break from dancing on its patio out back. Currently, the Weird Food halal trailer is parked outside, dishing up Mediterranean dishes like falafel and gyros.

article_title: Rock Out at These Austin Live Music Bars With Drinks and Food
name: Broken Spoke
address: 3201 S Lamar Blvd, Austin, TX 78704, USA
notes: Straight out of your favorite Western film, Broken Spoke is a real-life honky tonk bar on South Lamar, complete with a cast of local characters. Order something from the truly Tex meets Mex menu, like a quesadilla, barbecue plate, or chicken-fried steak, before gaining the courage to join the line dancing that takes over the dance floor. After an evening out at Broken Spoke, you’ll feel a touch more Texan than you did when you got there.

article_title: Rock Out at These Austin Live Music Bars With Drinks and Food
name: Radio/East
notes: The crown jewel of the Radio Coffee and Beer empire, this massive coffee bar on Montopolis Drive strikes a balance between being a cool spot to co-work during the day, inside or on the patio, and co-thrive with live music at night. While both the original Radio and the newer Radio Rosewood offer fantastic live music in relatively intimate spaces, Radio East offers an expansive open lawn for dancing in the crowd without feeling crowded, with killer acts taking the impressively sized stage. With coffee, cocktails, tacos, and its own branded food truck called Shortwave, Radio/East is broadcasting a strong signal to catch a concert and stay awhile.

article_title: Rock Out at These Austin Live Music Bars With Drinks and Food
name: Sagebrush
notes: Noticed the glitzy sign that says “Sagebrush” while stuck in traffic on South Congress heading south? Head inside this bougie meets backwater bar, where the space expands from the tiny facade with a vaulted ceiling over the dance floor into red velvet curtains framing the stage, and blue velvet walls acting as the background for the pool tables. A mesa sunset illusion illuminates the other side of the seating area. Outside, picnic tables dot the large backyard that doubles as the dining space for its rotating cast of food trucks. With country acts coming through alongside drag shows and queer line-dancing workshops, there are endless and fabulous goings-on hiding inside Sagebrush. Enjoy beer, live music, pool, and food from the on-site burger truck, Bubs.

article_title: Rock Out at These Austin Live Music Bars With Drinks and Food
name: The Far Out Lounge & Stage
address: 8504 S Congress Ave, Austin, TX 78745, USA
notes: Don’t let the tiny stone building fool you. Once inside the bar, the backyard at the Far Out Lounge opens up to a large expanse of shaded lawn and a stage for its rotating list of musical acts. While there are a few seats inside, you’ll likely want to grab a spot outside to catch the live performances. Chow down on a bite from Knuckle Sandwich while you do. With oversized sandwiches like its mushroom reuben, Knuckle Sandwich adds a picnic vibe to this spot.
hours_match_name: The Far Out Lounge and Stage

article_title: Rock Out at These Austin Live Music Bars With Drinks and Food
name: Sam’s Town Point
notes: Sam’s Town Point A bar time forgot, Sam’s Town Point is the place to go to when you’re missing the country days of Austin without someone trying to sell you on “authenticity.” Bite into a value-brand burger, sip a cheap beer, and catch a country act that will blow your boots off. With a sign inside that reads, “ Someday these days will be worth remembering,” a trip to Sam’s Town Point is assuredly going to be unforgettable.

article_title: The Best Indian Restaurants in Austin
name: Rock N Grill
address: 1702 N Mays St Suite A, Round Rock, TX 78664, USA
notes: At this Round Rock restaurant, find Southern and Northern Indian food, including goat and mutton dishes to pakoras (fritters), spicy fried fish to tandoori (grilled meats), paneer entrees, and dosas (savory thin South Asian pancakes basically). The menu covers practically every corner of cuisines found in the country, and even offers unique drinks inspired by places beyond India’s borders, like the Irani Chai, made with a black tea from Iran and mixed with boiled milk and sugars for a distinctively different and delicious taste. Expect plenty of tables should you go to dine in, or rock out and order in with Uber Eats and Doordash.

article_title: The Best Indian Restaurants in Austin
name: Suprabhat
address: 9225 W Parmer Ln Suite 108, Austin, TX 78717, USA
notes: Suprabhat in Davis Spring focuses on vegetarian dishes from across India. There are plenty of chaats, such as chopped samosas, along with idlis (thick rice cakes) topped with chutneys, and so many dosas. There’s also a section dedicated to Jain cuisines, where the dishes are made without onions and garlic. While the restaurant offers online orders to-go or for takeout with UberEats — although selecting that option means missing out on the kitschy dining space that is themed to an Indian village.

article_title: The Best Indian Restaurants in Austin
name: Bombay Street Food
notes: With food trucks in both Anderson Mill and Cedar Park, Bombay Street Food is all about Indian snacks. From the crispy bursting pani puri (crispy hallowed balls filled with chutney, spices, potatoes, and chickpeas), the flavorful ragda chaat (potato patties topped with chutneys) and vada pavs (deep-fried potato sandwiches), to the samosa chaats (chunks of samosas topped with chickpeas and chutneys), the menu goes the distance in importing over beloved street food dishes. Both locations have limited seating, with the Cedar Park truck offering covered picnic tables and Anderson Mill’s truck having a few tables as well, it is also easy enough to sample the street food without having to set foot outside through DoorDash.

article_title: The Best Indian Restaurants in Austin
name: Sangam Chettinad Indian Cuisine
address: 6001 W Parmer Ln #140, Austin, TX 78727, USA
notes: While the Milwood restaurant offers dishes from the entire country of India, its specialties lean more towards Southern Indian, especially Chettinad cuisine from the Tamil Nadu state. The breakfast menu includes egg dosas, idiyappam (rice noodles) paired with coconut milk, and potato masalas. The lunch and dinner menus are more expansive, with entrees such as mutton chops, tangy fish curries, boiled egg masalas, idlis, dosas, and halwa (a pudding-ish dessert). You can grab any mealtime dish to go or have it delivered on Uber Eats and Doordash, but consider coming into Sangam, getting a table, and sampling a few margaritas from the bar before ordering some masala.

article_title: The Best Indian Restaurants in Austin
name: New Sitara Indian Cuisine
address: 1779 Wells Branch Pkwy Suite 102, Austin, TX 78728, USA
notes: The Wells Branch restaurant serves great Indian food for lunch and dinner with an Indian buffet. New Sitara’s tempting dishes will make it difficult to avoid second and third helpings. There is so much to sample, including Goan shrimp, all sorts of tandoori proteins from paneer to chicken wings, biryanis, and rasmalai (a cheese-based sweet). While a buffet is best experienced in person, you can semi-attempt one at home by having its dishes delivered or taken to-go, but unless you have enough serving trays, why not go in person?

article_title: The Best Indian Restaurants in Austin
name: Desilicious cafe
address: 4101 W Parmer Ln Ste C, Austin, TX, 78727, US
notes: The Milwood South Indian restaurant with an incredible punny name offers snacky vegetarian and vegan dishes. The cafe-like menu spans dosas savory and sweet (yes, there’s a Nutella one), along with various breads such as paratha and idli, bajji (fried vegetables and meats), and a really great drinks menu, including Indian filter coffee and masala chai. Takeout orders can be placed in person or over the phone; there are indoor and outdoor dine-in services.

article_title: The Best Indian Restaurants in Austin
name: Bombay Express
notes: In far north Austin, this vegetarian and vegan restaurant serves up daily specials with both Northern — aloo (potato) parathas, puri bhaji puris paired with potatoes — and Southern regional fare, like many dosas. Inside, Bombay Express’s plain interior keeps the focus of each visit on the vibrant flavors coming out of the kitchen. Opt for the thalis — essentially meals — where diners can order two curries with rice, dal, snacks, and salad in their dining room or have it at home via delivery or to-go.

article_title: The Best Indian Restaurants in Austin
name: Saffron Indian & Nepalese Cuisine
address: 3616 Far West Blvd, Austin, TX 78731, USA
notes: More Indian and Nepalese specialties — plus some Indo-Chinese takes — can be found at this Northwest Hills restaurant, which continues to be a favorite amongst Austinites. Its namesake spice, saffron, shows up in the goat curry, scallops, chicken lazeez, and in the mixed tandoori grill. The rest of the menu is spared the seasoning, but it is still plenty spiced and flavorful. Grab a table inside under the impressive mural of Hindu gods or order a taste of saffron to your home to go or delivered by Uber Eats and Doordash.

article_title: The Best Indian Restaurants in Austin
name: Bombay to Kathmandu Kitchen & Curry Pizza
address: 6700 Middle Fiskville Rd Suits 406, Austin, TX 78752, USA
notes: Now in Highland, Bombay to Kathmandu serves great Indian and Nepalese dishes. Momos, kormas, and thalis, plus some fusion like french fry chaat with chutney, yogurt, and chickpeas, can all be seen served around its dining room along with the signature dish: curry pizzas. Chicken makhani, paneer and chicken tikka masala, saag paneer, and lamb vindaloo all top crispy pie crusts for a spiced-up slice of pizza. While there’s plenty of room inside, why not order a curry pizza to go or delivered by Uber Eats or Doordash to shake up your pizza delivery routine?

article_title: The Best Indian Restaurants in Austin
name: Dosa Shack
notes: This Indian vegetarian food truck in Allandale South teases gym goers at neighboring Austin Simply Fit. Dosa Shack is all about dosas, from original styles with fillings such as potatoes and onions or cottage cheese, as well as uttapam, chaat, and sandwiches like its paneer spinach melt. Beyond the dosas, diners can grab a table on the fake grass atop the parking lot asphalt and sample samosa nachos with a side of peri peri mayo or satisfy their sweet tooth with a rose caramel or triple chocolate doffles (waffle bites made with dosa batter on sticks). Skip the wait and order online, or have your dose of dosa delivered by Uber Eats, Favor and Doordash.

article_title: The Best Indian Restaurants in Austin
name: Mousam Spice House
notes: Mousam Spice House, with locations at North Campus, South Austin, and North Austin, is perfect for anyone looking for South Asian foods with a touch of Mexican style. There are burritos stuffed with paneer and chicken, tacos with paneer or chicken, and Chicken 65 that’s deep-fried, spiced and served with a house chutney. There are also classic curries, dal, momos, and samosas alongside Indo-Chinese dishes like gobi chicken Manchurian, deep-fried chicken in soy sauce, and chow mein with paneer, for a wide geographic range of flavors — all found in this food truck at a gas station. While you could snack as you fill your tank, it’s a better idea and experience to order it to go or delivered via Uber Eats, Postmates, and Doordash.

article_title: The Best Indian Restaurants in Austin
name: Electric Gravy
notes: Sisters with Chinese-American cuisine cooking neighbor Old Thousand, Electric Gravy is what would happen if a Mumbai bar suddenly found itself on East Eleventh. Before indulging in a curry like the Goan-style pork vindaloo with its spicy and tangy curry sauce, take a chip and dip it in the curry queso, made of tomato masala, tadka, and, of course, cheese, to experience the remix of expectations for flavors at a sit-down Indian restaurant. The space inside is a playful, technicolor bar with Bollywood posters and curtains made from a rainbow of different fabrics. Facing the street, grab a table on their covered patio while sipping a dirty chai espresso martini in the shade. Electric Gravy is also available to-go, or delivered by Uber Eats, Postmates, Favor, and Doordash.

article_title: The Best Indian Restaurants in Austin
name: Nasha
notes: Indian food with influences from Texas and Mexico can be found at this East Austin restaurant. Look for spicy curries, paneer quesadillas, keema (ground lamb) enchiladas, and South Asian tacos with fillings like chicken Tandoori and lamb masala. The fusion approach carries over into the margarita menu with a curried version, a cardamon one, and a pomegranate option. There is another location on South Congress and a Bee Cave location, and all have a playful and colorful design. While the South Congress spot is more of a typical outlet in a strip mall and only has seats inside the vibrant restaurant, the one on East 7th is host to both a well-sized and shaded patio, along with tables inside. Anyone uninterested in dining out can always pick up an order to-go or have it delivered by Favor.

article_title: The Best Indian Restaurants in Austin
name: Bombay Dhaba
notes: The Bouldin Creek truck offers a nice array of north and south Indian specialties, such as dosas, idlis, fantastic samosas, and entrees like paneer tikka masala and spinach lamb. Takeout orders can be placed in person; there are outdoor dine-in areas.

article_title: The Best Indian Restaurants in Austin
name: Tandoori Lounge
address: 3601 W William Cannon Dr #450, Austin, TX 78749, USA
notes: Southcross Plaza’s South Asian restaurant is centered on tandoori of all sorts, where spiced items are cooked in a special clay oven, including lamb, goat, shrimp, paneer, chicken, and pomplet — a type of fish cooked bone-in. There are also more general pan-Indian dishes like samosas, koftas, aloo gobi, curries, and some Indo-Chinese specialties cooked up and served in this bar-meets-biryani restaurant. For those looking to indulge from home, you can order to-go and have it delivered by Uber Eats and Doordash.

article_title: The Best Indian Restaurants in Austin
name: Asiana Indian Cuisine
address: 801 E William Cannon Dr, #205, Austin, TX 78745, USA
notes: This Far South Austin spot serves up some of the best not-to-be-missed South and North Indian dishes inside an unassuming strip mall parallel to I-35. From vegetable samosas, gobi manchurian (cauliflower florets cooked with soya sauce), to tandoori meats and sweet or salty lassis (a blended drink of yogurt, ice, water and spices), Asiana focuses on the vibrant flavors and hosts a simple dining space inside the restaurant, while also providing pickup via Clover and delivery by Uber Eats, Doordash, and Postmates.

article_title: The Best Indian Restaurants in Austin
name: Jaipur Palace
notes: Jaipur Palace is named after the capital of the state of Rajasthan, and serves a variety of regional dishes in Southpark Meadows. The menu highlights include tandoori gobi (cauliflower grilled in a tandoor), parathas and stuffed kulcha bread, biryanis, and a sweet and savory mango chicken dish. With a small patio with picnic tables out front and a well-appointed interior with cascade blue walls holding up unique pieces of art, Jaipur Palace brings a sense of taste to the strip mall in more ways than one. For a taste at home, order to-go, or have it delivered by Uber Eats, Doordash, or Favor.

article_title: Austin Restaurants With Great Views
name: Oasis Texas Brewing Company
notes: Locals know to go to this brewery/restaurant out on far northwest Austin for nachos, burgers, and beers, plus a spot-on view of Lake Travis. This laid-back spot is popular for its stunning sunset views and live music. The restaurant features a large patio space and an indoor area, but it closes for inclement weather.

article_title: Austin Restaurants With Great Views
name: Bulevar
notes: The Mexican restaurant is perched on a hill in the Arboretum area, so there are beautiful views of the rolling hills of West Austin from the enclosed patio and tables by the window in the dining room. The patio windows open up for an alfresco experience when it’s nice out. The gluten-free menu has charred carne asada, smoky duck enmoladas, and spicy salsas.

article_title: Austin Restaurants With Great Views
name: Ski Shores
notes: The classic joint that draws lake rats was revamped in 2021 by vibe experts McGuire Moorman Lambert Hospitality. The updated Ski Shores still offers the same lively views of boats and jet skis whizzing by from the open and breezy patio, but now there are stylish fire pits and picnic tables where the paint is fresh as opposed to peeling off. The menu also got an upgrade, with items like a $38 lobster roll, but generally, it’s still burgers and chicken tenders, plus the opportunity to commune with turtles, fish, and ducks.

article_title: Austin Restaurants With Great Views
name: Lutie’s
address: 4100 Red River St, Austin, TX, 78751, US
notes: The fancy, verdant New American restaurant within the Commodore Perry Estate is beautiful inside and out. The patio and many tables in the dining room overlook the estate’s beautifully manicured grounds. Plus, the restaurant earns high marks for its dishes, such as the fresh raw vegetables in the petit aioli or the buttery kouign amann ice cream.

article_title: Austin Restaurants With Great Views
name: County Line on the Hill
address: 6500 Bee Caves Rd, Austin, TX 78746, USA
notes: Located in a historic building that is an institution that has been around for 50 years, the West Hills Lake location of the Austin-born barbecue restaurant chain presents an incredible 20-mile view of the Texas Hill Country from its covered twinkle-lit stone patio. The barbecue is also fantastic, particularly the snappy sausage and peppery turkey breast. The indoors is more of a classic barbecue joint, which is a different kind of beautiful view.

article_title: Austin Restaurants With Great Views
name: Mozart's Coffee
address: 3825 Lake Austin Blvd, Austin, TX 78703, USA
notes: This West Austin coffee shop’s expansive tree-shaded patio is right on the lake, making it perfect for leisurely drinks and snacks. Many sober dates have taken place here over pretty cakes and Italian sodas. In addition to the water views and the trees of Red Bud Isle across the lake, Mozart’s often hosts events or live music, making for a picture-perfect outing.

article_title: Austin Restaurants With Great Views
name: Sway
address: 3437 Bee Caves Rd, West Lake Hills, TX 78746, USA
notes: Austin’s only remaining location of the upscale Thai restaurant is a rare spot with food that is as good as the view. The covered upstairs patio offers a longshot view of Downtown Austin, which is the perfect backdrop for catching up with friends over drinks and plates of shu mai, panang short rib curry, or a consistently creative preparation of whole fish. The indoor dining room features large community tables with no view, so head to the patio for more engaging conversation.

article_title: Austin Restaurants With Great Views
name: Codependent Cocktails + Coffee
address: 301 West Ave STE 110, Austin, TX 78701, USA
notes: Located at the base of the Independent Condo Tower in downtown Austin, this coffeehouse by day, drinks spot by night, boasts floor-to-ceiling windows that overlook Shoal Creek as it winds its way through the city. The verdant foliage, both inside and out, gives the space a treehouse-like effect from the interior or the patio. Food is a morning and afternoon affair, featuring snacks like tacos, avocado toast, and a charcuterie board, and drinks lean into tropical vibes, such as the Suit & Thai, which combines vodka, dragonfruit, pandan, melon, lemongrass shochu, and Champagne foam.

article_title: Austin Restaurants With Great Views
name: Arriba Abajo
notes: One of Downtown’s better rooftop restaurants, Arriba Abajo, took over the Wax Myrtle’s space at the Thompson Hotel and is serving an Oaxacan menu that’s much better than a hotel restaurant needs to be. The desert plant-laden patio space contrasts with the tall buildings surrounding it for a beautiful view. Food-wise, this is a place for tacos, like the tender carne asada or earthy mushrooms, best paired with one of the bar’s many mezcals. There are a few indoor tables that offer the rooftop view, but the patio is the best option.

article_title: Austin Restaurants With Great Views
name: Nido
address: Rooftop, 1211 W Riverside Dr, Austin, TX 78704, USA
notes: Arguably, the most picturesque views of Downtown come from across the river, at the Loren Hotel at Lady Bird Lake. The Zilker restaurant’s patio has a beautiful view of the lake, the sparkling skyscrapers of the cityscape, and the rolling hills beyond. There’s also an indoor dining room and cocktail lounge, but the best views are on the patio. Food here is inoffensive but not spectacular (brick chicken, oysters, and drinks close to $20 each), but the view is truly unmatched.

article_title: Austin Restaurants With Great Views
name: Arlo Grey
address: 111 E Cesar Chavez St, Austin, TX 78701, USA
notes: In downtown Austin, the Line Austin Hotel’s restaurant — run by Top Chef winner and now host Kristen Kish — is a prime spot for watching the city’s famous bats. There are floor-to-ceiling windows overlooking the Colorado River and the Congress Avenue Bridge. Kish’s menu includes creative smaller plates like crispy rice with crab and saffron aioli, and a great burger. The hotel’s rooftop bar, P6, offers a different perspective on Downtown views.

article_title: Austin Restaurants With Great Views
name: Jacoby's Restaurant & Mercantile
address: 3235 E Cesar Chavez St, Austin, TX 78702, USA
notes: The grassy outdoor area of the Texan restaurant in Govalle boasts a peaceful view of the Colorado River through a canopy of trees. Jacoby’s uses beef from the family’s ranch, so the steaks and burgers are popular. It also makes for an aesthetically pleasing brunch, with mimosas served in fancy carafes and French Toast made from blueberry bread. There’s an indoor dining room that offers the same upscale-rustic vibe but no views.

article_title: Austin Restaurants With Great Views
name: Mattie's
address: 811 W Live Oak St, Austin, TX 78704, USA
notes: Remodeled from a historic South Austin farmhouse, this Southern fine dining restaurant in Bouldin Creek is an oasis right between South First and South Lamar. There are views of shaded oaks and grassy knolls as well as beautiful free-roaming peacocks, including a fetching all-white fowl. Mattie’s has excellent fried chicken and classic milk punch from a generational family recipe. Sip a cocktail on the porch to take in the views, or enjoy the historical experience inside the dining room.

article_title: Where to Eat and Drink on South Congress in Austin
name: The Continental Club
address: 1315 S Congress Ave, Austin, TX 78704, USA
notes: Open since 1955, the Continental Club hosts live music nightly on the stage that Stevie Ray Vaughan, the Replacements, and Robert Plant have all played on. The no-frills dive bar is the perfect spot to grab a cheap beer and reminisce about how Austin used to be. Check the schedule, as shows may be sold out and usually require a cover or ticket to enter.

article_title: Where to Eat and Drink on South Congress in Austin
name: Equipment Room
notes: For fantastic high-end cocktails in a cool indoor space soundtracked by hip music, head straight to this stylish bar tucked inside the Hotel Magdalena. The bar offers classic cocktails like an espresso martini along with its “b-side” version, like Bottom of the River with gin, nori amaro, black sesame aperitivo, macadamia liqueur, and coffee. The bar also offers snacks like shrimp chips or a kobayashi dog. Reserve online to ensure prime seating.

article_title: Where to Eat and Drink on South Congress in Austin
name: Lucky Robot
address: 1303 S Congress Ave, Austin, TX 78704, USA
notes: Chef Jay Huang focuses on sustainable and responsible sourcing at this South Congress Japanese restaurant. It’s popular for happy hour, where diners can snack on beef hot rock and sample dry-aged maki in the cherry blossom-adorned dining room or on the outside patio. There’s also online takeout for when pants are just not happening.

article_title: Where to Eat and Drink on South Congress in Austin
name: Perla's Seafood and Oyster Bar
address: 1400 S Congress Ave Ste B100, Austin, TX 78704, USA
notes: Perla’s patio is perhaps one of the best spots for people-watching in all of Austin. The brunch is particularly strong, with a lobster frittata, crab Florentine, fancy French toast, and cute cocktails. It’s part of the McGuire Moorman Lambert family of restaurants, so expect to pay for the impeccable aesthetics.

article_title: Where to Eat and Drink on South Congress in Austin
name: Home Slice
address: 1415 S Congress Ave, Austin, TX 78704, USA
notes: Follow the fragrance of garlic to find this pizzeria dishing out New York-style pies and more. The giant, stuffed-to-the-brim Italian sub here has a dedicated fan club. If you can’t grab a table indoors or outdoors at the restaurant proper, order online or duck next door to More Home Slice for pizza to-go.

article_title: Where to Eat and Drink on South Congress in Austin
name: Otoko
address: 1603 S Congress Ave, Austin, TX, 78704, US
notes: Hidden upstairs in the South Congress Hotel, Otoko offers a moody, minimalist dining experience centered around an intimate 12-seat omakase, led by chef Yoshi Okai. The menu blends Tokyo-style sushi with Kyoto-style kaiseki, shifting nightly based on seasonality and Okai’s whims. Expect standout bites, such as toro with caviar or house-aged mackerel. Arrive early or linger after at Watertrade next door, where the cocktail bar leans heavily on Japanese spirits and low lighting.

article_title: Where to Eat and Drink on South Congress in Austin
name: Maie Day
address: 1603 S Congress Ave, Austin, TX, 78704, US
notes: At the bottom of the South Congress Hotel, Maie Day feels equal parts casual steakhouse and celebratory dinner spot, with high ceilings, eclectic art, and party vibes. Chef Michael Fojtasek’s menu is playful and shareable, featuring options like juicy New York strip, crab claws, and sides like mac and cheese or loaded fries. The cocktails lean refreshing, with standouts like the basil-infused and vodka-heavy Coastal Grooves. The interiors are loud, bright, and never too serious, while the chocolate cake is almost as large as your torso.

article_title: Where to Eat and Drink on South Congress in Austin
name: Vespaio
address: 1610 S Congress Ave, Austin, TX 78704, USA
notes: Vespaio gives the feel of an old-school Italian restaurant without being too stuffy, with lots of dark wood, a wine wall, and fresh handmade pasta, plus a couple of tables outside. The menu is classically Italian-American, with dishes like cioppino, cacio e pepe, and pizza. Though the restaurant is more romantic, order to-go for an at-home date night.

article_title: Where to Eat and Drink on South Congress in Austin
name: June's
address: 1722 S Congress Ave, Austin, TX 78704, USA
notes: Wine is the focus at this all-day cafe, with decor and a menu inspired by French sensibilities. Find dishes like French onion soup, a shaved jambon sandwich, and bone marrow bolognese. With a long marble bar, black and white tile, and outdoor tables, it’s fancy without being too fancy and a great option for a romantic dinner or catching up with friends.
