article_title: Where to Eat and Drink on South Congress in Austin
name: Magnolia Cafe
address: 1920 S Congress Ave, Austin, TX 78704, USA
notes: An Austin classic since 1979, this diner dishes out family-friendly breakfast and hangover-curing fare. The Mag Mud, a dip with black beans, queso, avocado, and pico de gallo, is a must. Though it’s no longer open 24 hours, diners have until midnight daily to grab those life-changing gingerbread pancakes in the dining room, on the patio, or to-go.

article_title: Where to Eat and Drink on South Congress in Austin
name: The Meteor
address: 2110 S Congress Ave, Austin, TX 78704, USA
notes: Anyone looking to grab a bottle of wine, a slice of pizza, and/or bike gear will find a home at the Meteor, which is an extremely casual counter-service cafe, bottle shop, and bike shop. It offers coffee, pizza, breakfast sandwiches, and grain bowls. Order online to skip the line, though bike chains aren’t available to go.

article_title: Where to Eat and Drink on South Congress in Austin
name: Toasty Badger
notes: This family-friendly, retro-by-the-way-of-the-1970s diner with East Coast vibes offers cheery murals, counter seating, and a menu full of cozy breakfast hits. Order towering pancakes, eggs all day, or get lunchy with a Monte Cristo or fried chicken sandwich. The house coffee’s strong, lending to a neighborhood hangout that feels like it’s always been there.

article_title: Where to Eat and Drink on South Congress in Austin
name: Habana Austin
address: 2728 South Congress Avenue, TRLR 2, Austin, Texas 78704, United States
notes: Tucked beneath a canopy of tropical greenery, this nearly 25-year-old restaurant blends Cuban comfort food with a casual, breezy charm. The space includes a shady patio, thatch-roof cabanas, and plenty of cold mojitos. The lechon asado and ropa vieja keep regulars coming back, along with pressed Cubanos and garlicky yuca frita. Bonus: You can even order cigars to smoke in a cabana out back. There’s also a late-night menu that’s available until midnight on the weekends.

article_title: Where to Eat and Drink on South Congress in Austin
name: Taste of Ethiopia
address: 3801 S Congress Ave #107, Austin, TX 78704, USA
notes: South Congress is home to the second location of this casual Ethiopian restaurant that’s especially popular with vegetarians. Expect hearty stews like doro wot (chicken with berbere spices) and yemisir wot (slow-cooked lentils), all served atop wonderfully spongy injera bread. The interior dining room is not the most impressive in the city, but the hospitality is some of the friendliest.

article_title: Where to Eat and Drink on South Congress in Austin
name: El Borrego de Oro
address: 3900 S Congress Ave, Austin, TX 78704, USA
notes: This casual Mexican restaurant is fairly nondescript inside and out, but the food is fantastic. Drop in for all-day breakfast, lunch, or dinner and join dedicated regulars for excellent enchiladas, crispy tacos, and caldos. The rich birria is also a must-try.

article_title: Where to Find the Greatest Sandwiches in Austin
name: Ng Cafe
address: 13000 North Interstate Highway 35 #200 Bldg 12, Austin, TX 78753, USA
notes: At Scofield Farms, Ng Cafe serves up some of the city’s best banh mi, featuring fresh baguettes baked on-site each morning that steal the show from all its other sandwiches, thanks to the crisp crust and soft center. Fillings include pâté, ham, rib-eye, and shredded chicken. Settle into the cozy, cute interiors with your satisfying sando. Online orders are welcome.

article_title: Where to Find the Greatest Sandwiches in Austin
name: Tropicana Cuban Restaurant
address: 9616 N Lamar Blvd #141, Austin, TX 78753, USA
notes: North Austin’s Tropicana dishes out pressed Cuban sandwiches that are hot and melty. The Cubano, medianoche, and tortas all start with house-baked bread that gets crisped to order. Juicy pork, smoky ham, and melted Swiss cheese combine to create a hearty, flavorful sandwich. Order a Cuban espresso to finish the meal strong at this festive strip-mall spot. It accepts online orders.

article_title: Where to Find the Greatest Sandwiches in Austin
name: Mi Tradición
notes: In Wooten, the brightly-colored Mi Tradición uses its house-made bread as the base for hefty Mexican tortas. Try the Chilanga with milanesa, ham, and chorizo, or go for the Hawaiana if pineapple is your thing. Every sandwich brings a rich mix of textures and bold flavors. There’s also a second location off William Cannon in South Austin. Grab a Mexican pastry from the case for later. Ordering online is an option here.

article_title: Where to Find the Greatest Sandwiches in Austin
name: Choo Sando
address: 5222 Burnet Rd Suite535, Austin, TX 78756, USA
notes: Choo Sando crafts Japanese sandwiches that balance a sweet and savory flavor profile with a photogenic presentation. Fluffy milk bread holds together layers of pork katsu, egg salad, or seasonal fruit and cream. The shop began as a pop-up but quickly found a loyal following that goes well beyond its Rosedale following. The sandwiches are perfect for pick-up. Some sandos are available for pre-order 24 hours ahead.

article_title: Where to Find the Greatest Sandwiches in Austin
name: New World Deli
notes: Hyde Park’s New World Deli keeps it classic with a menu of cold cuts, grilled sandwiches, soups, and burgers in a charming old-school counter-service setting. The Reuben comes with corned beef, pastrami, turkey, or turkey pastrami, so you can tailor it to your preferences. The curried chicken salad sandwich is unreasonably good. Yes, pickup orders are available online.

article_title: Where to Find the Greatest Sandwiches in Austin
name: Mum Foods
notes: A cross between a Texas barbecue joint and an East Coast deli, Windsor Park’s Mum Foods smokes its own brisket, pastrami, and turkey, giving each sandwich serious depth. Go simple with egg salad or smoked chicken salad, or go big with the Rachel, which is piled high with slaw, Swiss, and Russian dressing. This deli started at farmers’ markets and hasn’t lost its homegrown roots since moving to its own brick-and-mortar, earning a Michelin recommendation in 2024. Deliveries are offered through DoorDash, Grubhub, Seamless, or Toast Local are available.

article_title: Where to Find the Greatest Sandwiches in Austin
name: Otherside Deli
address: 1104 W 34th St Ste D, Austin, TX 78705, USA
notes: Also serving killer pastrami, Otherside Deli in Old West Austin builds standout pastrami and corned beef sandwiches that hit all the right notes. The meats are juicy and sliced thick, and served on rye or soft rolls that hold up under pressure. It’s a no-frills counter-service joint with big flavor and serious sandwich chops. The turkey sandwich is also worthy of an order (which you can place online).

article_title: Where to Find the Greatest Sandwiches in Austin
name: FoodHeads
notes: This West Campus favorite combines relaxed vibes with the precision of a sandwich shop. Pulled pork and squash-mozzarella options headline a menu that always keeps things interesting outside of the usual turkey-and-cheese options. The shady patio provides a perfect spot to linger. Warning: Lunch gets pretty busy when the UT kids are in school, and the best way to avoid that trap is to place an order online.

article_title: Where to Find the Greatest Sandwiches in Austin
name: Atlas Sandwich Company
notes: Inside the Armadillo Den in Far South Austin, Atlas Sandwich Company crafts Greek-inspired sandwiches that are packed with flavor. Lamb and chicken marinated in herbs get loaded with tzatziki, smashed potatoes, and fresh vegetables. The spicy whipped feta sauce belongs on everything. Grab a beer and enjoy it under the trees. Ordering online for pickup and delivery is available.

article_title: Where to Find the Greatest Sandwiches in Austin
name: Vic and Al’s
address: 2406 Manor Rd Ste D, Austin, TX, 78722, US
notes: Cherrywood’s Vic & Al’s brings Cajun flavor to its loaded po’boys. The cochon de lait packs in slow-roasted pork layered with all the fixings that make this sandwich worthy of taking a nap after eating. The bread holds firm, while the fillings stay rich and juicy. It’s comfort food with a Louisiana drawl.

article_title: Where to Find the Greatest Sandwiches in Austin
name: La Plancha
address: 1701 E Martin Luther King Jr Blvd Unit 101, Austin, TX 78702, USA
notes: This Blackland restaurant serves Mexican tortas with bold flavors and soft, warm bread. The menu includes brisket barbacoa, al pastor with grilled pineapple, Buffalo fried chicken, and a smoky burger-style torta. Breakfast features a chilaquiles torta, while a vegetarian option showcases a quinoa and black bean patty. Each sandwich arrives with house-pickled jalapeños and escabeche, accompanied by fries as a side option. Online orders make it quick and easy to get a delicious sandwich here.

article_title: Where to Find the Greatest Sandwiches in Austin
name: Walton's Fancy and Staple
notes: West Sixth Street’s gorgeous deli-slash-florist strikes a balance between tradition and bakery charm. The Turkey Sweet sandwich layers bacon, white cheddar, caramelized onions, and apple chutney on a buttery croissant. Sandwiches here feel a little dressed up without going over the top. It’s a solid lunch spot with a pastry counter worth browsing. Get the sandwich you want quickly by ordering online.

article_title: Where to Find the Greatest Sandwiches in Austin
name: Uptown Sports Club
notes: Aaron Franklin’s relaxed all-day Eastside eatery serves po’ boys with a New Orleans spirit and an Austin touch. Fried shrimp, roast beef, and fried green tomatoes each get a turn in the spotlight. The sandwiches feel indulgent but stay balanced. Pair one with a cocktail and settle in for a long lunch or festive dinner. Take it to go or order online.

article_title: Where to Find the Greatest Sandwiches in Austin
name: Austin Rotisserie
address: 111 Congress Ave p400, Austin, TX 78701, USA
notes: This Downtown food stall, located at the Fareground food hall, turns out some of the best baguettes in town — crisp on the outside and tender inside. Several sandwiches feature its signature roasted chicken, topped with house-made roti sauce, while others include ingredients such as ham and salted butter, duck with pâté, or tuna with olives and egg. The menu leans French but stays casual and approachable. Order for pickup or pre-schedule it online.

article_title: Where to Find the Greatest Sandwiches in Austin
name: Handsome Dan’s
notes: Ready to explore a new sandwich shop? Handsome Dan’s is the bodega sandwich-loving Austinites need, and it opened in February on the Eastside. The cafeteria- and drug store counter-style seating is certainly retro. Josh Hare, the man behind Hops and Grain, has described Handsome Dan’s as a place designed to be just like “your high school football coach” — a stand-up spot that is dependable and delivers. Keeping that in mind, Dan’s serves grab-and-go sandwiches, but diners can stay awhile and devour them in the dining room or out on the patio. Pair your handheld pick with beer, some of which is brewed on site, or Dan’s selection of Rick’s Near Beer, a non-alcoholic option.

article_title: Where to Find the Greatest Sandwiches in Austin
name: R&B’s Steak and Fries
notes: East Austin’s R&B’s cranks out cheesesteaks loaded with thin-sliced ribeye and your choice of Cheez Whiz, Provolone, or American cheese (go with Whiz). Each sandwich arrives hot on an Amoroso roll with plenty of melty goodness inside. Outdoor seating provides a space to dive right in. It is just as yummy when you order online.

article_title: Where to Find the Greatest Sandwiches in Austin
name: ThunderCloud Subs
address: 2021 E Riverside Dr, Austin, TX 78741, USA
notes: The Austin-based ThunderCloud chain keeps things fast, fresh, and surprisingly good. The California Club with turkey, avocado, and bacon always satisfies. Sandwiches come piled high and served with a side of nostalgia. Many locations offer a drive-through for added convenience. Obviously, don’t skip the Thunder Sauce. Order online for pickup at all its locations.

article_title: Where to Find the Greatest Sandwiches in Austin
name: Home Slice Pizza
address: 1415 S Congress Ave, Austin, TX 78704, USA
notes: Travis Heights’ Home Slice isn’t just about pizza. Subs like the meatball, chicken, and eggplant parmesan, along with one of the best Italian subs in town, round out a strong sandwich lineup. Pro tip: The sandwiches are huge, so get a half unless you want leftovers, and add cherry peppers for a little extra kick. The North Loop locale offers the same signature subs. Just like the pizza, you can order these sandwiches online.

article_title: Where to Find the Greatest Sandwiches in Austin
name: Casper Fermentables
address: 4715 S Lamar Blvd, Sunset Valley, TX, 78745, US
notes: In Sunset Valley, Casper Fermentables serves sandwiches on house-baked sourdough and focaccia that deliver bold, fresh flavors. The Spicy Turkey features Sichuan chile oil, while the kimchi tuna melt balances funk and heat. The menu pairs creative sandwiches with a selection of house-fermented goods. Seasonal vegetables and pickles often add brightness and texture to the offerings.

article_title: Where to Find the Greatest Sandwiches in Austin
name: Bella Donna Subs
notes: Southeast Austin’s Bella Donna Subs keeps it simple with cold cuts and strong flavors. The Flying Turkey comes with spicy mustard and avocado for a clean, bold bite. Sandwiches arrive fast and hold up well. Be sure to grab a cookie on your way out.

article_title: Where to Find the Greatest Sandwiches in Austin
name: Tucci's Southside Subs
address: 801 E William Cannon Dr, Austin, TX 78745, USA
notes: Tucci’s serves hefty Italian subs, either cold or toasted. Try the spicy Italian hoagie, grilled eggplant, or hot ham and Swiss for a filling bite. The full-size sandwiches are generous, so consider ordering a half unless you plan to have a second meal. Both South Austin locations offer indoor seating and easy online ordering. Pickup and delivery orders are online.

article_title: Where to Find the Greatest Sandwiches in Austin
name: Knuckle Sandwich
address: 8504 South Congress Avenue, Austin, Texas 78745, United States
notes: Knuckle Sandwich brings bold East Coast deli energy to South Austin, offering a menu full of flavor. The signature Italian sub layers Olympia Provisions’ mortadella, capicola, and salami with Parm crisps, za’atar vinegar, and amba aioli on za’atar-dusted bread. Other standouts include the Not Boring Turkey Sammie, a lamb sandwich with tzatziki and beet horseradish slaw, and a mushroom Reuben made with lion’s mane. Find the food truck at Ants Beer Cave or Far Out Lounge.

article_title: The Best Hidden Gem Restaurants in Austin
name: Taquito Aviles
notes: In a city with as many taco options as Austin, it’s hard to choose hidden gem spots. Aside from the breakfast tacos, the goat options at this casual North Austin taqueria truck make it a standout. Try the rich birria de chivo taco or the spicy consomé de chivo with handmade corn tortillas. Take your tacos to go, or sit at one of the few shaded picnic tables and scarf down your meal al fresco.

article_title: The Best Hidden Gem Restaurants in Austin
name: Bartlett’s
notes: This longtime American restaurant in North Shoal Creek gives off classic vibes with big cozy booths, a small outdoor area with a fireplace, and warm hospitality. It’s an excellent place for a burger or steak and a martini, and there’s a strong selection of gluten-free options. The upscale-yet-casual setting works equally well for a business lunch or catching up with family, but if you must dine elsewhere, you can always order to-go.

article_title: The Best Hidden Gem Restaurants in Austin
name: Asahi Imports
notes: With a location in Brentwood and another in South Lamar, this Japanese market secretly serves amazing Japanese food to go. Aside from flavorful onigiri, Asahi dishes out filling bento boxes with mains like chicken karaage, fried shrimp, and miso salmon, plus an array of incredible imported snacks to peruse like Pocky, chips, and candy. Don’t miss the creamy soft-serve offered on weekends in flavors like matcha and melon. Neither location has a dining area, so it’s best to call ahead and order your meal to go.

article_title: The Best Hidden Gem Restaurants in Austin
name: Choo Sando
address: 5222 Burnet Rd Ste 535, Austin, TX, 78756, US
notes: This unassuming Japanese cafe in Rosedale focuses on two items done beautifully well: sushi boxes and adorable Japanese sandwiches on fluffy milk bread offered in savory and sweet varieties. Try the tamago, a combination of egg salad and soft-boiled eggs; the katsu sandwich stuffed with panko-covered pork chop and slaw; or the classic strawberry with whipped cream, which makes for a light dessert. If you’re craving nigiri and sushi, go all out with the omakase box, but be sure to order online in advance. Takeout orders can be placed online or in person. The shop is sparse but warm, with a small indoor and outdoor dining area.

article_title: The Best Hidden Gem Restaurants in Austin
name: Feral Pizza
notes: Ordering online from this gas station-adjacent pizza truck in North Loop is essential, as the garlic-laden sourdough pizzas often sell out. But, the advance planning is worth it, as the husband-and-wife team running the trailer put extra love into finishing each crispy pizza with ingredients like creamy ricotta, scratch-made sausage, or homemade ranch. There is a picnic table outside the trailer, but it’s best to take these pizzas to go.

article_title: The Best Hidden Gem Restaurants in Austin
name: Nate’s Baked Goods & Coffee
notes: Nate’s is a great way to start your morning near Downtown, if you know where to look. Though it may be overshadowed by its neighbor, Better Half, this small breakfast and lunch spot has quality pastries like housemade muffins and scones, fluffy biscuits, and weekend kolaches and danishes. The savory options include a chicken salad sandwich with avocado and bacon that has quite a following. The whole menu is served while the cafe is open, so grab a stool inside or outside and order the afternoon frittata of your dreams.

article_title: The Best Hidden Gem Restaurants in Austin
name: Fixe Southern House
notes: This upscale Southern restaurant is one of the better restaurant options Downtown, and it’s totally underappreciated. There’s a full bar, though the bourbon and whiskey cocktails get particular attention, like the Porch Swing with bourbon, peach liquor, lemon, tea, and spices. The dining room has a modern farmhouse feel, and the menu delivers nonstop stick-to-your-ribs hits like fried chicken, deviled eggs, and a little gem salad with cracklings. The fluffy biscuits with honey butter are a must-order, and the cinnamon roll biscuit, only served for brunch, is worth the visit alone.

article_title: The Best Hidden Gem Restaurants in Austin
name: Palm Pizza
address: 1701 E Cesar Chavez St, Austin, TX, 78702, US
notes: Neapolitan pizzeria Bufalina attracts a lot of attention, but New York-style offshoot Palm Pizza has truly excellent pies. Though it doesn’t have the best ambiance — seating is all outdoors and overlooks a parking lot — the pies are incredible. The Palm is a favorite, amped up with melty stracciatella, and thick-crust square slices available in pepperoni or cheese that are a must. Dine in and pair your pizza with wine or cold beer when the weather’s hot, or order online for pickup or delivery.

article_title: The Best Hidden Gem Restaurants in Austin
name: Habanero Cafe
address: 501 W Oltorf St, Austin, TX, 78704, US
notes: The little family-owned Mexican restaurant in Dawson is a beloved dining choice by those in the know. For almost 30 years, the chori-migas plate and menudo have been go-tos for anyone needing to recover from a night out. The small, homey space also offers terrific enchiladas, fajitas, quesadillas, and more. Beware of the fiery habanero salsa — it is no joke.

article_title: The Best Hidden Gem Restaurants in Austin
name: Moreno Barbecue
notes: For under-the-radar barbecue with a minimal line, head to the Garrison Park restaurant, which fires up a variety of meats like brisket, pork belly, smoked half chickens, and Sunday barbacoa. Pick your meats to pile into sandwiches or tacos, or try the super-rich brisket grilled cheese for a decadent barbecue meal. Sides include the usual suspects — gouda mac and cheese, creamed corn, and coleslaw. There’s an air-conditioned indoor area or a large shaded patio for dining, or order to-go online.

article_title: The Best Hidden Gem Restaurants in Austin
name: Cafe Malta
address: 3421 W William Cannon Dr, Austin, TX, 78745, US
notes: Joining a proud tradition of exceptional Austin restaurants located in strip malls, Cafe Malta punches above its weight in terms of ambiance and creative European bistro fare. Expect seasonal dishes like fresh salads, coq au vin, and meaty brisket ravioli served with a pesto made of Texas pecans at surprisingly reasonable prices. A few tables are available outside, but the clean, bright space and white tablecloth service inside make for a romantic date night. Place your order to-go if you have other plans.

article_title: The Best Hidden Gem Restaurants in Austin
name: The Little Darlin’
address: 6507 Circle S Rd, Austin, TX, 78745, US
notes: If the Little Darlin’ weren’t so far south, it would be one of the most popular bars in Austin. It nails all the essential bar elements — stellar drinks and solid food, including a hefty burger, a cold udon noodle salad for a perfect lunch, and an extra-rich macaroni and cheese made with three cheeses. Inside, the bar features standard decor, including battered wooden tables, but the sprawling patio with twinkle lights and a stage for music and shows is where the action is.
